title: secret_leak scanner: 128 high-sev false positives on vendor/ (cobra)
description: A fresh greetcli repo with 'go mod vendor' triggered 128 high-severity 'secret_leak' findings on vendored cobra/pflag/mousetrap. Hits are entropy-only matches on Apache 2 license URLs (http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0), CONDUCT.md text, bash completion code, and pflag boilerplate. None are real secrets. Junior-engineer first-run experience: 'veska findings list' shows 128 RED findings before they have written any code. Fixes: (a) exclude vendor/, node_modules/, third_party/ from secret_leak by default; (b) tune the high-entropy rule to ignore URLs and license-file paths; (c) at minimum, downgrade severity for symbols inside files whose path starts with vendor/.
close_reason: Added vendor/node_modules/third_party/bower_components/jspm_packages to secrets-scan ignore list, with segment-anywhere matching so monorepo vendor/ also covered. Tests pin: vendored Apache URLs no longer flagged; substring (vendored_data/) does NOT match. internal/application/checks/secretsscan.go

title: vuln-scan: touchesGoMod compares basename to full path, never fires
description: VulnScanCheck.Run gates on touchesGoMod(in.FilePaths), which does slices.Contains(paths, "go.mod"). But FilePaths is populated from PromotionBatch.Files[].Path, which carries the full filesystem path that the cold scan / fsnotify watcher passes through Ingester.Save (see coldscan.go walkAndSave -> save(path,...) at internal/application/coldscan.go:272). Result: touchesGoMod returns false on every promotion that includes go.mod, so the vuln-scan check is a silent no-op even when [vuln_source] provider="osv" is configured. Repro: enable osv, register a repo with known-vulnerable deps (e.g. dgrijalva/jwt-go v3.2.0+incompatible / gin v1.6.0 / yaml.v2 v2.2.2), reindex — findings list is empty even after the OSV cache hydrates. Fix: either store repo-relative paths in staging/PromotionBatch (matches the doc comment 'FilePaths entries are repo-root-relative') or change touchesGoMod to match by filepath.Base==go.mod / strings.HasSuffix("/go.mod"). Same suspicion applies to any future check that gates on a manifest filename.
close_reason: Fixed: touchesGoMod now matches by filepath.Base so it catches both repo-relative and absolute paths. Added regression test TestVulnScanCheck_GoModTouched_AbsolutePath covering the cold-scan/fsnotify path.

title: eng_get_call_chain returns empty nodes/edges for a function with obvious callers
description:
eng_get_call_chain on node_id for Execute() in /home/jrose/src/reglet/cmd/reglet/root.go returns {nodes:[], edges:[], included_staging:false}. The same node returns proper callers via eng_get_blast_radius (which includes 'main' package as an entry). So CALLS edges exist in some form but call_chain's traversal returns nothing.

Reproduce:
1. veska repo add /path/to/cobra-cli-repo
2. wait for cold scan complete
3. find Execute via eng_find_symbol
4. call eng_get_call_chain with that node_id

Investigate application/blastradius vs the call-chain service in application; likely a missing edge-type filter or wrong direction enum.
acceptance_criteria: 1) eng_get_call_chain returns the same callers eng_get_blast_radius surfaces. 2) Unit test in application covers an Execute()-style cobra root.
notes: Investigation 2026-05-23: with new daemon binary, the bug is more nuanced. (1) Auto-link queue had 394 pending so CALLS edges for reglet may not be built. (2) The agent snippet docs eng_get_call_chain as 'incoming/outgoing CALLS edges' but tool_get_call_chain.go BFSes only OutgoingEdges. So even with edges fully built, asking 'what calls Execute' (incoming) returns nothing. Two possible fixes: (a) add an incoming-direction traversal (or accept a 'direction' param), (b) update snippet to say 'outgoing only; use eng_get_blast_radius for inbound'.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska repo add returns SQLITE_BUSY error but actually succeeds
description:
When registering a second repo concurrently (or while another scan is active), 'veska repo add' returns 'insert repo: database is locked (5) (SQLITE_BUSY)' as a failure exit, but 'veska repo list' shows the repo was actually inserted. The cold scan even kicks off. A user retrying the command will hit duplicate-row errors or assume the repo isn't registered.

Reproduce: while a cold scan is in flight on repo A, run 'veska repo add /path/B'. CLI prints SQLITE_BUSY, exit 1; repo list shows B as unindexed/added.

Fix direction: either retry under the busy lock with a short backoff (sqlite busy_timeout / explicit retry loop in internal/repo's insert path), or roll the partial insert back so the error message is truthful. Surfaced through cmd/veska/repo.go -> daemon eng_add_repo path.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Reproduce case (concurrent add during scan) no longer returns SQLITE_BUSY to the user. 2) Either succeeds cleanly or fails with no row inserted. 3) Test in internal/repo or cmd/veska/repo_test.go covers the contention path.
close_reason: Closed

title: doctor status 'healthy' rollup misses post_promotion_queue failures
description:
'veska doctor status' reports 'status: healthy' while 'veska doctor post_promotion_queue' shows degraded with failed/auto_link: 3 and 394 pending. The top-level rollup omits queue health from the aggregated subsystems list (currently embedder, egress, config, ingestion).

A new user trusts doctor status as the single signal; today it gives false reassurance while a real subsystem is broken.

Fix: add queue_state to the rollup in cmd/veska/doctor.go's status command; treat 'failed > 0' or 'pending > some-high-water-mark' as degraded; expose the same field from eng_get_status.
acceptance_criteria: 1) doctor status reflects post_promotion_queue health. 2) eng_get_status includes a queue_failed/queue_pending counter (or a degraded_reasons entry). 3) Test covers the failed>0 case.
close_reason: Closed

title: Agent init snippet teaches contract daemon does not honor (auto-resolve repo_id/branch/cwd)
description:
'veska init --agent claude' writes CLAUDE.md saying: 'repo_id and branch are usually optional. When exactly one repo is registered the daemon auto-resolves repo_id; when branch is omitted it defaults to the repo active branch.' In reality every MCP call returns 'missing required params: repo_id, branch' or 'branch is required'.

Additionally eng_get_current_repo requires explicit cwd param even when veska-mcp is invoked from inside the registered repo — the stdio shim does not forward cwd.

AI agents read the snippet, paste calls verbatim, and bounce on their first attempt. This is the contract failure most likely to make the product look broken to LLMs.

Fix direction: either (a) implement the auto-resolution in the daemon (single-repo => infer repo_id; missing branch => active_branch; eng_get_current_repo => fall back to os.Getwd of the *daemon*? no — have veska-mcp inject cwd from its own process env into params), or (b) rewrite cmd/veska/init_agent.go snippet to match real required-param behavior.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Either the snippet matches behavior, or behavior matches the snippet. 2) An MCP call with no repo_id/branch from a registered cwd works end-to-end through veska-mcp. 3) tests/mcp covers the auto-resolve case.
close_reason: Verified against freshly-built daemon: snippet IS accurate. resolveRepoIDOrSingleton auto-resolves repo_id when exactly one repo registered; resolveBranchOrActive auto-resolves branch from active_branch. My original test had 5 repos (so singleton path didn't trigger, correctly) AND was hitting a stale daemon binary that pre-dated resolveBranchOrActive. After rebuild + daemon restart, MCP calls without branch work end-to-end. Cwd-from-shim is a separate, smaller enhancement — filed as follow-up.

title: Ingester.findingStorage mutex on Save hot path guards write-once value
description: Ingester.findingStorage() takes an RWMutex RLock on every Save (ingester.go:30,53-64), but SetFindingStorage is called exactly once at wire.go:470 before any Save runs. b.findings only needs pools.Write (opened before buildCore) so it can exist before NewIngester. The mutex guards a never-concurrent write = pure per-Save hot-path overhead.
acceptance_criteria: findings dependency passed at construction (option or positional); mu/RWMutex removed from Ingester; tests pass; layercheck OK
close_reason: findings now an immutable construction option (WithFindingStorage); removed RWMutex from Ingester Save hot path. Done within ott7 full options conversion.

title: Remove dead RevalidateQuerier methods superseded by ApplyDecisions
description: CloseAsRevalidatedObsolete and RefreshAnchorHash on ports.RevalidateQuerier (internal/core/ports/revalidate.go) have zero production callers — the revalidate handler writes exclusively through the batch ApplyDecisions. Only the sqlite adapter impl and their own tests reference them. Remove both port methods, their sqlite.RevalidateRepo implementations, and the dead tests. HasInboundEdges and NodeSignaturePair on the same port are live (read-side) — keep them.
acceptance_criteria: Both methods gone from the port, the RevalidateRepo adapter, the fakeRepo test stub, and the adapter round-trip tests; go build + targeted tests green; handler unchanged.
close_reason: Removed CloseAsRevalidatedObsolete + RefreshAnchorHash from ports.RevalidateQuerier and sqlite.RevalidateRepo; zero production callers (handler uses batch ApplyDecisions). Folded the deleted Close test's actor-stamp assertion (service:veska/system) into the ApplyDecisions MixedBatch test so no coverage lost. Tests green with sqlite_fts5.

title: Validate actor_kind in NewSuppression
description: NewSuppression (internal/core/domain/suppression.go:86-121) validates id, target, reason, actorID, and scope but stores actorKind into the struct with no validActorKinds check. It is the only actor-kind entry point in the domain that skips validation — NewActor, WithActorKind, and Finding.Close all enforce it. Same invariant gap fixed earlier in Finding.Close.
acceptance_criteria: NewSuppression returns an error for an unrecognised ActorKind (mirroring NewActor's 'invalid actor_kind'); a table/unit test covers the rejection; existing suppression tests still pass.
close_reason: Added validActorKinds check in NewSuppression (suppression.go) + TestNewSuppression_ErrorInvalidActorKind. Commit b597d2a. Closes the last unvalidated actor-kind entry point in the domain.

title: Collapse StagingArea telescoping stage API
description: internal/application/staging.go has six near-duplicate stage methods: StageFile -> StageFileWithUnresolved -> StageFileWithParseData, mirrored by three StageIfCurrentGeneration* variants. The full variant takes 8 positional params. Each 'With...' layer was bolted on per-ticket. Collapse to a single Stage(key, StagedFile, ...StageOption) (or a ParseResult value struct) with generation-guarding as one option.
acceptance_criteria: One stage entry point taking a value struct; generation guard expressed as an option/param, not a parallel method family; no 8-arg call sites; callers updated; tests green.
close_reason: Collapsed six telescoping stage methods (StageFile/StageFileWithUnresolved/StageFileWithParseData + three StageIfCurrentGeneration* variants, the full one taking 8 positional params) into a single Stage(repoID, branch, filePath, StagedFile, ...StageOption) bool. Generation guarding is now WithGenerationGuard(gen, gate) instead of a parallel method family. Reuses the existing StagedFile value struct as input. Migrated 52 call sites (1 production in ingester.go, 51 tests across 11 files). No 8-arg calls remain; build+vet+targeted tests green with sqlite_fts5.

title: deps index <module> does not retro-resolve CALLS in the parent repo
description: Junior journey: veska deps index github.com/spf13/cobra succeeded ('indexed 506 nodes'), but afterwards: (a) 'veska deps list --repo <cli>' still shows cobra CALLS=0 with the chained_selectors_unresolved note, (b) 'veska blast AddCommand' returns 'no nodes in chain' even though greetcli/cmd/root.go literally has rootCmd.AddCommand(helloCmd). The user just paid the cost to vendor and index the dep — they expect the call graph to *resolve* afterward. Either deps index should trigger a reparse of importing repos that targets the newly-known external symbols, or the deps index help/output should say bluntly: 'this adds nodes but does NOT resolve call edges into them; run veska reindex <parent-repo> to pick them up.' Right now it silently looks broken.
close_reason: Root cause was deeper than the bd title suggested. 'deps index' itself was working; the missing piece was upstream — no cross_repo_edge_stub ever existed for rootCmd.AddCommand(helloCmd) because the Go parser only collected var origins from short_var_declarations inside function bodies. Package-level 'var rootCmd = &cobra.Command{...}' was never visited, so extractCallsFromBody saw rootCmd.AddCommand and emitted UnresolvedCall{PkgQualifier:"rootCmd"}. At promotion, file.Imports["rootCmd"] missed (rootCmd is not an import alias), findImportByPackageName missed (no registered repo has package named 'rootCmd'), and the stub was silently dropped. Deps index then had nothing to retro-resolve against. Fix: (1) new collectPackageVarOrigins walks file-scope var_declaration/var_spec nodes; (2) new originPkgFromRHS handles three RHS shapes — call_expression with selector function (pkg.New()), composite_literal with qualified_type (pkg.Type{}), and unary_expression(&) wrapping a composite literal (&pkg.Type{}); (3) extractCallsFromBody and extractAnonCallsInTopLevelVars now accept the file-scope origins map and merge it under function-scope short-var origins (Go shadowing semantics). After fix on the junior fixture: 'blast AddCommand' returns 2 cross-repo edges to Command.AddCommand/Command.Flags in ext:github.com/spf13/cobra; 'deps list' shows cobra CALLS=3 (was 0) with TOP_SYMBOLS Execute/AddCommand/Flags; the chained_selectors_unresolved CALLS=0 warning is gone for cobra. Pre-existing greetlib resolution (Render, New, Farewell via local := origins) unchanged. New tests: TestParseFile_PackageVarCompositeLiteralOrigin, TestParseFile_PackageVarConstructorOrigin. Full app/sqlite/mcp/parser test sweep clean under sqlite_fts5 tag. Commit 216f9dc.

title: secret_leak high-entropy fires on long doc comments (false positive)
description: Junior journey: indexed spf13/pflag, got a HIGH-severity secret_leak finding on text.go:62 — which is a normal cobra-style multi-line doc comment for TextVar. The 'high-entropy' rule treats long English prose as a secret. This is the loudest possible signal (HIGH sev, first finding shown) for a benign doc comment, in a well-known OSS dep, on the user's very first external scan. Likely fix: skip comment tokens, or raise the entropy/charset gate so prose can't trip it. Repro: clone spf13/pflag at HEAD; veska repo add; veska findings list shows '[high] secret_leak text.go: high-entropy at line 62'.
close_reason: Root cause: local entropy fallback runs additively alongside gitleaks. Gitleaks correctly ignored pflag text.go:62; local heuristic fired because looksLikeIdentifier was tripped by tokenisation. tokenRe captured 'encoding.TextUnmarshaler.' (24-char min length + trailing sentence period). identifierChainSeparators split on '.' producing ['encoding','TextUnmarshaler','']; empty trailing element failed isIdentifierWord's empty-string guard, so token treated as non-identifier → entropy fired. Fix: skip empty parts in looksLikeIdentifier's per-component loop — empty components only arise from leading/trailing separator punctuation. Non-empty isIdentifierWord still rejects real secrets carrying '+'/'=' or punctuation outside the '.','/','-' separator set. Regression covered by TestTrailingSentencePunctuationFalsePositive (period, trailing slash, trailing hyphen). Commit f133468.

title: search: emit 'searching: <repo>' header so cwd-scoping isn't silent
description:
Repro: From /tmp/junior/greetcli with both repos registered, 'veska search greeting' returns only greetcli hits with no indication that greetlib was excluded. The header just reads '# tier: top|strong|weak ...'. New users assume search fanned out across all registered repos.

Acceptance:
- 'veska search <q>' prints a one-line 'searching: <repo-alias-or-shortid> (use --repo to override)' header above results when cwd resolves to a registered repo.
- 'veska search <q> --json' output is unchanged (header goes to stderr or is omitted in --json mode).
- 'veska search <q>' from outside any registered repo continues the cross-repo fan-out path with a 'searching: all repos' header.

DoD: cmd/veska/search.go header logic + test in cmd/veska covering all three modes (cwd-scoped, explicit --repo, no-repo fan-out).
close_reason: DoD verified: cwd-scoped header 'searching: 52d6c257dfe2 (use --repo to override)' on stderr; --json suppresses header (stderr empty, stdout clean JSON); from /tmp 'searching: all repos'. Commit c8c72b6 — emitSearchHeader threaded through runSearch/daemonSearch in cmd/veska/search.go; tests in cmd/veska/search_header_test.go.

title: calls/blast: bare symbol names don't fan out across registered repos
description:
Repro (from /tmp/junior/greetcli, both greetlib + greetcli registered):
  veska calls Hello   → 'calls: daemon: symbol not found: Hello'
  veska blast Greeter → 'blast: daemon: symbol not found: Greeter'

veska symbol Hello finds it in greetlib (Greeter.Hello). veska blast Greeter --repo <greetlib-id> works. Sibling .1 fixed node_id resolution; .6 fixed cross-repo CALLS edges. Bare-name fan-out across registered repos is still broken — calls/blast scope to cwd's repo and miss the symbol's owning repo.

calls --help advertises '--repo: default: fan out across registered repos' which is the contract; behaviour doesn't match.

Acceptance:
- veska calls Hello (from greetcli) returns greetlib's Greeter.Hello hits.
- veska blast Greeter (from greetcli) returns the cross-repo edge set.
- Regression test in cmd/veska covers both with two-repo fixture.

DoD: Both commands resolve bare names through registered repos when --repo is omitted; help text matches behaviour; tests pass.
close_reason: DoD verified: resolveSeedOwner cwd-pin path now falls through to fan-out on CodeNotFound (internal/infrastructure/mcp/tools_helpers.go); commit 3d42980. End-to-end repro from /tmp/junior/greetcli: 'veska blast Greeter' returns cross-repo edges; 'veska calls Hello' resolves to greetlib's Greeter.Hello (degraded with chained_selectors_unresolved as expected — Hello only calls fmt.Sprintf).

title: calls/blast reject names that symbol/context accept ('symbol not found: Hello')
description:
Repro (from /tmp/junior/greetlib, branch main):

  veska symbol Hello   → method  Greeter.Hello (66f083714906)
  veska context Hello  → 12-node neighbourhood OK
  veska calls Hello    → 'calls: daemon: symbol not found: Hello'
  veska blast Hello    → 'blast: daemon: symbol not found: Hello'

Also rejected: 'Greeter.Hello' and the 12-char short_id from symbol's own output.
Only the full 64-char node_id is accepted (and even then, see solov2-IZH6 child re: cross-repo).

Impact: This is the first interaction a new user has with the graph. The four commands look like siblings in --help; having two of them honour a name while the other two demand full node_ids is a hard friction point and looks like a bug.

Fix direction: route calls/blast through the same NodeLookup resolver that symbol/context use, accepting name | symbol_path | short_id | node_id uniformly.
close_reason: Fixed by lowering cmd/veska looksLikeNodeID threshold from 32 to 12 hex chars (matching the documented short_id width), and by routing nodeID seeds in resolveSeedOwner through expandNodeIDPrefix in all three paths so short prefixes are expanded to the canonical 64-char id before BFS. Verified: 'veska calls 66f083714906' / 'veska blast 66f083714906' now return graph results (or 'no nodes in chain' for direction=out with no outgoing edges) from the symbol's own cwd. Nonexistent-symbol case still returns 'symbol not found' as expected.

title: veska search --repo <git-url> clones+indexes but searches return 0 results
description:
Junior journey, key value-prop bug.

Repro:
  veska search 'ServeHTTP' --repo https://github.com/gorilla/mux
  → 'cloning...', 'index ready', then 'no results'

But the same query against the resulting ephemeral short_id works:
  veska search 'ServeHTTP' --repo 8e11e8cfc322  → 10 hits in mux.go etc.

And no-scope fan-out also finds them:
  veska search 'ServeHTTP'  → Router.ServeHTTP top hit.

So clone+index succeeds, the symbols are searchable from every other path, but the CLI does not pass the resulting repo_id into eng_search_semantic when --repo is a URL — it likely forwards the URL string as the repo filter, which matches nothing post-clone. This breaks the headline 'clone+index+search a public repo' example in 'veska search --help' (see help text).
close_reason: Fixed in working-tree changes; verified end-to-end against the junior-journey repos.

title: veska repo add . sends literal '.' to daemon → resolved against daemon CWD
description: Junior journey: ran 'veska repo add .' from /tmp/junior/greetlib; daemon errored 'not inside a git work-tree: /home/jrose'. The CLI should resolve '.' (and any relative path) to absolute against the user's CWD before RPC, since the daemon's CWD is unrelated to the user's. Absolute path works fine.
close_reason: Fixed in working-tree changes; verified end-to-end against the junior-journey repos.

title: veska changed: new symbol reports line range :0-0 AND degraded=non_symbol_changes_only
description:
After 'git commit' that adds a new method, 'veska changed' reports:
  + method  /tmp/junior/greetlib/greet.go:0-0  Greeter.Whisper
  [degraded: non_symbol_changes_only]

Two bugs in one output:
  1. Line range :0-0 — symbol was added at greet.go:27-29, not 0-0.
  2. Degraded reason 'non_symbol_changes_only' is contradicted by the symbol diff line right above it.

Repro: add a func to greetlib/greet.go, commit, then 'veska changed --repo <id>'.
close_reason: Two fixes: SymbolChange now carries LineStart/LineEnd from domain.Node.Lines (CLI was rendering :0-0 because the wire type didn't include them); and the non_symbol_changes_only degraded reason only fires when ALL symbol buckets are empty (previously it fired on any file with no symbol diff, even when other files DID have symbol changes — directly contradicting the response).

title: calls/blast: --repo required despite help saying 'default: fan out'
description:
veska calls <symbol> on a multi-repo daemon errors 'repo_id is required' even though 'veska calls --help' says '--repo string ... (default: fan out across registered repos)'. Same with veska blast. Either honor the documented default (fan out), or fix the help text. veska symbol does NOT require --repo on the same setup — inconsistent across CLI verbs.

Repro:
  veska repo add /tmp/junior/greetlib --wait
  veska repo add /tmp/junior/greetcli --wait
  veska calls Shout            # errors
  veska calls <node_id_short>  # still errors
  veska symbol Hello           # works fine, no --repo

Journey context: junior-engineer first-run with 2 small repos.
close_reason: Added resolveSeedOwner helper that fans out across registered repos when repo_id is absent; eng_get_call_chain and eng_get_blast_radius use it. Honors the 'default: fan out across registered repos' contract documented in CLI --help. Ambiguity and not-found cases produce actionable errors naming the candidates.

title: secret_leak FP: veska init --agent writes .mcp.json that scanner then flags
description:
End-to-end self-inflicted false positive. Ran 'veska init --agent claude' which generated .mcp.json with a path like '/home/jrose/src/engram/solov2/bin/veska-mcp'. The very next vuln/secret scan flags that exact line as a high-severity 'secret_leak' under rule 'high-entropy'. Veska is creating the file, then alerting on it.

Fixes to consider:
- Allowlist veska-managed files (.mcp.json, docs/veska/*) in the secret scanner by default.
- Tighten high-entropy rule so absolute filesystem paths don't trip it (they have low Shannon entropy across the / separator).
- At minimum, when veska init writes .mcp.json, suppress findings for that file's veska-managed line ranges.

Repro: fresh init, 'veska init --agent claude' inside any registered repo, 'veska findings list' — top result is the auto-written .mcp.json.
close_reason: Added looksLikeFilesystemPath heuristic to the local entropy fallback so absolute multi-segment Unix paths (>=3 components) skip the high-entropy rule. .mcp.json — which veska itself writes during 'veska init --agent' — embeds the absolute path of veska-mcp; that line tripped the rule on the junior journey. Heuristic is narrow (requires leading slash + >=3 path-shaped components) so single-slash base64-style secrets are still caught; pinned by TestBuiltinScanner_FilesystemPathHeuristicStillCatchesSecrets.

title: vuln findings not auto-closed when dep is upgraded
description: Junior-eng journey hit this on first try: bumped gopkg.in/yaml.v2 2.2.1 -> 2.4.0 (which fixes the CVEs), committed, and ran 'veska reindex'. 'veska deps list' correctly reflects 2.4.0, but 'veska findings list' still shows the same 3 yaml.v2 CVE findings as 'open'. Vuln findings appear to be append-only and never reconciled against current dep versions. This makes the scanner near-useless for ongoing hygiene: you fix the vuln and the dashboard keeps screaming. Repro: see /tmp/junior/greetcli journey. Expect: findings whose affected-range no longer matches the resolved version should auto-close (or transition to 'resolved') on the next vuln-scan.
close_reason: Added FindingStorage.CloseSupersededByRule(repo, branch, rule, keep) port + sqlite impl, plus an optional checks.AuthoritativeChecker interface. The Runner now reconciles after each Save: if a check implements AuthoritativeChecker and declares a rule for the current Input, prior open findings under that (repo, branch, rule) whose IDs are not in the freshly-returned keep set are closed with reason='revalidated_obsolete'. VulnScanCheck opts in for rule='vulnerable_dependency'. Verified end-to-end against the junior journey: 4 open vuln findings on yaml.v2 v2.2.1, bumped to v2.4.0 + reindexed, the 3 resolved yaml CVEs are now closed while jwt-go's GHSA-w73w-5m7g-f7qc remains open. Pinned by TestRunner_AuthoritativeCheckReconcilesPriorFindings, TestRunner_NonAuthoritativeCheckSkipsReconcile, TestRunner_AuthoritativeOptOutHonored, and TestFindingRepo_CloseSupersededByRule.

title: dead-code + auto-link rules fire on vendored deps (232 + 88 noise findings)
description: Same fresh greetcli vendor tree produced 232 'dead-code' (low) and 88 'auto-link' (low) findings on cobra internals. While low-severity, they dominate 'veska findings list --include-low' and obscure findings in user code. Apply the same vendor/node_modules/third_party path filter to these rules. Related: secret_leak false-positives (linked).
close_reason: New pathfilter package centralises IsVendored(path) (segment-anywhere, substring-safe). Wired into dead-code, auto-link (short-circuits on row.Payload), and secret_leak (replaces inline copy). Tests pin: cobra/node_modules/third_party filtered; substrings (vendored_data) pass through. internal/application/pathfilter/

title: doctor degraded: 'unindexed repo ext:github.c' invisible to 'veska repo list'
description: After 'veska deps index github.com/spf13/cobra', doctor reports: 'status: degraded ... 1 unindexed repo(s): [ext:github.c]'. That repo never appears in 'veska repo list' so the user cannot inspect, reindex, or remove it. Also the alias is truncated to 8 chars at a meaningless boundary ('ext:github.c'). Fixes: (a) 'repo list' should show ext:* synthetic repos (perhaps under --include-external); (b) the truncated alias in doctor output should be the full module path or repo-id.
close_reason: (1) doctor checkIngestion now skips ext:* synthetic repos which by design have no LastPromotedSHA; (2) 'veska repo list --include-external' surfaces ext: repos created by 'veska deps index'. Direct-DB fallback path also honors the flag. cmd/veska/doctor.go, cmd/veska/repo.go

title: Verify blast_radius traverses cross_repo_edges in reverse (callers via inbound cross-repo CALLS)
description:
Narrowed scope (was: 'misses cross-repo callers' generally; the original observation was downstream of solov2-9rc2 chained-selector parser gap).

The narrower, still-unverified question: when a cross-repo CALLS edge IS captured by the parser (e.g. mycli.runner.Run -> greetlib.New, which the junior-journey confirmed exists), does eng_get_blast_radius on the callee (greetlib.New, direction=in/inbound) include the cross-repo caller (mycli.runner.Run) in its result?

This is the load-bearing question for the library-author journey: 'if I change this exported symbol, what consumers break?'

Repro plan (once 9rc2 is fixed, or using a non-chained example today):
- two repos registered (A imports B)
- A has fn F that makes a NON-chained call to B's exported G (so the parser captures the edge)
- mcp eng_get_blast_radius {symbol:G, repo_id:B} -> assert entries OR cross_repo_edges includes A.F

If today blast_radius only walks intra-repo edges, that's the bug; fix is symmetric to whatever eng_get_call_chain does (which already returns cross_repo_edges).

Relates to: solov2-9rc2 (chained-selector parser gap obscures broader testing of this on the demo repo).
close_reason: Added resolver.ResolveStubsTargetingNode (reverse lookup keyed by dst node's import path + symbol_path). Wired through InboundResolveFunc + With{Blast,Graph,ContextPack}InboundResolveFunc options to blast_radius (gated by direction != callees), call_chain (gated by dirIn), and context_pack (always — pack walks DirBoth). CLI 'veska context' now resolves both src and dst symbol names. Verified e2e: 'veska context New --repo greetlib' surfaces 'Run --CALLS--> New' from the mycli caller.

title: CLI 'veska context' drops cross_repo_edges from MCP response
description:
`eng_get_context_pack` over MCP returns cross_repo_edges, but the CLI `veska context <sym>` strips them. Result: a junior asking 'what does Run touch?' sees only same-repo nodes even though greetlib.New is a clear callee.

Repro:
- /tmp/veska-journey/mycli has runner.Run() -> greetlib.New() + g.Hello()
- veska context Run shows 4 same-repo nodes only
- MCP eng_get_call_chain returns 1 cross_repo_edge (Run->greetlib.New)

Fix: surface cross-repo callees (and callers when direction=in) in CLI output, resolving node_ids via a follow-up get_node call.
close_reason: contextPackResponse envelope embeds Pack and adds cross_repo_edges (parity with call_chain/blast). Wired via WithContextPackResolveFunc in daemon. CLI 'veska context' now renders 'src --KIND--> dst-symbol in repo' lines, resolving the dst symbol name via a follow-up eng_get_node call. Verified e2e on the two-repo greetlib/mycli journey.

title: repo add --wait silently degrades to direct-write when daemon not running
description:
Junior-engineer journey: after `veska init -y` the daemon is not started. The next step a user takes is `veska repo add <path> --wait`. Today it succeeds but with a long inline error: `added repo X (direct write; daemon dial failed: ... -- restart daemon to cold-scan/live-watch)`. The --wait flag is silently a no-op. 

Expected: either (a) init -y starts the daemon, (b) repo add detects no daemon and prompts/starts it, or (c) --wait errors out instead of pretending to wait.

Repro: rm -rf ~/.veska; veska init -y; veska repo add /some/repo --wait
close_reason: --wait + no daemon now returns an error naming 'veska service start' and 'drop --wait' as remedies, instead of silently degrading to direct-write. Non-wait path keeps its bootstrap-friendly fallback (TestRepoAddCmd_DirectFallback still green). New test TestRepoAddCmd_WaitWithoutDaemon also asserts no repo row leaks to the DB on the error path.

title: README sanity-check examples fail when >=1 repos are registered
description: The README 'First call — 60 second sanity check' shows: printf '...eng_find_symbol","params":{"symbol":"Run"}}' | veska-mcp and similar for eng_search_semantic — both omitting repo_id. With 2 repos registered (the realistic multi-repo case the product encourages), both return -32602: 'repo_id is required'. Junior pipes through jq '.result.nodes[0]' and sees 'null', concludes the tool is broken. Either: (a) make eng_find_symbol/eng_search_semantic fan out across repos when repo_id is omitted (mirrors the cwd-fanout work in solov2-vm5w), or (b) update the README example to first call eng_list_repos and pass repo_id, with a clear note that repo_id is required when >1 repo is registered. (a) is the better fix.
close_reason: Implemented daemon-level fanout for eng_find_symbol and eng_search_semantic when repo_id is omitted and cwd doesn't match a registered repo. Per-hit repo_id field populated only when fanout is engaged. Smoke-tested against the live daemon with the 2-repo journey setup. Tests in tools_graph_test.go and tools_search_test.go. Commit c2c6237. Note: this is a basic option-(a) of solov2-bcn; that issue stays open for the deeper score-normalization design.

title: findings list: add --all flag for cross-repo listing
description:
'veska findings list' is one-repo-scoped today. Add --all flag that lists findings across every registered repo (via shared MultiRepoResolver from solov2-9q72). Output should group/prefix by repo id so the user can tell which finding came from where.

Acceptance:
- --all enumerates all repos via the resolver and concatenates findings, grouped by repo.
- Without --all behavior is unchanged.
- solov2-8kkj (findings show --repo) is sibling, not blocker.

Depends on solov2-9q72 (resolver).
close_reason: Added --all flag to 'veska findings list'. Mutually exclusive with --repo. Walks eng_list_repos, runs eng_list_findings per repo, concatenates results. Output gains a REPO column (12-char prefix) when --all is set. Per-repo errors are warned to stderr and the run continues. Severity sort + summary still operate over the combined set.

title: symbol: cross-repo hint when scoped lookup is empty
description:
When 'veska symbol <name>' returns no matches in the current repo, probe other registered repos via the shared MultiRepoResolver (solov2-9q72). If matches exist elsewhere, print a hint: 'no matches in <current>; N match(es) in <other-repo-id> (re-run with --repo <id>)'. Don't auto-promote — keep behavior predictable; the hint is enough.

Acceptance:
- Empty result + matches elsewhere => non-zero suggestion line on stderr or as part of structured output.
- Empty result + no matches anywhere => current 'no matches' behavior unchanged.
- Test covers both branches with a fake resolver.

Depends on solov2-9q72 (resolver).
close_reason: When 'veska symbol <name>' returns no matches in the scoped repo, the CLI now walks every other registered repo via eng_list_repos + eng_find_symbol probes and prints a one-line hint on stderr: 'hint: "X" has no matches here, but matches elsewhere — N in <short-id>, ... (re-run with --repo <id>)'. Stays best-effort: per-repo errors are skipped silently. JSON output is unchanged (hint suppressed under --json). When no cross-repo matches exist, behaviour is identical to before.

title: doctor: first-invocation egress probe lies about daemon state
description: Immediately after a successful `veska init && veska service start && veska repo add` (all proven to talk to the daemon over the socket), `veska doctor` reports 'status: stopped — daemon is not running (egress=stopped)' while `veska service status` correctly reports running=true pid=N. After a subsequent service restart, doctor correctly reports 'egress=healthy'. The egress probe seems to read a stale state file instead of round-tripping the socket on every invocation. Fix: doctor's egress check should always be a live socket round-trip (cheap, microseconds), not a cached/disk-derived signal.
close_reason:
Audit: egress probe is already a live socket dial. Verified with fresh binaries:
- internal/doctor/egress.go:27 calls net.DialTimeout("unix", path, 200ms) — live probe, no state-file read involved
- cmd/veska/doctor.go:586-633 calls CheckEgress() at probe time and computes egressStatus from the live result
- Live test: service stop → service start → immediate 'veska doctor' reports egress=healthy
- 'veska doctor egress' also reports both cli.sock and mcp.sock reachable
Original report ('egress=stopped — daemon is not running' until restart) was likely a transient race during initial setup, OR a pre-3f82b21 daemon binary still running. Cannot reproduce against current code.

title: secret_leak: replace entropy-only detector with gitleaks (or trufflehog) rules
description:
Two correctness issues with the current secret_leak rule:

1. FALSE POSITIVE: Go import paths are flagged as high-entropy secrets. Reproduced: `_ "github.com/dgrijalva/jwt-go"` triggered a 'high' severity finding ('high-entropy at line 9'). Import paths/URLs should be excluded.
2. FALSE NEGATIVE: textbook AWS Access Key ID shape `AKIA1234567890ABCDEF` was NOT flagged. The rule is entropy-only and has no known-pattern detectors (AWS AKIA, Stripe sk_live/sk_test, GitHub ghp_/gho_/ghs_, Slack xox[bpra]-, GCP service-account JSON, etc.).

Fix: vendor gitleaks rules (https://github.com/gitleaks/gitleaks) or trufflehog detectors. They already include both the import-path/URL exclusions and the well-known patterns we're missing. Keep entropy as a tertiary signal behind the curated rules so we don't drown the agent in noise.
notes:
SCOPING DECISION NEEDED before implementation:

Three integration options for gitleaks rules:
1. Library: import github.com/zricethezav/gitleaks/v8 — heaviest dep, runs gitleaks' own scanner. Brings cobra/viper/etc transitively.
2. Embed rules only: bundle gitleaks' default config.toml (~140 patterns) and run them through our existing regex engine. Lightest. Loses gitleaks' allowlist/stopword logic unless we port it too.
3. Shell out: requires user to have gitleaks installed. Rejected — junior-journey is about zero-friction.

Recommend option 2: embed rules toml at build time (go:embed), reuse our regex pipeline, port the import-path/URL allowlist explicitly (covers solov2-1rfo too). Keep entropy detector as tertiary signal behind curated rules.

Awaiting decision before claiming.
DECISION: import gitleaks v8 as a library (reglet pattern).

Reference impl: /home/jrose/src/all-reglet/reglet/internal/infrastructure/sensitivedata/redactor.go
- Imports github.com/zricethezav/gitleaks/v8/config + /detect
- Loads config.DefaultConfig via viper → config.ViperConfig → Translate() → detect.NewDetector
- Keeps a regex pattern slice as fallback when gitleaks init fails
- Wraps findings into local SecretFinding type (RuleID, Description, StartLine, EndLine, Match)

Plan for veska:
- New scanner adapter under internal/infrastructure/scanner (or wherever secret_leak lives today)
- Initialize gitleaks detector once at boot; cache on the analyzer struct
- Map gitleaks findings → existing secret_leak Finding shape
- Keep current entropy detector as tertiary signal, gated behind a flag (or remove entirely — TBD during impl based on signal/noise)
- Subsumes solov2-1rfo (gitleaks' default rules already exclude Go import paths via allowlists)

Acceptance:
- AKIA1234567890ABCDEF is flagged
- _ \"github.com/dgrijalva/jwt-go\" is NOT flagged
- Existing secret_leak tests still pass (or are updated)
close_reason: Imported github.com/zricethezav/gitleaks/v8 (reglet pattern); detector initialized at construction from gitleaks' bundled DefaultConfig. Scan now runs gitleaks AND the local regex+entropy path additively, de-duped by redacted-secret string. Gitleaks contributes ~140 curated rules (e.g. stripe-access-token, GCP service-account, twilio, etc.) plus its allowlists; local path remains as fallback when gitleaks init fails. Added a Go import-path allowlist to the entropy detector (subsumes solov2-1rfo): github.com, go.opentelemetry.io, gopkg.in, golang.org, k8s.io, etc. tokens are skipped. Smoke test confirms 'github.com/dgrijalva/jwt-go' no longer flagged. New tests pin import-path quiet path + gitleaks adding stripe detection.

title: search: improve out-of-repo UX (semble-style cross-repo search)
description: Today `veska search <q>` from outside a registered repo errors 'this dir is not registered; run veska repo add ...' even when other repos ARE registered. As a junior who just registered two repos elsewhere, this is dead-end UX. Take inspiration from https://github.com/minishlab/semble: support a top-level search that fans out across all registered repos, ranks results globally, and tags each hit with its repo. Acceptance: `veska search 'parse config'` from /tmp works and returns top-k across every registered repo, with repo column in the output.
close_reason: When 'veska search <query>' runs from outside any registered repo (cwd not matched by eng_get_current_repo) and target is empty, the CLI now falls back to a cross-repo fanout: eng_list_repos + per-repo eng_search_semantic, merged + score-desc sorted + trimmed to k. Each hit carries repo_id; output gains a leading 12-char REPO column when in fanout mode. Per-repo failures surface in degraded_reasons rather than aborting. Empty registry still errors via the existing 'not registered' path. JSON output adds repo_id as omitempty so single-repo behavior is byte-identical. Live smoke test from /tmp confirmed top hits ranked across two registered repos.

title: cross-repo resolver: shared multi-repo lookup primitive for symbol/findings/search
description:
Build the underlying multi-repo resolver that symbol/findings/search will share. Today each surface is one-repo-scoped with no awareness that other registered repos exist.

Scope (resolver only):
- New port (likely internal/core/ports or application): MultiRepoResolver with methods like LookupSymbol(name) []RepoMatch, ListRepos() — exact shape TBD against existing repo registry (internal/repo).
- Backed by the existing repos-table registry; no new storage.
- Returns per-repo hit counts so callers can format their own hints ('no matches in A; 1 in B').
- Unit tests with a fake registry + fake per-repo lookup.

Out of scope (split into child beads):
- symbol command UX (hint + auto-promote): see child solov2-XXXX
- findings --all flag: see child solov2-YYYY
- semble-style search fanout: solov2-vm5w (already blocked by this)

Acceptance:
- New resolver port + impl, with tests.
- No CLI surface changes in this bead.
- solov2-vm5w and the two new children can all import the resolver.
close_reason: Created internal/application/crossrepo with Resolver, RepoView, RepoMatch types. LookupSymbol walks every registered repo, returns per-repo hit counts in registry order, drops zero-hit repos, and routes per-repo lookup errors through errors.Join so callers that only render hints can ignore them. ListRepos is a thin pass-through for surfaces (findings --all). Tests cover empty registry, per-repo errors non-fatal, hit aggregation, empty-branch defaulting, empty-name rejection. layercheck OK.

title: init: prompt to enable [vuln_source] instead of writing it commented out
description: `veska init` writes ~/.veska/config.toml with [vuln_source] block commented out, so the OSV.dev vulnerability scanner is silently off after a clean install. README claims the check 'ships off by default; gated behind a [vuln_source] block' but a junior reading the README expects an opt-in path that doesn't require hand-editing TOML. Fix: during `veska init`, ask 'Enable OSV vulnerability scanning? [Y/n]' (-y accepts default). Skip prompt when --yes is set; respect a --no-vuln flag for users that want it off.
close_reason: Init prompts 'Enable OSV vulnerability scanning? [Y/n]' before writing config.toml. -y accepts the default (enabled). --no-vuln forces disabled and skips prompt. Existing config.toml is never overwritten. Template split into live + commented variants; chosen branch is written. Tests pin yes/no/--no-vuln paths.

title: reindex: route through daemon (pause/drain instead of requiring stop/start)
description: `veska reindex` currently refuses when daemon is up: 'daemon is running and would race us for the sqlite write lock; stop it first'. README tells users to run reindex after enabling vuln_source — contradicts the gate. Commit 3f82b21 claims reindex routes through daemon + adds eng_reindex_repo MCP tool, but neither shipped: CLI still races, and tools/list shows only eng_promote_repo. Design: CLI should send a reindex request to the daemon over the existing socket; daemon pauses watcher/queue, runs the cold-scan inline, resumes. No more stop/start dance.
close_reason:
Audit: reindex already routes through daemon. Verified end-to-end with fresh build:
- cmd/veska/reindex.go:144-163 dispatches via callMCP('eng_reindex_repo', ...) when reindexDaemonProbe() is true
- Direct-SQLite path is fallback for no-daemon case only
- Quoted error 'daemon is running and would race us for the sqlite write lock; stop it first' exists nowhere in code; reindex_test.go:269-293 has a negative test ensuring it never appears
- Live test: with daemon running, 'veska reindex <repo>' printed 'reindexing via daemon...' / 'reindex complete'; daemon.log showed cold scan: starting → promotion: complete → cold scan: complete (runs inside daemon, no stop/start)
Already shipped in 3f82b21.

title: veska reindex refuses to run while daemon up; README contradicts
description:
README quick-start tells the user, after enabling [vuln_source], to 'run veska reindex <path>'. In practice, with the daemon running, reindex prints:

  reindex: daemon is running and would race us for the sqlite write lock.
    stop it first:  veska service stop && veska reindex /path
    then restart:   veska service start

So a junior following the README must stop the daemon (which terminates the MCP socket and disconnects their editor), run reindex (possibly multiple times, once per repo), then restart. Several gaps:
  - README does not warn about this sequence.
  - Stopping the daemon to scan is a regression vs. add-time scans, which run inside the daemon.
  - reindex could instead dispatch via daemon MCP (eng_promote_repo / a new tool), or queue + take an advisory lock, or auto-stop-start with a single command.

Repro: enable [vuln_source]=osv, restart service, run ./bin/veska reindex /path.
Expected: scan happens without manual service cycling, or README explicitly documents the stop/start dance.
close_reason: DoD verified: new eng_reindex_repo MCP tool; veska reindex routes through daemon when up, falls back to direct mode when down; editor MCP stays alive; README updated (commit 3f82b21)

title: MCP tools/list returns empty inputSchema (no properties, no required) for every tool
description: Sample query of tools/list shows all 31 tools have inputSchema = {required:[], properties:[]}. MCP clients (Claude Code, Cursor, etc) rely on inputSchema to know argument names, types, and what is required. Currently they must call blind and parse the human error message ('symbol is required', 'node_id is required'). Fix: populate inputSchema with JSON-schema descriptions of every parameter the tool accepts. Without this, the MCP surface is effectively undiscoverable to anyone but the local CLI, which knows the args by convention.
close_reason: All 31 MCP tools now publish a JSON-schema (draft 2020-12) inputSchema. New file internal/infrastructure/mcp/tool_schemas.go centralises the literals; each ToolSpec references its schema. Verified live via 'tools/list': 28 tools advertise properties/required, 3 no-arg tools (eng_get_status, eng_get_config, eng_list_repos) advertise an empty properties object with a description — both forms are valid and discoverable. MCP clients no longer have to guess argument names; this also makes jtl5.10 mostly moot since aliases that the handler accepts (file_path/path, k/limit) are listed in the schema.

title: vuln scan does not run on first config-reload after enabling vuln_source; OSV cache cold
description: Repro: (1) fresh ~/.veska, init, repo add solov2 + tack; (2) uncomment [vuln_source] in config.toml; (3) veska config reload. Outcome: tack re-promoted, vulnrefresh+vuln-scan ran, but 'findings: 0' because OSV cache was being fetched concurrently. solov2 promoted before vulnrefresh started and was NEVER vuln-scanned. Second config reload (17 min later) hit SQLITE_BUSY on solov2 from a sink-before-delete, succeeded on tack (31 findings appeared). Asks: (a) gate the first vuln-scan on vulnrefresh completion so the first promotion after enable doesn't silently score 0; (b) ensure every registered repo gets a vuln-scan when [vuln_source] is enabled, not just whichever ones get re-promoted in the right window; (c) consider a 'veska vuln scan-all' command so users have a deterministic way to retry without restarting the daemon.
close_reason: Two-part fix: (1) drop the touchesGoMod gate in VulnScanCheck so partial re-promotes (eng_promote_repo from config reload, which only restages the latest commit) still run vuln-scan; (2) wire WithOnFirstRefreshOk on Refresher so the daemon kicks scanAllReposForVuln after the first successful OSV refresh, retroactively populating findings for every registered repo if the cache was cold when their last promotion ran. Non-Go repos (no go.mod) become a clean no-op instead of an error.

title: junior-journey round 3: fresh install + vuln scanner end-to-end (solov2 + tack)
description: Wipe ~/.veska, rebuild from source, follow README as a junior would. Index this repo and ../../all-reglet/tack. Exercise CLI surface (init, repo add, service, doctor, backup, wiki, context, findings, reindex, config reload). Exercise every MCP tool. Specifically validate the OSV vuln scanner end-to-end (config enable -> reindex -> findings). File a child bead for every friction/gap.
close_reason: Round 3 closed: all 12 child beads landed. Race fix (jtl5.5) collapsed WriteHot+WriteEmbed → single Write pool to end SQLITE_BUSY_SNAPSHOT at the architectural root, not at another retry loop. Vuln scanner (jtl5.4) gained scan-all-repos-on-first-refresh + ungated per-promote scan. MCP surface (jtl5.9) is now fully self-describing — all 31 tools publish JSON schemas. UX (jtl5.1/.7/.8) surfaces real failure reasons + elapsed times instead of frozen counters.

title: veska reindex runs cold scan in-process, racing the running daemon → SQLITE_BUSY
description:
Repro:
  veska service start
  veska repo add /path/to/tack    # works; daemon does the scan
  veska reindex /path/to/tack     # FAILS: 'promoter: sink before-delete for "...": database is locked (5) (SQLITE_BUSY)'

Daemon log shows the reindex never appears (no 'cold scan: starting' lines from daemon). The veska CLI is invoking the cold-scan in-process and competing with the running daemon for the sqlite write lock — specifically, the daemon's embedder worker is flushing vectors when reindex tries to do its before-delete in the sink. Repeatedly reproducible, even after waiting for the daemon to idle.

Reindex should dispatch through the daemon (same as  does — note the help text on  literally says 'via daemon' but  does not). Either route it over the MCP socket as a 'reindex' RPC, or refuse to run if the daemon is up.

User impact: high — the only way to retry a failed scan or pick up a config change (e.g. enabling vuln_source then wanting to scan retroactively, exactly what the README tells you to do) is broken on a default install.
close_reason: reindex now probes the MCP socket via the existing daemonRunning() helper from restore.go; if up, refuses with an actionable two-line hint (stop-reindex-start). Verified: refusal triggers when daemon up, full reindex still works when daemon down. Follow-up (real fix): route reindex through an eng_reindex MCP RPC so the daemon owns the write — filed as solov2-mdn3-followup if needed.

title: Secret scanner FP: Go Benchmark/Test function names match 'high-entropy' rule
description: Repro: scanning tack registered repo, the secret_leak rule fires on `func BenchmarkLoaderDiscovery(b *testing.B)` at internal/plugin/loader_bench_test.go:14 with rule "high-entropy". The detector inspects the entire line including the Go identifier; long CamelCase identifiers (Benchmark*, Test*) reliably trip entropy thresholds. Impact: every Go repo with non-trivial benchmark/test names will surface high-severity secret findings on first scan — a poor first-impression for new users and noise that drowns real leaks. Fix candidates: (1) skip lines matching `^func (Test|Benchmark|Example|Fuzz)`, (2) ignore identifiers entirely when computing entropy, (3) require additional signal (e.g., string-literal context, key=value shape) before flagging. Already saw a user suppression in the DB labeled "false positive: benchmark name" — confirming this hits real users.
close_reason: already fixed in e247108 (solov2-3455) — looksLikeIdentifier skip; stale finding in DB closed retroactively

title: eng_list_findings: 'rule' param silently ignored (filters not applied)
description: Repro: `eng_list_findings` with `params.rule="vuln"` (or any other rule) returns the FULL unfiltered list (all rules mixed in). `severity` filter works correctly. Verified on tack repo (9a4bd5499f1e): 237 findings returned regardless of rule. Impact: agents/users can't isolate vuln/secret/dead-code findings from MCP — must post-filter client-side, defeating the param's purpose. Likely missed switch arm in the SQL where-clause builder for list-findings. Add MCP/CLI test that asserts rule-filter narrowing.
close_reason: fixed in e4b8dd5

title: epic: resolve chained-selector CALLS edges (s.field.method)
description:
Followup from solov2-o2y5 (junior-journey sweep). On a hexagonal/DDD codebase, calls through interface-typed struct fields are the dominant call-chain edge type, but the treesitter Go adapter currently drops them — call_chain/blast_radius miss 75%+ of the real outgoing edges from any method that uses dependency injection.

See solov2-o2y5 closure note for detailed root-cause analysis and proposed fix outline. Touches:
- internal/infrastructure/treesitter/go.go: parseTypeDecl, collectCallNames, extractCallEdges, extractTopLevelVarInitCalls
- internal/core/domain: maybe richer UnresolvedCall (carry field-qualifier chain)
- internal/infrastructure/sqlite/promotion_store.go: cross-file resolver may need to honor field-qualifier hints
- tests: new fixtures for struct fields, interface fields, embedded structs

Acceptance: eng_get_call_chain direction=out on Promoter.Promote returns ≥ 7 outgoing edges (currently 2).
notes:
PHASE C+D LANDED (commit b707e28, 2026-05-26):
- migrations/0011_cross_repo_method_call.sql adds method_call INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 to cross_repo_edge_stubs.
- promotion_store.go emits a separate method-call stub (method_call=1, symbol_path=bare method name) for uc.IsMethodCall=true on the external-module path. Distinct stub_id namespace ('@method:' prefix) prevents collision with the plain pkg.Foo stub when both come from the same caller.
- resolver.ResolveCrossRepoEdge branches on stub.MethodCall: forward path matches by 'kind=method AND symbol_path LIKE <Method>' within the destination subpackage; ambiguity returns no edge.
- resolver.ResolveStubsTargetingNode (reverse) now ORs an additional WHERE clause for method-call stubs whose symbol_path equals the dst node's bare method name (split on last '.' when kind='method'). Plain symbol_path equality still applies for non-method nodes.
- Three new resolver tests + one new promotion_store test exercising forward, ambiguity-drop, and reverse paths.
- End-to-end mycli/greetlib journey (2026-05-26): both 'mycli context Run' and 'greetlib context Hello' now surface Run -> Greeter.Hello in their cross-repo edges. The journey demo this epic was filed to fix is fully working.

REMAINING — PHASE E (struct-field DI):
This epic's title parenthetical names the s.field.method shape — calls through interface-typed struct fields, the hexagonal/DI pattern that produced the original 'Promoter.Promote returns 2 not 7 edges' acceptance number. Phase A-D do NOT solve that; they solve the simpler local-var-from-constructor shape.

Phase E scope sketch:
1. parser: parseTypeDecl records struct field types; collectCallNames recognises 's.field.M()' when s is the method receiver and field is a known struct field of type T (or interface I).
2. UnresolvedCall grows ReceiverFieldType so the resolver knows the target type.
3. promotion-time resolver looks up T.M (or, for interfaces, finds methods named M on types implementing I) — same disambiguation as Phase B/C.
4. cross-repo stubs reuse the method_call=1 producer path; the bare method name is enough for the reverse resolver because method-name uniqueness within the dst package is the same constraint.

Phase E is its own multi-day chunk; tracking it in this epic to preserve continuity with the original DI motivation.
close_reason:
EPIC ACCEPTANCE MET (2026-05-26, commit 27a6c86).

Promoter.Promote outbound call_chain at d=1 on the real veska codebase now returns 7 distinct edges (up from 2 pre-epic):
  - Promoter.tracerProvider (internal method)
  - StagingArea.Snapshot (same-pkg struct field)
  - StagingArea.DeleteStagedFile (same-pkg struct field)
  - PromotionStore.Promote (same-pkg interface field)
  - CheckRunner.Run (same-pkg interface field)
  - AuditWriter.Write (CROSS-PACKAGE interface field, the load-bearing new edge)
  - StartSpan (package-qualified function call)

WHAT LANDED ACROSS PHASES:

PHASE A (parser local-var origins): `v := pkg.New(...); v.X()` → UnresolvedCall{Pkg,Name,IsMethodCall:true}.

PHASE B (in-module method resolution): bare-method-name suffix lookup against `<Receiver>.<Method>` for IsMethodCall=true; ambiguity skipped.

PHASE C (cross-repo method stub producer): new method_call INTEGER column on cross_repo_edge_stubs (migration 0011), distinct stub_id namespace via @method: prefix.

PHASE D (reverse method-stub resolver): forward + reverse resolvers branch on stub.MethodCall, suffix-match against bare method name; new bareName extraction (lastDot) for reverse lookups against method nodes.

PHASE E v1 (struct field types — concrete same-package): new collectStructFields prewalk + classifyFieldType captures pointer/qualified/identifier field types; collectCallNames recognises recvName.field.Method when field is same-package concrete.

PHASE E v2 (interface methods as nodes + cross-package field methods): parseInterfaceMethods emits a KindMethod node per method_elem in interface_type bodies (named IfaceName.MethodName, parity with parseMethodDecl). Cross-package chained selectors emit method-call UnresolvedCalls that flow through Phase C/D.

TEST-FILE AMBIGUITY HEURISTIC: lookupPromotedMethodInDir + ResolveCrossRepoEdge now prefer non-_test.go candidates when disambiguating method names. A test stub (`stubAuditWriter.Write`) in the same package as the production interface (`AuditWriter.Write`) no longer shadows the production target.

TESTS ADDED: 13 new tests across parser (6: ChainedSelector, StructField, InterfaceMethodsAsNodes, SamePackageInterface, CrossPackageInterface, PromoterShape), promotion-store (3: same-pkg chained method, ambiguity skip, cross-repo stub emission), resolver (3: forward method suffix match, ambiguity drop, reverse method stub matching), parser negative-case.

LIMITATIONS / DEFERRED:
- Embedded interfaces (`type X interface { Y; Other() }`) — Y's methods aren't unfolded into X. Same-call-site behaviour today: misses unless Y is in the local symbol map. Sub-bead candidate.
- Variable assignment between fields (`v := s.field; v.Method()`) — local-var-origin tracking doesn't reach into struct-field-derived locals. Rare in practice.
- Function-typed struct fields (`p.added(ctx, ...)`) — call shape `p.X(...)` is handled by receiver-method path looking for `Promoter.added` which doesn't exist; falls into UnresolvedCall and misses. Sub-bead candidate.

Full test suite green except pre-existing TestDoctorStubSubcommandsExitZero/pipelines flake (unrelated).

title: first repo add after fresh service install times out on MCP socket
description:
After 'veska service install && veska service start' and 'veska doctor status' reporting healthy, the first 'veska repo add <path>' times out reading from the MCP socket. CLI falls back to direct DB write but no cold scan fires; user must 'veska service restart' to trigger the startup-resync code path. Reproduced on the first TWO adds; a third add minutes later went through the daemon cleanly. Looks like a socket-ready race or per-process dispatcher init delay.

Error message: 'added repo <id> (direct write; daemon dial failed: read response: read unix @->/home/jrose/.veska/mcp.sock: i/o timeout — restart daemon to cold-scan/live-watch)'
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_suppress_finding records suppression but does not filter list_findings
description:
eng_suppress_finding returns suppression_id and writes a row visible via eng_list_suppressions, but the suppressed finding still appears in eng_list_findings with state='open'. Suppression has no observable effect on the open-findings stream.

Repro:
  FID=65b6937d5b2d64eacc3a08686aa49c38   # secret_leak fp on tack
  printf '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eng_suppress_finding","params":{"finding_id":"'$FID'","reason":"fp"}}' | bin/veska-mcp
  # Then list with state=open — finding is still there.
close_reason: Closed

title: vuln scanner skips // indirect deps
description:
OSV vuln-scan only flags entries from the direct require block; // indirect entries are silently skipped. In modern Go modules almost all transitive risk lives in indirect deps, so this neuters the check on real projects.

Repro: registered tack at /home/jrose/src/all-reglet/tack which has 'golang.org/x/crypto v0.48.0 // indirect'. OSV cache contains many <0.52.0 advisories for that exact module. eng_list_findings returns 0 vuln findings. Moving the same dep to a direct require in /tmp/veska-vuln-test produces dozens of findings.
close_reason: verified-not-a-bug after closer inspection. ReadGoMod (internal/application/manifest/manifest.go) iterates f.Require and includes ALL entries — direct and // indirect alike (no Indirect filter). Live verification: forced a fresh vuln-scan on tack by touching go.mod; daemon logged 'vuln-scan: scanned repo_id=9a4bd5499f1e deps=145 findings=31' and eng_list_findings now returns 31 vuln findings on tack's indirect deps. Original observation of 0 findings was likely a sequencing issue (vuln_source not yet wired into the daemon's running state when the first promotion ran), made invisible by the lack of a per-scan log line — fixed by solov2-fw6z's new INFO line.

title: eng_find_changed_symbols ignores N in HEAD~N / HEAD^ refs
description:
All HEAD~1, HEAD~2, HEAD~3, HEAD^, main~2 return identical results (the HEAD~1..HEAD diff). Only literal SHAs traverse multi-commit windows. Likely a ref-parser that recognizes only HEAD and HEAD~1 then falls through.

Repro:
  for r in HEAD~1 HEAD~3 HEAD^ main~2; do
    printf '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eng_find_changed_symbols","params":{"repo_id":"0b3b70ab9f89","branch":"main","ref_a":"'$r'","ref_b":"HEAD"}}' | bin/veska-mcp | jq '.result.added | length'
  done
  # all → 9; explicit SHA 5 commits back → 44
close_reason: could-not-reproduce in current state: HEAD~1, HEAD~3, HEAD~5 against HEAD (and HEAD~6/7/8 against the historical test SHA 3023804) all return appropriately-scaling result counts: a:27/59/68 and a:9/20/44 respectively. Symbol-count differences track git diff --name-only file counts. Verified ref_a='71dc978' (SHA) vs ref_a='HEAD~?' resolving to the same commit both produce identical results. The original observation of 'all HEAD~N return identical results' may have been a coincidence: HEAD~1..HEAD touched only 3 files (2 Go), and HEAD~3..HEAD added .beads/issues.jsonl + 2 more Go files (providers) — added-symbol counts could have plateaued by chance. Reopen if observed again with reliable repro.

title: call_chain/blast_radius miss interface-mediated calls
description:
On internal/application/promoter.go::Promoter.Promote, eng_get_call_chain direction=out depth=3 returns only 2 of ~8 outgoing edges. Calls through interface-typed fields are dropped: p.store.Promote(), p.audit.Write(), p.staging.Snapshot(), p.staging.DeleteStagedFile(). Same root cause makes blast_radius and call_chain direction=in return empty/near-empty for heavily-used methods in DDD/hexagonal codebases. Investigate treesitter Go adapter's call edge resolution for selector expressions whose receiver type is a named interface.

Repro:
  PID=292275788eb3bf30fa1b8246af499b7386d7373b784a75a790ddf825c629c4f8
  printf '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eng_get_call_chain","params":{"repo_id":"0b3b70ab9f89","branch":"main","node_id":"'$PID'","depth":3,"direction":"out"}}' | bin/veska-mcp | jq '.result.edges | length'
  # → 2, expected ~8
close_reason:
known-limitation; needs scoped epic. Root cause analysis: internal/infrastructure/treesitter/go.go collectCallNames at line 631+ handles only single-level selector_expression where operand.Type()=='identifier'. Multi-level selectors like 'p.store.Promote()' parse as nested selector_expression: outer { operand: selector_expression{p, store}, field: Promote }. The outer-operand check at line 634 (operand.Type()=="identifier") is FALSE for chained selectors and the call site is silently dropped. The collectCallNames comment at line 611 explicitly notes 'Chained selectors (s.field.X()) whose operand is not a plain identifier are still skipped.'

A correct fix needs (a) per-struct field-name→declared-type map built during struct_type parsing (currently parseTypeDecl at line 270 captures only the struct's name, not its fields), (b) wiring that map through extractCallEdges, (c) in collectCallNames, for outer selector with operand p.field where p==recvName, resolve field→type via the struct-field map, then look up 'type.method' in the symbol map. For interface-typed fields, also need a same-package implementation lookup or emit a polymorphic UnresolvedCall.

Estimated 1-2 days with tests covering: struct fields with simple types, interface-typed fields, pointer-typed fields, embedded fields, fields-of-fields chains. Skipping for this junior-journey sweep; file as scoped epic.

title: Sharpen product positioning: pick a wedge among search / graph / workflow
description: Veska currently does ~30 things across search, graph (call chains, blast radius), and workflow (findings, tasks, todos, wiki, contextpacks, ownership). The pitch isn't clear — 'code graph for agents' vs 'better search' vs 'findings/workflow backend'. Pick one as the wedge (the thing that hooks first-time users) and frame the others as the moat. Currently nothing is the wedge, which makes onboarding messaging diffuse and ceases competitive comparisons (e.g. Semble pitches one thing crisply). Strategic decision, not implementation. From Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
notes: Promoted to P1 2026-05-26 — this is meta work: the chosen wedge determines which other P2/P3 features matter. Should land before further feature spread (find_related, LSP adapter, IDE tooling) to avoid scoping in directions the positioning rejects. Adjacent decisions waiting on this: solov2-2g4r (find_related — search-parity churn vs moat?), solov2-pwuu (LSP — IDE-tooling vs agent-first?), solov2-kxo5 (URL search — moat-aligned per its re-scope).
close_reason:
POSITIONING DECISION (2026-05-26):

THE WEDGE
=========
Primary positioning: 'Veska is the multi-repo code graph for AI agents.'

Entry point (B element): natural-language search — the door. A new user types 'veska search ...' and gets back symbols, not files. Token-efficient by construction, local-first.

Framing (D element): the graph lives behind the agent's MCP tools. MCP is how the graph is consumed; it's not a sidecar.

Headline candidate for README + landing page: 'Local, multi-repo code graph for AI agents. Search is the door; cross-repo edges are the moat.'

WHITESPACE / DIFFERENTIATION
============================
- Semble: single-repo, cloud index. We are local + multi-repo.
- Cursor: single-repo, IDE-coupled. We are agent-facing + standalone.
- Sourcegraph: enterprise + cloud. We are local + per-developer.
- context7: hosted upstream docs. We are first-party + local.
Nobody owns 'local, multi-repo, agent-shaped graph.' That is the wedge.

CASCADE — DOWNSTREAM BEAD EFFECTS
=================================
Pursue (wedge-aligned, no change):
  - solov2-9rc2  (chained selectors) — graph quality. Required for moat to feel real.
  - solov2-1yev  (.scm refactor) — backbone for polyglot graph quality.
  - solov2-bchl  (vendor / module-cache indexing) — workspace = your repos PLUS your deps.
  - solov2-bcn   (cross-repo search semantic) — natively the door.
  - solov2-wise  (tokens-saved benchmark) — measures the B-element claim.
  - solov2-kcmo  (competitive evals) — needs to lead with multi-repo queries.
  - solov2-hl70  (RRF score legibility) — search-as-door must work cleanly.

Stay aligned with reframed messaging:
  - solov2-2g4r  (find_related) — was 'search-parity with Semble'; now reframed as the moat-pivot from a search hit ('after a hit, what else looks like this in the GRAPH?'). Schedule.
  - solov2-pwuu  (LSP adapter) — D-element ('graph behind your tools') via LSP for IDEs that don't speak MCP. Stays, but priority unchanged (P3) since MCP is the primary surface.
  - solov2-jtao  (skill wrapper) — same D-element rationale, non-MCP harnesses.

Defer / down-priority:
  - solov2-9fsn  (version-pinned --at <ref> queries) — context7's differentiator, not ours. Costly per the bead's own note. Stays P3 / parked.

Promote messaging:
  - solov2-cdw3  (fat binary) — the local-first claim depends on this shipping.
  - solov2-zo0w  (one-line install + MCP auto-config) — first 60 seconds is 'install veska, register two repos, ask a cross-repo question'.
  - solov2-q2az  (public repo + landing page) — must lead with the multi-repo pitch, not generic search.
  - solov2-sxxs  (publish benchmarks) — headline numbers should be 'recall@10 across a workspace,' not single-repo.

EXPLICIT NON-WEDGE
==================
'Code review / findings backend' (severity, suppressions, vuln scanner) stays as workflow value but is NOT the wedge — it does not lead. Same for the wiki surface (hot_zones, entry_points) — they support 'onboarding to a workspace' but are not the lead.

Decision committed; downstream beads will reference solov2-71xq in their notes.

title: One-line install + auto-config for popular agent harnesses
description: No equivalent of 'claude mcp add veska …' one-liner. No first-run auto-pick-a-backend. No AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md snippet for users to drop into projects. Ship: (a) install one-liner, (b) MCP-registration helper per harness (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, OpenCode at minimum), (c) ready-to-paste AGENTS.md snippet. From Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
notes: Reframed per solov2-71xq (2026-05-26): the install one-liner should drop a user into the wedge demo, not just 'register one repo'. Onboarding sequence we should optimize for: (1) curl|sh installer (depends on solov2-cdw3 fat binary), (2) veska service start, (3) veska repo add path-to-first-repo + path-to-second-repo (or the deps of the first), (4) veska context <symbol> shows cross-repo edges. The MCP auto-config for Claude Code / Cursor / Codex / OpenCode hands the agent the wedge-curated tool surface (solov2-1hnh).
close_reason:
MAJOR DELIVERABLES SHIPPED (2026-05-26, commit 482423f):

(a) ONE-LINE INSTALL — landed in solov2-cdw3 phase A (commit fab5928): make install (clone path) and make release-archive (tarball + install.sh) produce the same install UX. curl|sh from GitHub releases is gated on solov2-q2az (public repo decision); local-build path is unblocked.

(b) MCP-REGISTRATION HELPER — landed in this commit:
- agentFlavor grows an mcpConfigPath field. claude → .mcp.json, cursor → .cursor/mcp.json.
- New ensureMcpServerEntry merges {veska: {command, args:[]}} into the harness's mcpServers map. Idempotent: 'already registered' on no-op, 'updated' when the path changes (e.g. user reinstalled veska elsewhere), 'registered' on first write. Preserves OTHER server entries (github, linear, ...) and any unrelated top-level keys — drop-in safe for projects that already use MCP.
- New resolveVeskaMcpPath finds the veska-mcp binary on PATH first, then alongside the running veska. Absolute path, so the config doesn't break across shell sessions.
- veska init --agent also runs the MCP-config writer on idempotent re-runs (CLAUDE.md already present but .mcp.json missing → still recovers the MCP config). Verified e2e.
- 4 new tests: creates new file, preserves other servers + top-level keys, idempotent same-command, updates changed command.

(c) AGENTS.MD SNIPPET — already shipped pre-zo0w via agentSnippetBody; `veska init --agent codex` and --agent opencode both write to AGENTS.md.

GAPS — see solov2-<new bead, see >:
- codex MCP config (~/.codex/config.toml, user-level) and opencode MCP config (.opencode.json variants) need their own implementations; the formats are less standardized than .mcp.json and warrant separate research before writing.
- copilot / gemini / kiro: instruction-file-only by design (copilot uses LSP not MCP; gemini is a model not a harness; kiro's MCP story unclear). No follow-up needed unless a user requests.

END-TO-END VERIFIED: `veska init --agent claude -y` on a fresh project creates CLAUDE.md + .mcp.json with veska registered; re-run on (CLAUDE.md present, .mcp.json deleted) recovers the MCP config without complaining about the instructions.

title: Default to model2vec backend; demote Ollama to opt-in
description: README and defaults currently push Ollama as primary. Model2vec is a first-class CPU backend (internal/infrastructure/embedding/model2vec/) that needs no external service. Switch default-on-first-run to model2vec, update README, position Ollama as the higher-quality opt-in path. Pairs with the fat-binary packaging issue. From Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
close_reason: Already done in both code and docs (verified 2026-05-26). cmd/veska/init.go::resolveInitEmbedder elects model2vec by default (elect.Resolve → in-process); Ollama runs only when explicitly overridden via VESKA_EMBEDDER=ollama. README copy already messages this: 'No Ollama, no network, and no separate process is required for search' + 'Optional: Ollama' section explicitly framed as opt-in for the LLM review pipeline. The Semble-analysis observation that motivated this bead is closed by composite default election; no remaining work.

title: Packaging: single fat binary with embedded model2vec weights
description: No fat binary, no brew/curl|sh installer, no published releases. Capability is at parity with Semble (model2vec backend exists, embedded_on.go build tag bundles weights) but distribution isn't. First impression for a new user is 'build three Go binaries from source.' Goal: one-line install that drops a binary which works out of the box with no Ollama, no env vars. Surfaced in Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
notes: Promoted-by-positioning per solov2-71xq (2026-05-26): the 'local-first' element of the wedge depends on the fat binary shipping. Without it, the install story is 'build three Go binaries + download a model' — which contradicts the wedge claim. Keep at P1; this is now load-bearing for the messaging, not just convenient packaging.
close_reason:
PHASE A LANDED (commit fab5928, 2026-05-26). The 'first impression is build three binaries from source' problem is resolved:

WHAT SHIPPED:
- scripts/install.sh: shell installer that copies the three fat binaries from a sibling bin/ dir into $VESKA_INSTALL_DIR | $XDG_BIN_HOME | ~/.local/bin (default). Per-user, no sudo. PATH not-set hint printed when destination is outside PATH. 'next:' line points the user at 'veska init -y && veska service install && veska service start'.
- Makefile 'install' target: 'make install' chains 'make build' + scripts/install.sh — same outcome as the tarball path but from a clone.
- Makefile 'release-archive' target: produces dist/veska-<git-describe>-<os>-<arch>.tar.gz containing bin/{veska,veska-daemon,veska-mcp} + install.sh + README.txt. The tarball is the same shape a future GitHub release will ship; the user runs './install.sh' inside and gets the same outcome.
- README: new 'Install into your PATH' section under Build that documents make install + release-archive.
- .gitignore: dist/ excluded.
- TestWikiCmd_ByteIdenticalOutput updated to strip the solov2-otzn 'Generated:' header line before comparison (the underlying ranking output is byte-identical; only the wall-clock stamp varies).

WHAT'S DEFERRED — see solov2-brw6 (P2):
The 'single fat binary' literal goal (one executable, not three) is its own multi-touch refactor: cmd/veska-daemon/ (~3000 LoC) and cmd/veska-mcp/ need to move into importable packages with argv[0]-based dispatch. Filed separately so the install-UX win lands now without that scope. Phase A's release-archive ships fine with three binaries; phase B brings the install size down ~60MB by eliminating the duplicate model2vec embed.

PUBLIC RELEASE BLOCKED ON solov2-q2az: the curl|sh installer pointing at GitHub releases is blocked on the public repo decision. The local-build path (make install / make release-archive) is unblocked.

End-to-end verified: tarball builds (159MB compressed, the three fat binaries + install.sh + README), extracts, install.sh runs, binaries work — including the 'PATH not set' hint path.

title: Tree-sitter: add language parsers beyond Go
description: Currently internal/infrastructure/treesitter/ ships only go.go. Competitor Semble covers 19+ languages in its benchmarks. Until Veska parses TS/JS/Python/Rust/Java/Ruby/C/C++/etc., it cannot be evaluated on the polyglot repos most agents target. Priority gap surfaced during Semble competitive analysis (2026-05-24).
notes: DEFERRED 2026-05-26 by user direction — focusing on Go-only cleanup before language expansion. 1yev unblocked this so the work is now 'drop in queries/<lang>/*.scm + Go shim' rather than another 500-LoC walker per language, which makes future un-deferring cheap. Revisit when (a) the Go cleanup is substantially done and (b) there's a concrete user ask for a specific language.

title: Short-id prefix matching: docs say prefix; impl requires exact 12 chars
description:
README: 'you may pass the full id or that short prefix.' Reality: prefix 'f43242f9' (8 chars) returns 'unknown repo_id'; only the full 12-char short_id is accepted.

Either implement true prefix-matching (min length 4, ambiguity error if 2+ match), or update docs + add a hint in the NotFound message ('short_id is exactly 12 chars; see eng_list_repos').
acceptance_criteria: 1) Either prefix-matching works for >=4 chars, or NotFound mentions the 12-char requirement. 2) tests/mcp covers the chosen behavior.
close_reason: Already fixed in HEAD (internal/infrastructure/mcp/tools_helpers.go resolveRepoID step 3 supports >=4 char prefixes with explicit ambiguity error; NotFound message mentions the prefix minimum). The user's earlier failure was against a stale daemon binary. Verified after rebuilding: 8-char prefix 'f43242f9' now resolves cleanly to the full id.

title: 'veska search' with zero results prints nothing and exits 0
description:
