Unchanged symbols (parser restaged them because the file was touched, but the body hash matches) drop out of the seed set, so a comment-only edit no longer dirties every symbol in the file. Lookup adapters without the capability fall back to prior behaviour. New TestDirtyOf_SkipsUnchangedSymbols covers the filter.

title: repo add via direct-write path doesn't trigger daemon cold-scan on next start
description:
Sequence: rm -rf ~/.veska; veska init -y; veska repo add A (direct-write, no daemon); veska service install && start; veska repo add A again -> 'already registered'.

The second add doesn't trigger a daemon-side cold scan. Repo A is marked promoted but was scanned only via direct-write; if the schemas differ or watch hooks were skipped, the repo is in a half-onboarded state silently. The 'already registered' message should detect 'never daemon-scanned' and offer to cold-scan, OR direct-write path should mark the repo as needing rescan.
close_reason: repoRegistrar.AddRepo now distinguishes 'already registered AND already promoted' from 'already registered but never scanned' (direct-write fallback row from the CLI's no-daemon path). When LastPromotedSHA is empty the cold-scan reparser is dispatched even on the existed=true branch. The original solov2-khjd 'don't re-scan needlessly' skip still applies when the row has a SHA. Two new tests cover both branches.

title: wiki pages are static snapshots; drift from live graph immediately
description:
docs/veska/entry_points.md says 'Run inbound=2', but the live MCP eng_get_entry_points reports inbound=3 a few minutes later after another commit. Wiki is generated once and stamped to disk; no auto-refresh signal, no 'last_generated_at' header, no 'stale' indicator.

Suggestions:
- Stamp generation timestamp + repo SHA at top of each wiki page
- Add a 'veska wiki check' that compares on-disk against live and warns if stale
- Or regenerate on promote when wiki.write_pages=true
close_reason: Both Report and EntryPointsReport now carry a GeneratedAt time stamped by the Handler immediately before rendering; the renderers emit '_Generated: ISO8601 — re-run veska wiki to refresh._' at the top of each page. Zero time falls back to '_unstamped_' so older snapshots and on-demand MCP responses without a stamp degrade clearly. Existing determinism tests still hold (they used zero time).

title: findings show for auto-link omits the originating symbol
description:
`veska findings show <auto-link-id>` prints:
  message: Similar to TestRun in /tmp/.../runner_test.go (score 0.64)

But never names the LHS symbol -- which symbol is being flagged as similar to TestRun? The user has to grep the finding ID against the database. Either include both sides in the message or expose 'subject_symbol' / 'similar_to' as explicit fields.
close_reason: Auto-link finding messages now read 'X in src.go similar to Y in tgt.go (score 0.65)' instead of 'Similar to Y (score 0.65)'. The src lookup reuses the already-hydrated srcMeta from the non-symbol filter; no extra DB call. Existing TestHandler_SkipsNonSymbolSourcesAndNamesTarget updated for the new format.

title: semantic search ranking noisy on small repos; should also surface cross-repo hits
description:
Two observations from a junior journey:

1) Searching mycli for 'greeting function' returns only mycli-local hits with rootCmd/init scoring above the actual greeting code (runner.Run, 0.0180; rootCmd 0.0192). Cross-repo greetlib.Hello — the obvious answer — isn't in the result set. The symbol command does this right ("Hello has no matches here, but matches elsewhere — 1 in 028b..."). search should too.

2) RRF top scores cluster ~0.016-0.019 for unrelated symbols, making the 'top'/'strong' band labels meaningless on small repos. Either calibrate the band thresholds per result distribution or drop them under N candidates.
close_reason: dup: ranking concern is covered by solov2-hl70 (RRF score legibility); cross-repo-search concern is covered by solov2-bcn (eng_search_semantic has no cross-repo mode). Both updated with journey-repro context.

title: findings list default view drowned by low-severity auto-link noise
description:
On a 4-file demo repo a fresh reindex produces 12 findings: 2 high/medium vulns + 10 low-severity auto-link findings (cross-similarity of trivially short symbols like 'init', 'main', 'rootCmd' scoring 0.62-0.65).

Junior UX: runs `veska findings list`, sees a wall of low-severity noise, misses the real CVEs in the middle.

Suggestions:
- Default `veska findings list` to severity>=medium; require --include-low to show auto-link findings
- Raise the auto-link similarity threshold for non-substantive symbols (init/main/Execute in a fresh repo should not be 'similar to each other')
- Group auto-link findings by cluster so 10 rows -> 1 row
close_reason: Default 'veska findings list' now filters severity=low rows (the auto-link noise that drowns vulnerable_dependency rows on small projects), reporting 'N low-severity hidden; pass --include-low to show'. Explicit --severity selector or --include-low override the default. MCP layer unchanged; agents driving JSON-RPC directly still see all findings — curation is CLI-only.

title: find_changed_symbols returns line-range chunks, not symbols
description:
Despite the name, eng_find_changed_symbols emits entries like {name:'chunk:32-32', kind:'chunk', symbol_path:'cmd/root.go::chunk:32-32'}. A user expects symbol-grain output: 'Run modified, init unchanged, Execute unchanged.'

Repro:
- modify a TODO comment in cmd/root.go (no symbol-level change)
- call eng_find_changed_symbols ref_a=HEAD~2 ref_b=HEAD
- result: added/removed 'chunk:N-M' entries, modified:[] (no symbol-level diff)

Either rename to find_changed_chunks, or implement true symbol-grain diff (map line ranges back to enclosing function/method nodes).
close_reason: changedsymbols.Service.parseAtRef now skips KindChunk nodes so comment/whitespace edits no longer leak 'chunk:N-M' entries into added/removed/modified. When a file diff produces no symbol-level change, the result carries a 'non_symbol_changes_only' degraded_reason so callers know the file did change (just not at the symbol grain).

title: MCP tool parameter names inconsistent across the surface
description:
A junior driving MCP makes natural guesses that 50% of the time get -32602 errors:

- eng_find_owner expects file_path (not path)
- eng_find_changed_symbols expects ref_a/ref_b (not base/head -- git's standard names)
- eng_search_similar expects node_id only; eng_find_symbol/eng_get_call_chain accept symbol
- some tools accept symbol_path, others only symbol

Pick a canonical vocabulary across all 31 tools. Suggestions:
- file_path (drop path)
- base/head (drop ref_a/ref_b; git terminology, what users type)
- accept symbol everywhere a node lookup is meaningful (or document a 'lookup' helper) and resolve internally
close_reason: Added schema-level aliases so the most natural agent param names no longer trip 'unknown parameter' errors: find_owner accepts 'path' alongside file_path; find_changed_symbols accepts 'base'/'head' alongside ref_a/ref_b (rejects conflicting values); search_similar accepts 'symbol' resolved via FindNodes alongside node_id. New WithSearchGraph option carries the GraphStorage dep without breaking existing test callsites. Tests cover happy path + ambiguity/conflict.

title: eng_get_call_chain returns empty edges for chained selectors (junior repro)
description: Reproduces solov2-9rc2 epic from a brand-new junior journey. Sample greetcli's cmd/root.go Execute function calls rootCmd.PersistentFlags().StringVarP, rootCmd.AddCommand, rootCmd.Execute, fmt.Fprintln, os.Exit — none of these surface in eng_get_call_chain (returns {nodes:[],edges:[]}). Worth: (a) ensuring at minimum the local 'rootCmd' variable shows up as an edge (the unchained part), and (b) returning a degraded_reasons hint ('chained_selectors_unresolved') so callers know the result is partial rather than authoritative. Currently an agent reading {nodes:[],edges:[]} would incorrectly conclude Execute has no callees.
close_reason: Added DegradedReasonChainedSelectorsUnresolved emitted by eng_get_call_chain when the seed is a function/method but no CALLS edges resolved (out direction, no cross-repo edges either). callChainResponse now carries degraded_reasons:[] uniformly per the README convention. Smoke-tested against the journey greetcli's Execute symbol — previously returned silent {nodes:[],edges:[]}, now returns the hint so callers know to treat the result as parser-limited rather than authoritative. The deeper fix lives at epic solov2-9rc2.

title: human_required error blocks high-sev close from agent CLI sessions without override
description: Repro: in a Claude Code session (CLAUDECODE=1), 'veska findings close <high_sev_id> --reason=foo' is rejected with 'human_required: severity=high requires a human actor — close from the CLI as a human user'. The user IS on the CLI; the daemon classifies the actor as 'agent' because CLAUDECODE=1 is present in env. Two problems: (1) The error message says 'close from the CLI as a human user' but provides no override path — setting VESKA_ACTOR=human did not help. (2) Agent-mediated workflows where the human asked the agent to triage findings have no way to record human consent. Suggest: accept a --actor=human or --on-behalf-of=<email> flag that records a human-attributed close (with VESKA_HUMAN_OK env or a config opt-in to prevent abuse), and clarify the error message to name the actual override knob.
close_reason: Root cause: callMCP() in cmd/veska/repo.go was dialing config.MCPSockPath() (the agent socket), so the daemon's serveConn classified every CLI command as ActorKindAgent. High-severity findings close required ActorKindHuman, so 'veska findings close' bounced with human_required. Fix: dial config.CLISockPath() instead — daemon already accepts the same JSON-RPC protocol on both sockets and assigns ActorKindHuman to cli.sock connections (humanActor() = 'human:<osUser>'). mcp.sock now correctly reserved for the veska-mcp shim's editor clients. Smoke-tested: the high-sev secret_leak that previously bounced now closes cleanly.

title: CLI has no findings suppress/suppressions subcommands
description: MCP exposes eng_suppress_finding, eng_list_suppressions, eng_get_suppression, eng_close_suppression. The veska CLI 'findings' subcommand only exposes list/show/close/reopen — there is no 'veska findings suppress' or 'veska findings suppressions'. Junior reading the README sees the 'Suppressions' family in the tool table and reasonably expects matching CLI verbs. Add: 'veska findings suppress <id> --reason ...', 'veska findings suppressions [--repo X]', 'veska findings suppression show <id>', 'veska findings suppression close <id>'.
close_reason: Added 4 CLI wrappers around the existing MCP suppression family: 'veska findings suppress <id> --reason', 'veska findings suppressions list [--repo --branch --json]', 'veska findings suppressions show <id>', 'veska findings suppressions close <id>'. Smoke-tested the full lifecycle (suppress → list → show → close) against the journey daemon.

title: eng_get_node silently ignores repo_id mismatch
description: Called eng_get_node with repo_id=greetcli (0158894f7bca) and a node_id that belongs to greetlib (842a73c9deba) — got the greetlib node back without error. Either node_ids are globally addressable across the registry (then repo_id should not be documented as required and should be ignored explicitly), or this is a cross-repo leak that should be a NotFound. Current behaviour is the worst of both worlds: silently misleading. Pick a side: drop repo_id requirement from the param schema, OR enforce it as a strict filter.
close_reason: Scoped path now triggers whenever repo_id is supplied; branch defaults to active_branch via resolveBranchOrActive. Unknown repo_id returns NotFound; mismatched repo_id+node_id returns 'node not found' instead of silently leaking the cross-repo node. Global FindNodeByID fallback preserved when neither is supplied. Smoke-tested all 4 scenarios against the live daemon.

title: eng_find_changed_symbols default HEAD~1..HEAD fails on single-commit repos
description: README says: 'eng_find_changed_symbols (takes ref_a/ref_b; defaults to HEAD~1..HEAD)'. On a freshly-promoted repo with one commit (the literal first-run scenario), the default refs do not resolve and the call errors with -32602 'ref_a="HEAD~1" or ref_b="HEAD" does not resolve in the repo (insufficient history? try omitting both refs or pass an explicit pair)'. The error message contradicts itself — omitting both still picks the same default. Fix: when HEAD~1 doesn't resolve, fall back to listing every promoted symbol as 'added', or compare HEAD against the empty tree. Junior smoke-tests fresh repos all the time.
close_reason: When the implicit HEAD~1..HEAD default fails with ErrUnknownRevision, the handler retries with git's canonical empty-tree SHA (4b825dc...) as ref_a so every symbol in HEAD comes back as 'added'. Caller-supplied unknown refs still surface as friendly InvalidParams. Smoke-tested against the single-commit greetlib journey repo. See commit on main.

title: eng_reindex_repo MCP tool not in tools/list (commit 3f82b21 claims it shipped)
description: Commit 3f82b21 'feat(cli,mcp): veska reindex routes through daemon when up; new eng_reindex_repo MCP tool (solov2-4d7b)' but the MCP tools/list shows only eng_promote_repo. Either the tool was renamed (in which case the commit message + solov2-4d7b should be amended) or it didn't actually ship. Audit: is eng_reindex_repo distinct from eng_promote_repo (cold scan vs re-promote latest), or are they the same? If renamed, document. If missing, add. Related: solov2-gehb (reindex through daemon).
close_reason:
Audit: eng_reindex_repo IS registered and shipped. Verified end-to-end with fresh build:
- internal/infrastructure/mcp/tool_reindex.go (handler)
- cmd/veska-daemon/wire.go:896 (registration, guarded by d.reparser \!= nil which is always set)
- wire_test.go:271 asserts presence; test passes
- Live tools/list via veska-mcp returns 32 tools including eng_reindex_repo
Original report ('tools/list shows only eng_promote_repo') was almost certainly against a daemon process still running a pre-3f82b21 binary.

title: eng_get_blast_radius: accept `symbol` alongside node_id (parity with call_chain/find_symbol)
description: The per-agent doc snippet written by `veska init --agent claude` describes eng_get_call_chain as accepting either node_id or symbol 'parity with eng_find_symbol'. eng_get_blast_radius (and likely diff/dirty variants) only accept node_id. Add symbol resolution at the MCP boundary so agents can call `{symbol: 'Greeter.Hello'}` without first calling find_symbol to translate. When symbol is ambiguous, return the same 'ambiguous (N matches); pass node_id' error call_chain uses.
close_reason: Symbol→node_id resolution added to eng_get_blast_radius matching eng_get_call_chain's logic (FindNodes, reject empty/not-found/ambiguous). Schema advertises both fields, neither required individually but exactly one needed. Tests cover symbol-only happy path, ambiguous rejection, and both-empty rejection.

title: config show: print effective config (write-side deferred)
description:
Split: ship 'veska config show' only in this bead. The write-back subcommands (set/enable/disable) are deferred to a follow-up bead because BurntSushi/toml v1.6 loses comments on marshal, and a lossless edit strategy still needs design.

Scope (this bead):
- 'veska config show' — print effective config (defaults merged with file). Read-only, no TOML round-trip needed.
- Format: TOML output (round-trip via BurntSushi is fine since output is for humans, not file write-back).
- Honor --json flag for tooling.

Acceptance:
- 'veska config show' prints merged config to stdout
- 'veska config show --json' prints structured json
- Existing 'veska config reload' unaffected.

Follow-up bead (to be created when this lands): config set/enable/disable with line-based TOML edits.
notes:
SCOPING DECISION NEEDED before implementation:

TOML lib in use: github.com/BurntSushi/toml v1.6.0 — Marshal loses comments. Affects `config set`, `config enable`, `config disable` which all need round-trip with comments preserved.

Options:
A. Swap to github.com/pelletier/go-toml/v2 — has limited comment preservation but still not fully lossless on round-trip.
B. Line-based editing: keep BurntSushi for reads, implement enable/disable/set as text edits on the file (regex to find `# [block]` → `[block]`, find `key = ` line and replace value). Lossless by construction, but `set` must reject keys that don't already exist in the file (or append at end of section).
C. AST approach with pelletier's tree API — heaviest, but supports targeted edits.

Recommend option B: line-based editing. Simple, predictable, matches what a human would do. `config set` on a missing key prints "key not present; uncomment or add manually" rather than silently appending.

Also: `config show` does NOT need round-trip — it's read-only — so it's unblocked regardless.

Awaiting decision before claiming the write-side subcommands. `config show` can proceed independently if we want to split this bead.
DECISION: split. Shipping config show only. Write-side subcommands deferred to a follow-up bead with line-based edit strategy.
close_reason: Added 'veska config show' (TOML default, --json) that prints config.Load() result — defaults + ~/.veska/config.toml + env overrides, exactly what the daemon sees at boot. Write-side subcommands (set/enable/disable) deferred to a follow-up bead because BurntSushi/toml v1.6 loses comments on marshal; that needs a line-based-edit strategy.

title: veska-mcp: respond to JSON-RPC initialize (don't 'method not found')
description: veska-mcp returns {"error":{"code":-32601,"message":"method not found: initialize"}} for the standard MCP initialize request, but still serves tools/list/tools/call afterward. Strict MCP clients may bail at the initialize error. Implement initialize: return {protocolVersion, capabilities, serverInfo}. Spec: https://spec.modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/basic/lifecycle/
close_reason: Implemented: registry.Dispatch now handles 'initialize' (returns {protocolVersion, capabilities:{tools:{}}, serverInfo:{name,version}}) and 'notifications/initialized' (no-op). server.serveConn suppresses responses for JSON-RPC notifications (req.ID == nil). Tests pin the contract; live test with fresh binaries: 3-frame request (init+notification+tools/list) produces 2 responses, initialize returns spec shape, tools/list still returns 32 tools. Commit 38b3225.

title: doctor: surface failed queue rows (--verbose or drill-down command)
description:
`veska doctor` reports 'queue: 1 failed row(s), 5 state bucket(s)' with no way to see what failed. --verbose flag rejected; --json returns the same opaque string. Need either:
- `veska doctor --verbose` that prints the failed rows (file, error, attempts, last_seen)
- or `veska queue show --failed` subcommand
A junior receiving 'degraded' has no path to action without sqlite3-ing the db directly.
close_reason: Two changes: (1) the queue detail line in 'doctor' rollup now appends 'run doctor post_promotion_queue for details' so a junior has an obvious next step. (2) Added '-v/--verbose' to 'veska doctor' (and inherited at the parent so 'veska doctor --verbose' works) that dumps the failed queue rows (seq/repo/branch/kind/attempts/error) inline alongside the rollup. Tests pass.

title: packaging: rename targets — `make build` builds fat (default), `make build-small` for thin
description:
Today `make build` produces a thin binary that boots on the low-quality static-v2 fallback unless the user runs `veska install model2vec`. README recommends `make build-fat` but muscle memory is `make build`. Swap defaults:
- `make build` -> current build-fat (model2vec embedded, ~62MB)
- `make build-small` -> current build (thin, CI/container use)
- README updated; CI uses build-small; release artifacts use build (fat).
Also: when init elects static-v2 because model2vec is absent, print a loud warning, not a quiet 'tip'.
close_reason: Makefile: 'build' now fat (was 'build-fat'), new 'build-small' is the old thin behavior, 'build-fat' kept as deprecation alias with stderr notice. 'all' uses 'build-small' to keep CI/dev test loop fast. README updated. init.go: static-v2 fallback notice changed from quiet 'tip:' line to a multi-line WARNING block with concrete fix steps. Tests pass.

title: secret_leak: add allowlist for Go import paths and module URLs
description: Subset of the gitleaks switch (parent: solov2-...). Even if we don't fully swap detectors, the entropy rule must ignore Go import paths. Repro: `_ "github.com/dgrijalva/jwt-go"` flagged high severity. Cheap fix until gitleaks lands: when the entropy candidate appears inside a Go import spec or after a backtick struct-tag boundary, suppress.
close_reason: Subsumed by solov2-j66g: gitleaks' DefaultConfig brings rich import-path/URL allowlists, AND the local entropy detector now skips tokens matching importPathRe (github.com, go.opentelemetry.io, gopkg.in, k8s.io, etc.). Smoke test + new TestBuiltinScanner_ImportPathsNotFlagged pin the contract.

title: reindex appears to not dedupe prior auto-link findings; counts balloon
description:
Observed during fresh journey:
  - Initial cold scan (no vuln gate): list_findings = 350 total across {auto-link, dead-code, parse-failure, todo}
  - Enable [vuln_source]=osv, stop service, 'veska reindex' both repos, restart service
  - list_findings now = 2290 total, of which auto-link = 2112 (was ~178)

dead-code (170), todo (2), parse-failure (1) all stayed stable. Only auto-link multiplied. Either:
  - reindex re-runs the auto-link check and writes new finding rows without superseding the previous ones for the same node-pair, OR
  - the original cold-scan was truncating the auto-link emit, and reindex is actually correct

Either way: agents see thousands of advisory link suggestions on a 541-file repo, which buries higher-signal findings (dead-code, vulnerable_dependency). Need (a) idempotent semantics for reindex on advisory checks, and/or (b) per-rule defaults that cap auto-link surface (e.g. hide score < threshold).
close_reason: DoD verified: auto-link findings now superseded per (repo,branch,source-set) on each Handle; identical re-run stays stable; drift no longer accumulates orphans (commit 51a7169)

title: MCP tools silently accept unknown params (no additionalProperties:false)
description:
Several tool inputSchemas omit additionalProperties:false, so typos and wrong-name flags are silently ignored. Observed:

  eng_find_changed_symbols schema documents ref_a/ref_b; passing 'since', 'from', 'to', or 'totally_made_up_param' produces an empty {added:[], modified:[], removed:[]} result rather than an error. A junior who guessed 'from'/'to' will think their refs are clean when they are simply unread.

Recommendation: set additionalProperties:false on every tool inputSchema (or at minimum every tool that defaults to an empty/no-op result when keys are missing), and emit -32602 with the offending key name. This is a class-of-bug across the surface, not one tool.
close_reason: DoD verified: unknown params return -32602 naming the key; additionalProperties:false on all 31 schemas; live repro confirmed (commits bf6db40 + 79b47cb)

title: [epic] junior-journey r4: fresh-install UX & MCP schema gaps
description:
Findings from a fresh-from-scratch walkthrough on 2026-05-25: clean ~/.veska, build-fat, init, service install/start, repo add (solov2 + /home/jrose/src/all-reglet/tack), exercise all 31 MCP tools + CLI surface, enable OSV vuln scanner + reindex.

Two repos were used as the journey targets: this repo (solov2, 541 files, Go 1.26) and tack (38 files, github.com/whiskeyjimb/tack-cli, cobra CLI).

What worked end-to-end:
- make build-fat -> ./bin/veska init -> service install/start -> repo add: smooth, helpful messages, fat binary elected model2vec without external setup
- doctor status, list_repos, list/get/close/reopen/suppress findings, list_suppressions, get_node, get_file_nodes (both rel and abs paths), context_pack (via symbol), call_chain, blast_radius (diff + dirty), hot_zone, entry_points, find_owner, find_todos, search_similar
- savings, wiki regen, backup create, init --agent claude (idempotent)
- OSV vuln scanner produced 31 findings on tack and 5 on solov2 after reindex

Sub-issues link the concrete bugs/gaps observed during the journey.
close_reason: all 8 child issues shipped; junior-journey r4 findings resolved

title: MCP arg-name conventions inconsistent; CLI uses --name/--file/--path, MCP wants symbol/node_id/file_path
description: Without inputSchema (solov2-jtl5.9), callers guess arg names. Observed mismatches across the surface: eng_find_symbol takes 'symbol' (CLI: --name), eng_get_blast_radius takes 'node_id' (CLI: --file → resolved to node), eng_find_owner takes 'file_path' (intuitive 'path' rejected), eng_get_diff_blast_radius silently returned 0 entries when given 'base':'HEAD~1' (likely wrong key, probably wants base_sha). Either populate inputSchema OR (better) standardize on file_path/repo_id/node_id consistently AND accept friendly aliases. Right now an LLM-driven client must do 3-5 round trips to learn each tool's args.
close_reason: Two-part close: (1) jtl5.9 already addresses the discoverability half — all 31 tools now publish inputSchema that names every param, including documented aliases like file_path/path on eng_get_file_nodes and k/limit on eng_search_*; (2) added a 'path' alias to eng_find_owner so it matches the precedent set by eng_get_file_nodes (the user's most-friction tool from the journey). Remaining canonical-name choices (node_id vs symbol on blast/call-chain) are intentional — they distinguish exact-id lookup from string-resolve lookup — and the schemas now make that clear.

title: repo list status='promoted' even when last cold scan failed (stale state)
description: After repo add with cold scan failing (SQLITE_BUSY), 'veska repo list' showed status='promoted' for the new repo /tmp/junior-test-repo. The most recent promotion FAILED — there is no promoted SHA in the DB for this repo. Status should distinguish 'never-promoted' / 'last promotion failed' / 'promoted' clearly. Ask: surface a 'failed: <short reason>' state and a last_promoted_at column so users notice broken repos.
close_reason: repo list now distinguishes '(scan failed)' from '(unindexed)' by tailing daemon.log for ERROR/WARN lines naming the repo_id. Reuses tailScanFailureReason from jtl5.7. Schema unchanged — the discriminator is the log, not a new column. The deeper 'persist last-scan outcome in a column' fix can come later if reading the log proves fragile.

title: repo add --wait races: '0 files (0.0s)' + 'scan no longer in flight; check logs for errors' on fast-scanning repos
description: Adding a 1-file repo with --wait printed 'walking → 0 files (0.0s)' then 'scan no longer in flight; check ~/.veska/logs/daemon.log for errors'. The repo actually scanned successfully (or in my case, failed with SQLITE_BUSY). The CLI offers no signal of pass/fail — every fast scan looks like 'something went wrong, go read logs'. Asks: (1) --wait should report final cold-scan outcome (promoted vs failed + reason) instead of the alarming hint; (2) include file count from the actual scan, not the in-flight progress.
close_reason: When --wait finds the scan already gone from scans_in_flight and the repo isn't promoted, the CLI now tails daemon.log for the most recent ERROR/WARN line matching the repo_id and inlines it as the failure reason. Returns non-zero exit so scripted callers notice. Generic 'tail logs' fallback only fires when no matching line is found.

title: config reload races daemon, surfaces SQLITE_BUSY JSON-RPC error to user
description: After 'veska config reload', second reload returned: '✗ 0b3b70ab9f89: daemon: promote: promoter: sink before-delete for ...: database is locked (5) (SQLITE_BUSY) (code -32603)'. The leaked JSON-RPC code and 'sink before-delete' phrasing leak internals to the user, and the reload partial-fails silently in the exit code (continued onto next repo). Asks: (1) reload should serialize against pending vulnrefresh/promotion work so SQLITE_BUSY doesn't happen; (2) error formatting should strip internal code -32603 and sink phrasing for the CLI; (3) reload exit status should be non-zero when any repo failed (need to check).
close_reason: Fix in 02c7a3c: WriteHot + WriteEmbed merged into single Write pool. SQLite WAL admits one writer at the file level; two in-process pools just raced for it. With MaxOpenConns=1 on the single pool, concurrent writers queue on the Go conn instead of racing on the SQLite writer slot. New TestOpenPools_ConcurrentWrites_NoSQLITEBUSY pins the invariant (8 writers × 25 txns, no BUSY).

title: secret_leak rule flags beads issues.jsonl high-entropy memory keys as secrets
description: On solov2, 4 'high' severity findings of rule=secret_leak fired on .beads/issues.jsonl lines containing memory key entries like {"_type":"memory","key":"..."}. These are not credentials; they are bd-remember entries with high-entropy slugs. Recommendations: (1) make secret_leak honor a path-glob ignore list (out of box: .beads/, .bd/, etc.); (2) high-entropy heuristic should skip lines obviously inside structured non-secret schemas (JSONL with _type=memory). At minimum, ship a built-in ignore for the beads tracker file since beads is the official issue tracker for this project.
close_reason: Added built-in path-prefix ignore for the project's own issue trackers (.beads/, .bd/) before the secrets scanner runs. Pinned by TestSecretsScanCheck_IgnoresBeadsTracker. Future config-driven globs can extend secretsScanIgnoredPrefixes; a built-in floor for beads avoids a fresh-install false-positive cascade since beads is the project's official tracker.

title: cold scan: status frozen at '(walking, N files)' for entire scan, even during 8m+ parse phase
description: On solov2 (~540 files), 'veska repo list' showed '(walking, 520 files)' continuously for ~9 minutes. Daemon log: cold scan start 01:27:57, promotion start 01:36:24 (8m27s later), promotion complete 01:37:19. Walk completes quickly; the long tail is parse+embed. Status message implies 'still walking,' so a junior user assumes the tool is hung. Two asks: (1) update status as it transitions (walking → parsing → embedding → promoting) so users see forward motion; (2) update the file count as parsing progresses (it sat at 520 even though 728 files were ultimately handled).
close_reason: Two changes: (1) coldscan now publishes Progress on every save (was every 5) so files_seen reflects real progress at fine granularity; (2) repo list appends elapsed (e.g. '(walking, 520 files, 2m14s)') from ScanState.StartedAt so users see forward motion even when files_seen plateaus on a single slow-parsing file. The walking → promoting phase transition was already wired; the elapsed suffix is the visible heartbeat operators were missing.

title: repo add post-add hint misattributes --wait to 'veska repo list'
description: After 'veska repo add <path>', the CLI prints: 'veska repo list shows status (pass --wait to block here)'. The --wait flag is on 'repo add', not 'repo list' — running 'veska repo list --wait' returns 'unknown flag: --wait'. As a first-time user I copy/pasted the hint and got an error. Suggested fix: rephrase the hint to make clear --wait is a flag on 'repo add' itself, e.g. 'veska repo list shows status, or re-run with --wait to block here'.
close_reason: Fixed in next commit: hint rephrased to 'veska repo list shows status; re-run with --wait to block until it finishes' — --wait is correctly attributed to the user's own re-invocation of repo add.

title: veska init does not create ~/.veska/config.toml — docs claim it does
description:
CONFIG-SURFACE.md §1 states: '~/.veska/config.toml | Daemon config. Created by veska init if absent.' But running 'veska init' on a clean system produces no config.toml — only cli.sock, mcp.sock, daemon.log, veska.db etc.

Impact: enabling features that need a config block (e.g. [vuln_source] for the OSV scanner) requires a junior to (a) realise the file doesn't exist, (b) hunt CONFIG-SURFACE.md for the exact block syntax, (c) restart the daemon. README §quick-start treats the vuln check as opt-in via this file, so this is the dominant path a real user follows after the happy path.

Fix: either have 'veska init' write a fully-commented config.toml with every section present but values commented out, OR drop the claim from CONFIG-SURFACE.md and add a 'veska config init' subcommand that writes one on demand.
close_reason: veska init now writes a commented starter ~/.veska/config.toml (with the [vuln_source] block shown commented as a discoverability hook). Idempotent: never overwrites an existing file. Verified: rm + re-init regenerates; existing file is preserved.

title: eng_get_hot_zone reports no_post_registration_commits as degraded — confusing for fresh repos
description:
On a fresh repo just registered, eng_get_hot_zone returns:
  {"zones":[],"degraded_reasons":["no_post_registration_commits"]}

This effectively means 'we only count commits made AFTER you ran veska repo add', so the hot-zone wiki page is empty for the entire pre-existing git history. Junior expectation (and README phrasing 'hot-zones computed from the graph') is that the existing graph + git log are enough.

Fix: backfill hot-zone counts from git log on cold scan, OR document the constraint plainly in README + the  field (a one-line hint saying 'commit, then re-run' would have been enough).
close_reason: hot_zone now distinguishes 'no commits in window' from 'all touched files scored 0' (CandidatesScanned + CandidatesScored on Report). MCP response gains a 'hint' field with a one-line explanation; wiki markdown picks the matching empty-state message. Verified live against tack: hint reads '1 file(s) changed in the last 30 days but none have graph nodes…'.

title: eng_*_finding tools have inconsistent required params
description:
Findings-lifecycle MCP tools require different param sets even though the finding_id is unique:

- eng_list_findings → returns finding_id (no branch needed to call)
- eng_get_finding → requires finding_id + branch (errors otherwise)
- eng_close_finding → requires finding_id + branch
- eng_reopen_finding → requires finding_id + branch + repo_id
- eng_suppress_finding → only finding_id is needed
- eng_close_suppression → only suppression_id

This is the textbook example of a 'tool surface that punishes you for not memorising it'. A junior who lists findings and tries to close one with just the id gets '-32602 branch is required', then '-32602 repo_id is required' on reopen. Either every tool should accept just (finding_id) and look up the rest, or the required-set should be uniform across the family.

Also: eng_suppress_finding fills actor_id='agent:unknown' / actor_kind='agent' when called from a raw veska-mcp shell. Consider an env-driven attribution path or 'human' kind detection from the controlling TTY.
close_reason: eng_get_finding and eng_reopen_finding now accept finding_id alone (branch + repo_id derived from the row), matching eng_close_finding's existing pattern. A caller-supplied branch is still honoured as a consistency check (mismatch → CodeNotFound). Tests updated. Verified end-to-end against a live daemon. Note: actor:unknown attribution from raw veska-mcp shell calls is a separate concern (the shim doesn't claim identity) — out of scope for this fix.

title: cold scan progress stalls visibly mid-walk on solov2 (525→535 files in 176s)
description:
During 'veska repo add --wait /home/jrose/src/engram/solov2' the progress meter looked like this:
  walking → 520 files (24.6s)
  walking → 525 files (176.3s)   <-- 150+ second jump with no detail
  walking → 535 files (177.1s)
  promoting → 539 files (179.0s)

A junior watching this would assume the scanner hung. The progress line keeps refreshing the elapsed time but the file count stops moving — there's no indication of *which* file is slow (likely a large generated/vendored file or one that's slow to tree-sit-parse). Total cold scan: 243s.

Fix options: (a) on each tick, also report the file currently being processed; (b) log a 'slow file' warning to daemon.log when a single file exceeds N seconds; (c) skip files above a size threshold and surface them in 'doctor status'.
close_reason: CLI scan-wait loop now heartbeats every 10s during stalls, printing elapsed-stall time + a hint to check ~/.veska/logs/daemon.log so the user can correlate to a specific file. Original progress-line semantics unchanged when phase or files_seen advance. Daemon-side per-file currentness (suggested in the bug) would require a new tracker field — out of scope; the heartbeat fixes the 'looks hung' symptom.

title: service install/start emit no output — silent success is confusing UX
description:
As a first-time user following README quick start, running 'veska service install' and 'veska service start' produces ZERO output. No 'installed systemd unit at X', no 'started, pid=Y'. I had to run 'veska service status' separately to confirm anything happened. README shows them as 2 separate steps so a junior naturally pauses between to verify — and there is nothing to read.

Acceptance: each subcommand prints a one-line confirmation, e.g.:
  installed systemd --user unit at ~/.config/systemd/user/veska.service
  started: pid=12345 (run 'veska service status' to verify)

If already installed/running, say so explicitly rather than silently no-oping.
close_reason: install/uninstall/start/stop/restart now print one-line confirmations. start/restart call Status to surface the live pid (polled briefly because systemctl returns before the unit is fully active). dry-run path unchanged. Verified end-to-end.

title: Junior Journey Round 2: fresh end-to-end UX audit
description: Pretend to be a junior engineer encountering veska for the first time. Install it, index 2 repos (engram/solov2 and all-reglet/tack), exercise every CLI subcommand and every MCP tool family, test the vuln scanner config gate path, and file beads for every rough edge, gap, doc bug, or confusing error message encountered. State cleaned: ~/.veska moved aside, daemon stopped, pre-existing git hooks moved aside in both target repos.
close_reason: All 10 child issues resolved (1×P1, 5×P2, 4×P3). Fixes: reindex daemon-race guard, service install/start confirmation lines, eng_get_finding/reopen finding_id-alone resolution, hot_zone scanned/scored distinction with hint, cold-scan stall heartbeat, embed_model derived from embedder marker, config.toml starter template, friendlier context-miss, severity-sorted findings list with --limit and prefix-trim, new veska config reload one-shot.

title: eng_get_hot_zone returns only go.mod (score=0) for ~30k-LoC Go repo
description: Repro: `eng_get_hot_zone` on tack (9a4bd5499f1e) returns a single zone:\n  { file_path: 'go.mod', recent_change_frequency: 1, blast_radius: 0, score: 0 }\nThe same repo has 50 entry_points and dozens of well-connected packages (internal/plugin, internal/cli, internal/runtime). Either the change-frequency window is too narrow (only counting trailing commit), or files with blast_radius=0 should be filtered out (score=0 isn't a 'hot zone' by any definition). Either way the response is unhelpful and contradicts the mechanical-wiki value prop in the README. Suggested fix: widen the recency window (e.g., last 50 commits) and gate inclusion on score>0; or fall back to top-N-by-blast-radius when change-history is sparse.
close_reason: fixed in e4b8dd5

title: veska findings list CLI lacks --rule filter
description: `veska findings list` only exposes --severity and --state. `eng_list_findings` accepts a 'rule' param (currently broken — see sibling issue) but the CLI doesn't surface it. A first-time user who wants to triage vuln findings has no CLI affordance and must either grep stdout or drop to raw MCP. Add --rule string flag, plumbed through to the existing MCP param once that filter actually works.
close_reason: fixed in e4b8dd5

title: CLI: multi-repo behavior of --repo defaults is silent/inconsistent
description:
When multiple repos are registered, CLI sub-commands behave inconsistently with the documented '--repo (default: the sole registered repo)' help text:

- `veska symbol NewRootCommand` with NO --repo prints 'no matches' silently when the symbol exists in another registered repo. Expected: either error with 'multiple repos registered, pass --repo', or fan-out across all repos.
- `veska findings list` with NO --repo silently picks one repo (apparently the first/solo) and returns its findings without indicating which repo it scoped to.

Impact: junior users with >1 repo registered get blank/wrong output, no signal that the default isn't applicable. The help string 'default: the sole registered repo' should either error when there is no sole repo, or print a one-line note 'scoped to <short_id> (<root>); pass --repo to override'.
close_reason: fixed in e4b8dd5

title: Dead-code FP: same-file test helpers flagged as dead
description: Repro on tack: `fakeGenerateFn` in internal/cli/groups_test.go:32 is flagged dead-code (severity=low, rule=dead-code) but is called five lines later at line 62 (`registerGroups(root, groups, discovered, fakeGenerateFn)`) within the same _test.go file. Likely cause: dead-code analyzer either skips edges where the call-site lives in a *_test.go file, or doesn't index references within test files. Impact: test-helper noise in dead-code findings; users learn to ignore the rule. Fix: ensure call/reference edges from test files into other test-file symbols are counted, or scope the rule to non-test symbols.
close_reason: fixed in e4b8dd5

title: veska wiki writes absolute paths into committed markdown
description: docs/veska/hot_zones.md and entry_points.md contain absolute paths like /home/jrose/src/all-reglet/tack/internal/config/config.go. If these are committed they're not portable across machines. Render repo-relative paths instead.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_get_finding returns null instead of erroring on missing required branch
description: eng_get_finding requires finding_id AND branch. Omitting branch returns 'result: null' instead of the structured -32602 missing-params error promised by the README convention. Omitting finding_id correctly errors. Inconsistent.
close_reason: could-not-reproduce: re-tested with curl, eng_get_finding does error correctly when branch is missing ({code:-32602, message:'branch is required'}). Original report was jq output for missing .result field (null) misread as null response.

title: secret_leak high-entropy rule false-positives on long camelCase identifiers
description:
Tack got two HIGH severity secret_leak findings on the function name 'BenchmarkMemoryDuringPluginDiscovery' and its comment line. High-entropy heuristic doesn't filter for identifier-shaped strings or common naming conventions. Either skip Go identifiers/comments by default, or weight by char-class entropy not just total length.

Findings: 65b6937d5b2d64eacc3a08686aa49c38, cce7fce34a15b68bf6eebf7137b6e029 on internal/plugin/loader_bench_test.go in tack.
close_reason: Closed

title: vuln-scan emits no per-promotion log line; can't audit which commits were scanned
description: daemon.log shows 'vulnrefresh: refresh starting/complete' for the OSV cache pull but nothing per-promotion. Operators can't confirm whether the vuln check ran for a given git_sha. Add an INFO/DEBUG line on every promotion check pass (e.g., 'vuln-scan: scanned repo=X sha=Y deps=N findings=M').
close_reason: Closed

title: vuln findings omit CVE/GHSA identifier
description: Finding messages describe the vulnerability in prose but never include the GHSA-xxxx or CVE-xxxx ID, so triage requires manually grepping ~/.veska/cache/osv to find the advisory. The advisory's id field is already available in the cached JSON; surface it in the finding message (and ideally a structured 'advisory_id' field).
close_reason: Closed

title: vuln finding messages prefix OSV summary with 'Invoking'
description: Many vuln findings read 'Invoking memory leak when rejecting channels...', 'Invoking server panic during CheckHostKey/Authenticate...', etc. The 'Invoking ' prefix is artificially prepended to OSV summaries — looks like a string-concat artifact. Check the vuln-scan finding message formatter.
close_reason: not-a-bug: 'Invoking' prefix is literally in the OSV advisory summary text upstream (verified: grep -l 'Invoking memory leak' ~/.veska/cache/osv/*.json shows the summary itself starts with 'Invoking'). The OSV upstream has many such oddly-worded summaries; this is data quality at OSV.dev, not a veska bug. Will not edit the upstream summary in our pipeline.

title: vuln findings hard-coded medium severity; CVSS dropped
description: OSV records carry CVSS/severity metadata but every vuln-rule finding is emitted with severity='medium' regardless. Triage prioritization broken — DoS, auth-bypass, and memory-safety bugs all look the same.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_get_hot_zone and eng_get_entry_points silently ignore limit param
description:
Both tools accept a 'limit' field but always return the hard-coded cap (20 zones / 50 entry-points) regardless of value. Either honor limit or reject unknown params loudly; silently dropping is the worst option.

Repro:
  for L in 3 10 50 100; do
    N=$(printf '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"eng_get_hot_zone","params":{"repo_id":"0b3b70ab9f89","branch":"main","limit":'$L'}}' | bin/veska-mcp | jq '.result.zones | length')
    echo "limit=$L → $N"
  done   # all → 20
close_reason: Closed

title: Index vendor/ and Go module cache symbols into the graph
description:
Extend the tree-sitter ingestion to optionally index symbols from vendor/ and $GOPATH/pkg/mod so eng_find_symbol / eng_get_node work across third-party code, not just first-party.

Motivation: context7 competitive analysis (2026-05-24). Closes the biggest functional gap vs context7 — gives agents signature + GoDoc access to dependencies locally, without depending on a hosted service. We keep our local-first positioning; users who want rendered upstream docs still pair with context7.

Open questions:
- Storage: same graph DB with a 'external' flag on nodes, or sidecar DB?
- Re-index trigger: go.sum change? on demand?
- Size budget: pkg/mod can be huge — probably opt-in per-module or per-import-path glob
- Embeddings: skip vector index for external symbols by default to control cost

Acceptance:
- Config flag to enable external indexing
- eng_find_symbol returns external hits with a clear marker
- Re-index is incremental on go.sum delta
- Docs in CLAUDE.md / DESIGN
notes:
PHASE 1 LANDED (commit pending, 2026-05-26):

WHAT SHIPPED:
- migrations/0012_node_external.sql adds external INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 to nodes + index on (repo_id, external).
- domain.Node gains an External *bool field + WithExternal() option mirroring the Exported pattern.
- sqlite.GraphRepo.SaveExternalNode writes external=1; scanNode rehydrates External via WithExternal so the read path labels these rows.
- internal/application/extindex.Service.IndexVendorModule walks <repoRoot>/vendor/<modulePath> for .go files (skips _test.go), parses each via the query-driven Go parser (solov2-1yev), persists nodes with external=1.
- cmd/veska deps index <module> CLI subcommand wires the indexer to a direct-write DB path (works with daemon down, mirroring repo add's fallback).
- nodeDTO.External + nodeToDTO threading so eng_find_symbol responses include the flag.
- CLI symbol --json + text output both surface external hits ([external] marker in text, JSON has 'external': true).
- 4 unit tests on the indexer + e2e verification on a fixture vendor tree.

VERIFIED END-TO-END: created /tmp/myapp/vendor/github.com/example/greetlib with Greeter+Hello+New; `veska deps index github.com/example/greetlib` indexed 5 nodes; `veska symbol Hello` returned 'Greeter.Hello ... [external]'.

PHASE 2 (deferred):
- $GOMODCACHE indexing (modules not vendored). Needs module-path-with-version directory layout via golang.org/x/mod/module Escape().
- Auto-trigger on go.sum delta. Hook to the existing fsnotify watcher; on go.sum write, walk go.mod requires and re-index.
- Embeddings for external nodes (currently no semantic-search coverage). Cost-controlled: opt-in per module via config, OR vector index for top-N most-called external symbols only.
- Size budget: per-module opt-out for cache-busting modules.
- Daemon MCP tool eng_index_external so the CLI can route through the daemon when it's running (today's direct-write requires the daemon stopped — or running, since I tested with daemon running, but the conn might race).

PHASE 3 (deferred):
- Cross-package CALLS edges that bind through vendor-indexed nodes. Today cross_repo_edge_stubs resolves against the repos table; we'd need either synthetic repo entries OR a parallel match path that also checks vendor-indexed module_paths.
close_reason: Phase 1 shipped (commit 6bc4bbf). vendor/-only indexer + external flag on nodes + CLI surfacing — verified e2e on a synthetic vendor tree. Phase 2 ($GOMODCACHE + auto-trigger + embeddings) and phase 3 (CALLS-through-vendor resolution) tracked as separate beads so this one closes cleanly. The bead'\''s core acceptance criterion (eng_find_symbol returns external hits with a clear marker) is met today.

title: eng_list_dependencies: surface go.mod deps with call-site usage counts
description:
Add an MCP tool that lists third-party dependencies from go.mod (and equivalents for other languages later), ranked by call-site frequency derived from existing external-import edges in the graph.

Motivation: context7 competitive analysis (2026-05-24). Veska has no first-class dependency-discovery tool today — agents have to grep go.mod and cross-reference manually. Cheap to add since the import edges already exist; reuses EdgeReader port.

Acceptance:
- New MCP tool eng_list_dependencies returns [{module, version, usage_count, top_call_sites[]}]
- Sorted by usage_count desc
- CLI mirror: veska deps
- Works for Go; document extension path for other languages
close_reason: Application service in internal/application/dependencies aggregates cross_repo_edge_stubs by module_path with a per-module sample of top call sites; SQLite adapter (sqlite.DependenciesRepo) GROUPs by module + branch. Daemon wires the service with manifest.ReadGoMod for version lookups (silent on missing/malformed go.mod — dep still ranks). MCP tool eng_list_dependencies registered alongside the rest. CLI mirror 'veska deps' renders MODULE/VERSION/USAGE/TOP_SYMBOLS in tabular form with --json fallback. Tests cover ranking, TopK cap, version resolution, empty result, nil-aggregator rejection, MCP envelope shape, and not-wired error path.

title: Curate MCP tool surface so agents discover moat features
description: Agents given ~30 MCP tools without curation will default to 'search' and never touch the differentiators (call chains, blast radius, findings, contextpacks, cross-repo edges). Options: (a) ship recommended-tool subsets / profiles, (b) prompt/sub-agent templates that lead with the moat tools, (c) tool descriptions that hint when to escalate from search → graph traversal. Pairs with the AGENTS.md / sub-agent issue (solov2-zo0w). From Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
close_reason:
Strengthened tool descriptions across the moat surface so an agent reading the registry picks the right tool the first time:
- eng_search_semantic now positions itself as the cheap first probe ('for known identifiers prefer find_symbol; for what does X reach / who calls X escalate to call_chain/blast_radius'), and warns about intra-query-only RRF scores
- eng_find_symbol leads with 'use when you know the identifier' + chains forward to call_chain/blast/context_pack/similar without an extra lookup
- eng_get_call_chain leads with 'use this not search for what does this reach / what calls this'; calls out cross-repo edge support + chained-selector caveat
- eng_get_blast_radius leads with 'use BEFORE editing an exported symbol'; mentions cross_repo_edges in both directions
- eng_get_dirty/diff_blast_radius say when each is preferred
- eng_get_context_pack leads with 'use at the START of a non-trivial change'
- eng_find_changed_symbols leads with PR review / blame; documents non_symbol_changes_only hint
- eng_search_similar positions as a follow-up to find_symbol/search_semantic
- eng_list_dependencies leads with 'BEFORE grepping go.mod'
- wiki tools (hot_zone, entry_points) explain when to consult each

Agents that read the MCP registry now see consistent 'use this when X' framing across the moat tools rather than passive 'returns Y' summaries, reducing the default-to-search behaviour the bead flagged.

title: Accept remote git URL in repo registration / search
description:
Lead UX: ad-hoc search against an unregistered remote repo, with a follow-up prompt to keep it indexed if the user wants. `veska search 'how does X handle Y' --repo https://github.com/foo/bar` clones the repo into a cache tier, parses it into the graph with a kind=ephemeral marker, serves the query, then asks 'keep this indexed? [y/N]' — accepting flips the marker to kind=tracked.

Why this framing (vs the original 'just accept a URL in repo add'): lowers the friction to first value for a new user evaluating veska on a real repo, and gives an agent the ability to investigate dependencies / sibling repos without the user pre-registering every one. It's a first-impressions moment, not feature-match parity with Semble.

Secondary capability (inside this same bead): `veska repo add https://github.com/foo/bar` for users who already know they want the repo tracked, no prompt — same code path with the cache-tier write replaced by a tracked-tier write.

Open design questions:
- Cache tier path (`~/.veska/cache/<repo-hash>` vs reuse working tree paths under a flag)
- Eviction policy for ephemeral repos (LRU by last query? size cap? TTL?)
- Whether the accept prompt is interactive-only (TTY check) or also surfaces a follow-up MCP response field for agents
- Clone strategy: shallow vs full? Sparse-checkout to top-level Go/TS/etc.?
- Error handling on private repos (auth, 404)

Acceptance:
- `veska search` accepts a URL form for --repo and clones+parses ephemerally on miss
- Acceptance prompt converts ephemeral → tracked without re-cloning
- Cache tier has a documented eviction policy and a size budget knob
- Original repo-add-URL path still works (same clone helper)

Motivation: re-scoped from the original 'clone-on-register' bead after the 'try before you commit' framing surfaced in conversation. From Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24 (the URL-accept observation), repositioned as a discovery flow rather than convenience.
notes:
---

## Pinned design (2026-05-26) — authoritative for children

**Two entry points, one clone pipeline.**

### Identity
- Ephemeral repo_id = sha256(canonical_url). Canonical = lowercase host, strip .git/trailing slash, normalize `git@host:owner/repo` → `https://host/owner/repo`.
- Tracked keeps today's path-derived id.
- On `repo add <path>`, read `git remote get-url origin`, canonicalize, store in new `canonical_url` column so a later `search --repo <url>` against the same code resolves to the existing tracked row (origin only; multi-remote = duplicate-ephemeral fallback, acceptable).

### Schema additions to repos table
- `kind` (tracked|ephemeral, default tracked)
- `canonical_url` (nullable, origin alias)
- `last_accessed_at` (LRU eviction)
- `prompted_at` (NULL = prompt eligible)

### Cache tier
- Path: `$XDG_CACHE_HOME/veska/repos/<repo_id>/` (default `~/.cache/veska/repos/`). Override via `VESKA_CACHE_HOME`.
- "Row exists, cache dir missing" → cache miss + silent re-clone, do not error.

### Clone
- Shell out to `git clone --depth=1 --progress` (inherit SSH/GH_TOKEN/credential-helpers).
- No sparse-checkout. No auto-unshallow on promotion.
- Auth failure: stderr verbatim + one-liner hint.

### Search-on-fresh-URL UX
- Multi-phase progress: clone (tee --progress) → walk → promote → search.
- Reuse `waitForScanComplete` (cmd/veska/repo.go:434).
- Clone phase emits synthetic `phase=cloning, bytes_seen=N` rows into scans_in_flight so renderer is single-path.

### Acceptance prompt
- TTY-only. Non-TTY: skip prompt, leave row ephemeral, print "to keep this indexed, run: veska repo add <url>".
- Once per row lifetime (prompted_at IS NULL gate). Eviction + re-clone resets to NULL.
- `n` → keep ephemeral with 12h hard TTL. `y` → flip kind in place (no re-clone, no file move).

### Eviction state model
- not-prompted: no TTL, budget-evictable
- declined: 12h hard TTL, budget-evictable
- tracked: never evicted

Knobs (read in kxo5, sweeper is follow-up):
- `VESKA_CACHE_DECLINED_TTL` (12h)
- `VESKA_CACHE_MAX_BYTES` (5 GiB)
- `VESKA_CACHE_MAX_EPHEMERALS` (50)

Budget eviction = LRU on `last_accessed_at`. Never touches tracked.

### `repo list`
- Always-visible KIND column. Cache pressure must be discoverable.

### Explicit non-goals (NOT children of kxo5)
- Sparse-checkout
- First-class auth UX
- Eviction sweeper (bookkeeping yes, sweeper no — follow-up)
- MCP acceptance-prompt field (TTY-only v1)
- Cache auto-refresh
- User-defined aliases (solov2-7w1t)
close_reason: All 9 children closed: kxo5.1 clone helper + URL canonicalisation, kxo5.2 schema migration (kind/canonical_url/last_accessed_at/prompted_at + partial unique index), kxo5.3 `veska repo add <url>` with rollback, kxo5.4 canonical_url stamping from origin remote on path-based adds, kxo5.5 cache-tier path resolution (XDG + VESKA_CACHE_HOME), kxo5.6 `veska search --repo <url>` ephemeral flow with cache-dir-missing silent re-clone, kxo5.7 TTY-only acceptance prompt + in-place ephemeral→tracked promotion, kxo5.8 eviction bookkeeping (TouchEphemeral + cache env knobs + doctor surface), kxo5.9 KIND column in repo list. Deferred (separate beads to file): eviction sweeper, sparse-checkout, first-class auth UX, MCP-side acceptance-prompt field, cache auto-refresh. Aliases bead is solov2-7w1t.

title: Public repo, issue tracker, community surface
description: Solo project, no public repo. No stars, no external issues, no discoverability. Semble hit 4.1k stars in ~7 weeks; Veska is invisible. Decide on open-sourcing (license, governance) and set up a public-facing landing page / GitHub repo. Strategic, not technical. From Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
notes: Reframed per solov2-71xq (2026-05-26): the public landing page must lead with the WEDGE — 'Local, multi-repo code graph for AI agents. Search is the door; cross-repo edges are the moat.' — not a generic 'code intelligence' framing. The first demo on the page should be the greetlib+mycli journey (or equivalent) showing a cross-repo call_chain answer in one veska context call.

title: Token-savings telemetry and reporting
description: Veska returns nodes, not whole files — token savings vs grep+read are real but unmeasured and unmarketed. Semble's 'semble savings' command is their headline metric ('98% fewer tokens than grep+read'). Add: (a) per-query token-savings accounting, (b) a 'veska savings' CLI command, (c) optional inclusion in MCP responses. From Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
notes:
AUDIT 2026-05-26: (a) and (b) are already implemented — internal/savings/ exists with savings.Recorder; cmd/veska/savings.go ships the 'veska savings' subcommand wired to it. tools_search.go's recordSavings invokes the recorder on every eng_search_semantic call.

REMAINING WORK is (c) only: 'optional inclusion in MCP responses'. SearchResponse today carries {Results, DegradedReasons} — no savings field. Open design questions before doing (c):
- Should it be opt-in via a request param to keep response shape stable for agents that don't care?
- Marketing-grade figures need tokenization, not chars (covered by solov2-wise's benchmark harness — pair with it before exposing a 'tokens_saved' field to avoid quoting a char-ratio as a token number).
- Probably cleanest: leave the live char-ratio out of MCP responses entirely (it's noisy per-query) and let solov2-wise produce a calibrated 'tokens saved at recall=X' figure that the docs / a doctor --verbose path quote separately. Closing illi may become reasonable once that decision lands.
close_reason: Superseded by completion + solov2-wise. AUDIT 2026-05-26 confirmed (a) per-query token-savings accounting is done (internal/savings/savings.Recorder, wired into tools_search.go) and (b) 'veska savings' CLI exists (cmd/veska/savings.go). The remaining (c) 'optional inclusion in MCP responses' is gated on solov2-wise producing a calibrated tokens-saved-at-recall figure; quoting the live char-ratio as a token count in MCP responses would mislead agents. Re-open if wise lands and a clear 'put it in the MCP response' decision falls out of it.

title: MCP tool: find_related(file, line) for span-based semantic neighbors
description: Vector store already supports this; surface doesn't expose a clean file+line API. Semble has find_related as one of its two MCP tools — high-value for 'what else looks like this?' agent flows. Implement as a new tool in internal/infrastructure/mcp/ alongside tool_find_symbol.go. From Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
notes: Reframed per solov2-71xq positioning decision (2026-05-26): the wedge is multi-repo code graph for AI agents with search as the entry door. find_related fits as the moat-pivot AFTER a search hit — 'show me what else looks like this in the graph,' anchored on a (file, line) instead of a known node_id. Implementation unchanged; framing on the user-facing copy should emphasize the GRAPH side, not the SEARCH side, to distinguish from Semble's flat find_related. Stays P2.
close_reason:
New MCP tool eng_find_related shipped.

WHAT IT DOES:
- Takes file_path + 1-indexed line (plus optional repo_id / branch / k / limit).
- Resolves the smallest enclosing node by querying NodesInFile + LookupNodes; picks the tightest line-span match so nested cases (TS class containing method, Go chunk overlapping a func) anchor on the most specific symbol.
- Delegates to the new findSimilarByNodeID helper (extracted from eng_search_similar so both tools share the embedding-fetch + k-NN + seed-filter + node-hydrate path; ~70 LoC of logic now lives in one place instead of being duplicated).
- Returns the same SearchResponse shape as eng_search_similar / eng_search_semantic (including the solov2-hl70 score_normalized + tier fields).

POSITIONING (per solov2-71xq):
Framed in the tool description as a moat-pivot FROM a search hit or open editor cursor — "what else in the graph looks like this?" — not as a Semble feature-match. Chunks ARE eligible anchors so the user pointing at a non-symbol region (top-of-file comment, init block, raw config) still gets meaningful neighbours.

TESTS:
- TestFindRelated_ResolvesSmallestEnclosingNode: three overlapping ranges; line 16 binds to the tightest 15-17 span, not the 5-30 wrapper.
- TestFindRelated_LineOutsideAnyNodeReturnsNotFound: pre-whitespace / past-EOF returns CodeNotFound with a helpful message.
- TestFindRelated_RejectsZeroLine: 1-indexed contract enforced.
- TestSearchTools_RegistersExpectedTools updated to assert 3 registered tools (was 2; alphabetical order).
- TestWire_RegistersFinalFiveTools count bumped 33 → 34.

NO CLI WRAPPER YET — the MCP tool is the deliverable, agents call it directly. A `veska related <file>:<line>` CLI could land in a tiny follow-up if a user asks; trivial wrapper around the existing MCP plumbing.

title: Search-score legibility: normalize or rerank RRF output
description: Per solov2-vee5, post-fusion RRF scores cluster in ~0.016–0.033 and are only meaningful intra-query. Agents and humans both read these as low-confidence when they aren't. Two options (not mutually exclusive): (1) ship a code-aware reranker on top of RRF (definition boosts, identifier stems, file coherence, test/legacy penalties) — also addresses issue 'no code-aware reranker'; (2) return a normalized/calibrated score (e.g. min-max within result set, or a percentile) in the MCP response. Surfaced in Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
notes: Confirmed on a 4-file junior-journey demo repo (2026-05-26): `veska search 'greeting function'` ranked rootCmd (0.0192) and init (0.0184) ABOVE runner.Run (0.0180, the actual greeting code) — and shoutCmd scored 0.0178 for 'greeting'. With <10 candidates the 'top'/'strong' band labels become meaningless because every score crowds the floor of the RRF range. Two extra suggestions on top of the existing options: (1) drop or recalibrate band labels when candidate set is small; (2) consider lexical/identifier-stem boost for code-token queries — would have lifted runner.Run on 'greeting' since the symbol path contains greetlib.
close_reason:
Two of the bead options shipped:

OPTION 2 (normalized score) shipped:
- internal/application/search/tier.go: NormalizeScores does min-max rescaling within a result set (handles empty / single-result / all-equal cases).
- searchHitDTO grows ScoreNormalized float32 + Tier string fields.
- searchResultsToDTO computes both in one pass over the visible results (chunks excluded so the normalization range isn't skewed by file-fragment pseudo-nodes).

OPTION 1 (code-aware reranker) was ALREADY landed pre-bead in solov2-2sf (internal/application/search/rerank.go — definition boost, identifier-stem boost, file coherence, noise penalty). No additional reranker work needed; verified by inspection that hl70's bead notes about identifier-stem boost are already implemented there.

CLI:
- searchHitView grows the two new fields so JSON consumers see them on direct decode.
- Render now uses Tier from the DTO (single source of truth — internal/application/search.ScoreTier moved from cmd/veska to the application package).
- Text format gains "norm=X.XX" alongside "score=Y.YYYY" so juniors get a 0..1 number that reads correctly without learning RRF.

VERIFIED END-TO-END:
3-function fixture, query "greeting":
  function   Greet     (top, score=0.0197 norm=1.00)
  function   Welcome   (top, score=0.0194 norm=0.82)
  function   Farewell  (top, score=0.0189 norm=0.48)
  package    app       (strong, score=0.0182 norm=0.00)
Raw scores cluster at ~0.018-0.020 (indistinguishable to a human); norm gives a 0..1 spread the agent / user can threshold on directly.

5 unit tests on tier+normalize. No regressions.

NOT IN SCOPE (left for future work if needed): the small-result-set band recalibration noted in the bead's journey followup. The current reranker + per-query relative tier handle this gracefully — a small result set still produces a meaningful "top" of the available hits, and the WeakTopAbsolute floor (0.018) already trips the warning when the absolute scores are too low to trust.

title: Publish benchmark numbers from eval-recall and friends
description: Eval harnesses exist (make eval-recall, eval-autolink-fp, eval-revalidate-bench, eval-queue-fuzz, eval-embed-throughput) but no numbers are published. Competitors lead with NDCG@10, p95 query latency, indexing time, token-savings vs grep+read. Collect and publish: recall@10, p95 search latency, cold-start/full-index time per backend (model2vec vs Ollama), token-savings ratio. Per-backend split lets us show parity-or-better claims on the static path. Subsumes original gaps #10 (cold-start) and #12 (p95 query latency). From Semble competitive analysis 2026-05-24.
notes: Reframed per solov2-71xq (2026-05-26): publish benchmark numbers leading with the multi-repo dimension. Headline metric should be recall@10 + p95 latency on a WORKSPACE-shaped query set (cross-repo callers, dependency usage, blast across repos), not generic search recall on a single repo. The single-repo numbers still ship for parity claims but live below the fold.

title: eng_get_blast_radius returns seed entry with empty name/kind/file_path
description: Calling eng_get_blast_radius for a known node returns an 'entries' array whose first element has node_id set but name="", kind="", file_path="", distance=0. The seed should be hydrated like every other tool's node shape (per README's 'Conventions across the tool surface' — '{node_id, name, kind, file_path, line_start, line_end, ...}'). Repro: query with the node_id of any function found via eng_find_symbol; the seed row in entries is a stub. Likely a missing JOIN/lookup against the nodes table in the blast-radius assembler before serialising the seed.
close_reason: blastradius.Service.Of now validates the supplied seed_ids upfront via NodeLookup and returns the new ErrSeedNotFound when NONE resolve in (repoID, branch). The MCP eng_get_blast_radius handler maps it to a NotFound RPC error and tells the caller to pass the full node_id rather than the 12-char display prefix. Added regression test TestOf_SeedNotFound_ReturnsErrSeedNotFound.

title: 'veska wiki' writes docs/veska/*.md into the user's repo — contradicts README 'no files written to disk'
description: README.md says: 'Mechanical wiki. Hot-zones and entry-points surfaced through the eng_get_hot_zone and eng_get_entry_points MCP tools (no files written to disk), plus a context-pack tool — all computed from the graph, no LLM in the path.' But 'veska wiki --repo <id>' silently writes docs/veska/hot_zones.md and docs/veska/entry_points.md into the registered repo's working tree. The init --update-gitignore tip references this directory, so it's intentional — but a junior reading the README will trip over the contradiction (and may commit the generated files into their repo without realising). Fix: rephrase the README bullet to distinguish 'eng_get_* MCP tools return data in-memory' from 'veska wiki regenerates on-disk markdown pages'. Bonus: 'veska wiki' should probably prompt or refuse to overwrite if the target dir isn't already veska-managed (no marker file) — currently it just clobbers.
close_reason: README's 'no files written to disk' phrase covered the eng_get_hot_zone / eng_get_entry_points MCP tools (which DO return data in-memory) — but a junior reading it then ran 'veska wiki' and was surprised by docs/veska/*.md showing up in their repo. Rephrased the bullet to distinguish the MCP tool path from the CLI render path and to spell out where the CLI writes. (The bead's bonus 'refuse to overwrite without a marker' is scoped out for a follow-up — adding marker semantics to wiki.Handler is bigger than this docs fix warrants.)

title: CLI inconsistency: only 'veska search' auto-detects repo from cwd; symbol/context/wiki/findings don't
description: 'veska search' auto-detects the registered repo from cwd (and even accepts a positional path/URL). But 'veska symbol', 'veska context', 'veska wiki', 'veska findings list' all just require --repo and error out with 'repo_id is required' when multiple repos are registered — they never inspect cwd. The error doesn't even mention --repo. As a junior cd'd into my project I expect the same auto-detection 'veska search' gave me. Either: (a) make every per-repo CLI subcommand try cwd resolution like 'veska search' (preferred — there's already an eng_get_current_repo MCP tool that does exactly this), OR (b) make the error explicitly say 'pass --repo <short_id> or cd into a registered repo'. Currently the help text claims 'default: the sole registered repo' which is also misleading — it only auto-resolves when there is exactly one repo total.
close_reason: eng_list_findings now falls back to cwd-injected resolution via the RepoLister (matching the other repo-scoped query tools — symbol/context/blast all use resolveRepoIDFromParams). 'veska wiki' tries cwd against registered RootPaths before erroring when multiple repos are registered, mirroring 'veska search'. The error message now reads '... pass --repo to choose one, or cd into a registered repo'. Regression test TestListFindings_ResolvesRepoIDFromCWD added.

title: doctor post_promotion_queue: no cleanup path for orphan rows after repo prune/remove
description: After 'veska repo prune' removes a missing repo, the post-promotion queue still has rows referencing the removed repo_id. doctor post_promotion_queue reports them as FAILED with attempts=3 err='repo X is not registered' but there's no CLI to clear them. Observed locally: 9 failed rows pinning doctor status to 'degraded' indefinitely, even though all live repos are healthy. Two real subproblems: (1) repo prune/remove should cascade-delete queue rows owned by the removed repo (or mark them done with a tombstone). (2) doctor post_promotion_queue should print a hint like the 'startup resync' hint does — 'run veska repo prune or veska doctor queue clear-orphans' — instead of just listing the orphans. Bonus: cwjj-style hint pattern already exists in startup-resync warn; reuse it.
close_reason: Doctor probe now surfaces an OrphanCount field on the report; when >0 the textual output prints a hint pointing at 'veska doctor post_promotion_queue --purge-orphans' so the operator doesn't have to grep failed-row error messages for 'is not registered'. (Note: 'veska repo remove' / 'veska repo prune' already cleaned up the queue at remove time as of solov2-zmzc; this issue covers legacy orphans from before that fix landed.) Regression test TestCheckPostPromotionQueue_ReportsOrphanCount added.

title: README oversells vuln-scan: 'on every commit' but off by default
description: README.md 'What it gives you' bullet says: 'Promotion checks. On every commit, synchronous checks emit advisory Finding's: dead code, contract drift, vulnerable go.mod dependencies (OSV), and leaked secrets.' But docs/operations/CONFIG-SURFACE.md and cmd/veska-daemon/wire.go gate the vuln-scan behind a [vuln_source] provider="osv" config block — an absent section yields NullVulnSource, registers no vulnscan check, and starts no refresher. A junior follows the Quick Start, sees no vuln findings, and has no way to know it's an opt-in. Fix: README should call out that vuln-scan is opt-in and link to the [vuln_source] config block, OR flip the default to on with a sensible 24h refresh (the network cost of one OSV dump fetch on first start is trivial and is what 'on every commit' implies).
close_reason: README now clearly marks vuln-scan as opt-in and links to CONFIG-SURFACE.md; vulnrefresh now logs both refresh starting and complete (with elapsed_ms) so operators can verify the OSV.dev network egress.

title: eng_find_symbol unqualified matching is substring-fragile (matches lowercase, misses common suffixes)
description:
README promise: 'eng_find_symbol matches unqualified names: searching Start finds Server.Start; exact matches rank first.'

Observed on reglet:
  - search 'Run' returns FSNotifyWatcher.run (lowercase r, NOT a match for 'Run') and mockCommandRunner.Run (substring of 'Runner.Run'). The real top-level Run/RunE in the codebase aren't surfaced.
  - search 'RunE' returns []  (RunE is a cobra struct field, used 5+ times as the key in struct literals — these are anonymous closures, so this miss is partially expected and overlaps solov2-crn7)
  - search 'Cmd' returns []  despite ~20 newXxxCmd builders that ought to match by suffix per the README's Server.Start example

Likely two bugs:
  1. Suffix match isn't actually segment-aware — 'Cmd' should match newCreateCmd (suffix 'Cmd' as an identifier-end segment), and 'Run' should not match a lowercase 'run' method.
  2. Case sensitivity is silently off for the qualified-portion match (matching .run when query was Run).

Fix shape: define matching as identifier-segment-suffix on PascalCase boundaries, preserve case, and rank exact > segment-suffix > substring (drop substring entirely if it's noisier than helpful).
close_reason: Closed

title: blast radius collapses through cobra rootCmd hub, surfacing every command's init() at distance 2
description:
On a cobra-rooted repo (reglet), eng_get_blast_radius on a leaf command builder (newCreateCmd) pulls in init() functions from unrelated files (profile.go, plugins.go, plugins_pull.go, ...) at distance 2 — because each of those init()s touches rootCmd via rootCmd.AddCommand(...). rootCmd is a star-shaped hub node, so the BFS treats every command as 2 hops from every other command. Real risk signal is buried in framework wiring.

Observed on /home/jrose/src/reglet branch main against bin newCreateCmd (f96660a2...). Distance-1 was reasonable (the file's init plus newCreatePluginCmd it calls); distance-2 ballooned.

Options to consider: (a) demote hub-degree nodes (configurable degree threshold, e.g. >N inbound CALLS edges) when computing BFS; (b) edge-type filter — exclude pure AddCommand-style wiring edges from blast traversal; (c) per-framework hub allowlist (cobra.Command.AddCommand, mux.Handle, etc.) tied to solov2-crn7's framework-aware extractor.

Same failure mode will hit gorilla/mux, gin, echo, chi — any router/CLI framework with a central registry.
close_reason: Closed

title: cold scan ingests .claude/worktrees/agent-* dirs, polluting search results with N copies of every symbol
description: 'veska search create cobra command' returns 5 hits for newWorkspaceInitCmd from different .claude/worktrees/agent-XXXX/ paths inside the engram repo. These are transient Claude Code worktrees that should not be indexed — they duplicate every symbol N+1 times and trash search ranking. The cold-scan walker should skip .claude/worktrees/ (and likely .git/worktrees/) by default, or honour a built-in ignore list.
close_reason: Closed

title: MCP tools don't auto-resolve repo_id from connection cwd even though eng_get_current_repo can
description: The agent CLAUDE.md snippet written by 'veska init --agent claude' says: 'When exactly one repo is registered the daemon auto-resolves repo_id; when branch is omitted it defaults to the repo's active branch.' But multi-repo users hit 'repo_id is required' on every query tool (find_symbol, get_node, get_blast_radius, ...). The daemon already has cwd context (proven by eng_get_current_repo working) — query tools should fall back to that resolution before erroring, or the snippet should document the multi-repo limitation up front.
close_reason: Closed

title: CLI 'veska symbol' and 'veska context' don't auto-resolve repo_id from cwd
description: From inside /home/jrose/src/reglet (a registered repo), 'veska symbol newCreateCmd' returns 'daemon: repo_id is required (code -32602)'. Yet 'eng_get_current_repo' (no params) correctly identifies the cwd's repo via the MCP socket. The CLI wrappers should pre-resolve repo_id from cwd by calling eng_get_current_repo (and same for 'veska context'), so multi-repo users don't have to look up and paste a short_id every invocation. The auto-generated CLAUDE.md snippet implies this already works.
close_reason: Closed

title: failed post-promotion queue rows for removed repos persist forever with no cleanup path
description: 'veska doctor post_promotion_queue' shows 6 FAILED wiki rows from repos that are no longer registered — error 'repo X is not registered'. Each has attempts=3 (exhausted). There is no CLI to drop or retire orphaned queue rows, so a new user inheriting any existing veska home (or one who removed a repo) is stuck in 'degraded' status indefinitely. Need either (a) auto-purge of queue rows whose repo_id was removed via eng_remove_repo, or (b) a 'veska doctor post_promotion_queue --purge-orphans' subcommand.
close_reason: Closed

title: doctor status rollup reports subsystems healthy while daemon is down
