description: As a first-time user I ran 'veska doctor status' before starting the daemon. Output: 'status: broken (embedder=healthy, egress=broken, config=healthy, ingestion=healthy, queue=degraded)'. The rollup labels embedder/config/ingestion 'healthy' even though there's no daemon process to host them — those checks should report 'unknown' or 'unreachable' when the socket is absent, otherwise the rollup is misleading and contradictory at first glance.
close_reason: Closed

title: README: 'veska init' vs 'veska service install' decision guide missing
description:
Two install paths shown without a decision rubric. Add a 2-sentence pick-for-me block: laptop dev work -> service install (auto-restart, logs under ~/.veska/logs, survives reboot); ephemeral try -> background &.

Also call out the uninstall path.
acceptance_criteria: 1) README has a clear pick-for-me paragraph next to the daemon-start commands.
close_reason: Closed

title: Parked task tools (eng_get_active_task etc) inconsistent across docs
description: README mentions eng_get_active_task / eng_set_active_task / eng_get_task_history in the family table marked '**Not yet wired**' but elsewhere reads as if they were live. They are not registered on the socket and return 'method not found'. Either: (a) remove from public docs entirely until wired, or (b) keep mention but ensure every reference is flagged. Currently confusing to new readers.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Single coherent treatment of the parked task family across README, agent snippets, and docs/*. 2) The agent init snippet does not advertise them.
close_reason: Closed

title: README missing '60-second hello world' walkthrough with real tool output
description: README jumps from install straight to a 31-tool MCP table. A new user has no idea which 3-4 tools to try first. Add a short section: register one repo, wait for cold-scan-complete, run eng_find_symbol + eng_get_context_pack via veska-mcp, show sample output. Should fit on one screen.
acceptance_criteria: 1) README has a 'First call' section between Quick start and MCP tools. 2) Two concrete printf|veska-mcp examples with expected output.
close_reason: Closed

title: Stale 'missing' repos accumulate; need 'veska repo prune'
description: Repos whose root directory is gone show as (missing) in repo list and the daemon logs ERROR for each on every boot ('startup resync: HEAD for repo ... No such file or directory'). Add 'veska repo prune' (drop all missing entries; --dry-run flag) and have daemon log a single WARN per missing repo at boot instead of ERROR per boot.
acceptance_criteria: 1) 'veska repo prune --dry-run' lists missing repos. 2) Without --dry-run, removes them and uninstalls hooks if reachable. 3) Daemon downgrades repeated missing-root errors to WARN.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska search: unknown cwd repo should suggest 'veska repo add .'
description: Today 'veska search' against a path that isn't registered returns empty results silently. A natural junior expectation is to run it inside a project they're working on. cmd/veska/search.go should detect 'cwd resolves to a path not in repo list' and emit: 'no registered repo found for /current/path; run veska repo add . to index it'.
acceptance_criteria: 1) When cwd has no registered repo, error/suggestion is printed (exit 1). 2) Test covers it.
close_reason: Already implemented in cmd/veska/search.go resolveSearchTarget: empty target with non-git cwd emits 'cwd %q is not a git repository; pass <path> or cd to a registered repo'; empty target with git cwd not in repo list emits '%q is not registered; run veska repo add %s or pass it as <path>'. Verified live from /tmp and a fresh git init under /tmp/jrtest-search. My original finding was based on passing an explicit registered path, which bypassed this branch.

title: Wiki hot_zones empty state reads as 'broken feature' on fresh install
description:
On a fresh repo, docs/veska/hot_zones.md renders: 'No hot zones: no files changed in the look-back window.' A junior assumes the feature is broken or mis-installed. Replace with a friendlier empty state: 'Hot zones populate after commits land while veska is registered. Re-run veska wiki once you have a few commits on this branch.'

Tweak application/wiki render text.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Empty hot_zones page has the clearer message. 2) Same treatment for entry_points if/when empty.
close_reason: Closed

title: MCP pytest suite: fix outdated sad-path + changed-symbols cases
description:
Several cases in tests/mcp/test_sad_paths.py and test_changed_symbols.py assert error messages that no longer fire after recent UX fixes (5vu1 branch defaulting, npjs ref defaults, lcz6 call_chain symbol parity, v4ob get_node simplification).

Concrete breakage (post-session 2026-05-23):

test_sad_paths.py MISSING_REQUIRED_CASES — these now error with 'unknown repo_id: x' (resolveRepoID runs first; the param they were testing as 'required' is now optional or auto-resolved):
  - line 35: eng_search_semantic, {repo_id:'x', query:'y'} — expected 'branch'
  - line 40: eng_get_context_pack, {repo_id:'x'} — expected 'required'
  - line 42: eng_find_todos, {repo_id:'x'} — expected 'required'
  - line 43: eng_find_owner, {repo_id:'x'} — expected 'required'
  - line 51: eng_get_hot_zone, {repo_id:'x'} — expected 'required'
  - line 52: eng_get_entry_points, {repo_id:'x'} — expected 'required'

test_changed_symbols.py:
  - test_changed_symbols_requires_refs expects an error when both refs are omitted, but solov2-npjs (already closed) made refs default to HEAD~1..HEAD. Replace with a 'defaults to last commit' assertion.

Fix approach: replace bogus repo_id='x' with the real fixture (so the test exercises the genuine missing-arg path) — that's faithful to the test's intent. For test_changed_symbols, flip the assertion: call with no refs, assert ok=True (defaults applied).

Out of scope: positive coverage for new behaviours — covered by separate bead.
close_reason: Closed

title: `veska init --agent claude` silently writes/modifies .gitignore in the user's repo
description: Running `veska init --agent claude` in a user repo creates (or appends to) .gitignore with veska-managed markers and 'docs/veska/'. Output mentions it as one line: 'veska: wrote .gitignore at <path>'. A junior expects --agent to only write the instruction file — surprising side effect on a tracked file. Make it opt-in via a flag (e.g. --update-gitignore) or prompt before writing. At minimum, surface a clearer confirmation.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska wiki --repo accepts only full sha; rejects short_id from `repo list`
description: `veska repo list` prints 12-char short_id (e.g. 6754f6c9957f). Passing that exact value to `veska wiki --repo 6754f6c9957f` returns 'wiki: repo "6754f6c9957f" is not registered'. Must use the full 64-char sha. CLI/MCP inconsistency — MCP tools accept the 12-char short_id (when exact). Make the CLI use the same resolver.
close_reason: Closed

title: file_path inconsistency: absolute paths from find_symbol, repo-relative from find_changed_symbols
description: eng_find_symbol/eng_get_file_nodes return '/tmp/myrepo1/main.go'; eng_find_changed_symbols returns 'main.go'. Agents that dedupe by file_path silently break across tools. Pick one (relative is portable across machines and matches what users type in editors) and document it; add a regression test that asserts the convention for every tool.
close_reason: Closed

title: JSON contract violation: eng_find_changed_symbols returns null instead of [] for empty removed/modified
description: README guarantees: 'Empty result collections serialize as [], never omitted.' Observed: eng_find_changed_symbols returns {"added":[...],"removed":null,"modified":null}. Breaks downstream agents that assume the contract. Audit all tools for this; fix the JSON marshaling to use empty slices instead of nil.
close_reason: Closed

title: doctor status reports 'broken' before daemon start with no actionable hint
description: Fresh `veska init` followed by `veska doctor status` (per the README quick-start ordering) prints: 'status: broken (embedder=healthy, egress=broken, config=degraded, ingestion=healthy)' and exits. No mention that the fix is to start the daemon. doctor status should detect 'cli.sock + mcp.sock missing' as 'daemon never started' and print the next-step command (`veska-daemon &` or `veska service install && veska service start`) instead of a scary broken rollup.
close_reason: Closed

title: branch param required even when repo has a single active_branch
description: eng_find_symbol/eng_search_semantic/eng_get_file_nodes/eng_get_call_chain/eng_get_blast_radius/eng_get_context_pack/eng_find_changed_symbols/eng_find_todos/eng_find_owner all reject calls without 'branch'. With one registered branch this is pure friction. Default to the repo's active_branch when omitted; require only when ambiguous.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_get_call_chain misses calls made from anonymous-function bodies (cobra Run: pattern)
description:
Setup: a standard cobra CLI where the subcommand calls into a function via an anonymous Run handler:

  var serveCmd = &cobra.Command{ Run: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) { Serve() } }

`eng_get_call_chain` for node `Serve` returns `{nodes:[], edges:[]}`. But `eng_get_blast_radius` for the same node finds the package-level reach (distance:1 to package main). The CALLS edge from the anon func into `Serve` is not extracted by the tree-sitter pass.

This is the dominant call pattern in any cobra-based CLI, so call_chain is effectively blind on a huge class of Go projects. Fix the parser to follow CALLS edges out of nested function literals (and probably method-value/closure callbacks too).
close_reason: Closed

title: MCP tool responses inconsistent: file_path absolute vs relative, name vs symbol_name
description:
README claims a canonical node shape across all tools: `{node_id, name, kind, file_path, line_start, line_end, ...}`. Observed deviations on a fresh repo:

1. `eng_get_entry_points` uses `symbol_name` instead of `name` (also missing `node_id`).
2. `eng_get_hot_zone` returns relative paths (`main.go`) while `eng_find_symbol`, `eng_get_blast_radius`, `eng_get_file_nodes`, `eng_search_semantic` all return absolute paths (`/tmp/jr-veska/mycli/main.go`).

Fix: pick canonical forms (recommend: `name` + relative-to-repo `file_path` everywhere, with an explicit `repo_root` in the response envelope if absolute paths are still needed) and audit every tool's response schema against the contract documented in README §Conventions.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_find_changed_symbols leaks raw git stderr on single-commit repos
description:
On a freshly-init'd repo with one commit, calling `eng_find_changed_symbols` (which defaults to `HEAD~1..HEAD`) returns an internal error that leaks raw git output:

  -32603 find changed symbols: changedsymbols: list changed files: git diff HEAD~1..HEAD in /tmp/jr-veska/mycli: exit status 128: fatal: ambiguous argument 'HEAD~1': unknown revision or path not in the working tree.

This is a predictable case (single-commit repo, shallow clone, orphan branch). Should detect and either (a) return an empty result with `degraded_reasons: ['insufficient_history']`, or (b) return a typed `-32602 invalid params` error with a friendly message. Also generally: never leak raw `exit status 128` git stderr to MCP clients.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_get_diff_blast_radius rejects short_id that other tools accept
description: `eng_list_repos` returns 12-char `short_id`, and the README documents: 'anywhere a repo_id is required you may pass the full id or that short prefix'. This holds for most tools (`eng_find_symbol`, `eng_search_semantic`, `eng_get_blast_radius`, `eng_get_context_pack`, etc.) but breaks for `eng_get_diff_blast_radius`, which returns `-32002 repo not found: fe3e59848eeb` for the exact short_id those other tools just accepted in the same session. Likely the short-prefix resolver isn't applied on this code path. Audit all eng_* tools for consistent short_id handling and centralize the resolution.
close_reason: Closed

title: CLI errors print twice (cobra Error: prefix + manual stdout echo)
description:
Multiple commands print their error message twice. Examples:

`./bin/veska doctor status` on degraded state:
  status: degraded (embedder=healthy, ...)
  Error: status: degraded
  status: degraded

`./bin/veska search 'query'` outside a registered repo:
  Error: search: "..." is not registered; run `veska repo add ...` ...
  search: "..." is not registered; run `veska repo add ...` ...

Likely cause: command `RunE` returns the error AND prints it to stdout/stderr manually, while cobra prints it again with the `Error:` prefix. Fix: pick one path — either return the error and let cobra print, or print + return nil. Set `SilenceErrors`/`SilenceUsage` on RunE-returning commands as appropriate.
close_reason: Closed

title: auto-link finding fires on tiny/single-file repos using chunk:N-M as the message target
description:
Immediately after `veska repo add` on a 32-line single-file repo, an auto-link finding appears: 'Similar to chunk:1-22 in /tmp/jr-veska/mycli/main.go (score 0.68)'. Two problems:
1. The finding is noise on tiny repos — there is no meaningful similarity to flag, just self-similarity to the same file's own header chunk.
2. The message exposes the internal `chunk:1-22` artifact name to the user, which is meaningless without knowing veska internals.

Proposed: (a) skip auto-link when target/source share the same file_path, (b) suppress auto-link below some min repo size, (c) re-phrase finding message in terms of node name + file:line rather than internal chunk slugs.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_find_owner blame fallback returns null on freshly-committed files
description: On a repo with one author and recent commits, eng_find_owner returns owner=null with reason 'no CODEOWNERS file in repo root or .github/; git blame also yielded no author'. git log clearly shows the committer. Blame fallback appears broken — possibly trying to blame an uncommitted path, mis-resolving the repo root, or filtering authors too aggressively.
close_reason: Closed

title: chunk:* pseudo-nodes leak into MCP search results
description: eng_search_semantic and eng_search_similar return entries with name='chunk:1-26' / 'chunk:37-38' alongside real symbols. These are internal file-fragment nodes used for embeddings of free-floating code; they confuse users and agents who don't know what to do with a chunk hit. Options: filter from default results, rename to a clearer label (e.g. 'file fragment'), or add a flag to opt them in.
close_reason: Closed

title: call_chain returns empty for cobra/anonymous-func wrapped callees
description: In standard cobra CLI code (var greetCmd = &cobra.Command{ Run: func(...){ Greet(args[0]) } }), eng_get_call_chain for Greet returns {nodes:[], edges:[]} even though main -> anon -> Greet is obvious in source. Tree-sitter likely doesn't emit a CALLS edge through the anonymous function inside the var initializer. Either (a) extract calls from anon func bodies and attribute them to the enclosing var/decl, or (b) document the limitation prominently — this is the dominant Go-CLI pattern.
close_reason: Closed

title: stale repo entries never auto-prune; repo list shows promoted for missing roots
description: Repos whose root directory no longer exists on disk remain in 'veska repo list' with status=promoted forever. Add a doctor check for missing roots, or mark status=missing and surface in 'repo list'. Repro: veska repo add /tmp/foo; rm -rf /tmp/foo; veska repo list still shows it as promoted.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska repo add returns instantly with no scan progress signal
description: 'veska repo add' prints 'added repo XYZ (via daemon)' and exits while the cold scan runs in the background. The only way to know when indexing is done is to tail ~/.veska/logs/daemon.log. 'repo list' shows status=promoted immediately even when scans_in_flight is non-empty. Options: add --wait flag, surface a 'scanning' status, or print a tip pointing at the log.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska search in non-repo cwd silently auto-registers and errors
description: Running 'veska search QUERY' (no path-or-url) from a directory that isn't a git repo triggers a cold scan of cwd which then fails on rev-parse HEAD, leaving stack-y output and possibly a half-registered repo. Should detect non-repo cwd up front and either (a) print 'not in a registered repo; pass <path> or cd to one' or (b) default to searching across all registered repos. Repro: cd /tmp; veska search 'foo'.
close_reason: Closed

title: findings/suppressions ops require branch+repo_id even though finding_id is unique
description: eng_list_findings, eng_get_suppression, eng_suppress_finding, eng_close_finding all reject calls that pass only finding_id, demanding branch and repo_id. Since finding_id is globally unique and already encodes which repo/branch it belongs to, requiring the redundant params is friction and contradicts the spirit of README's 'short-id everywhere'. Either derive repo/branch from finding_id, or document the requirement loudly in the MCP tools table.
close_reason: Closed

title: TS parser discards all symbols on any syntax error (same as 7nkm, ts.go:60)
description: ts.go:60 bails out of symbol extraction entirely when hasErrorNode(root) is true — the TypeScript twin of solov2-7nkm (fixed for Go). Same impact: a transiently-broken TSX/TS file mid-edit erases all its symbols/edges from the graph until syntax is fixed, and the watcher re-parses precisely then. Apply the same recovery: record the ParseFailure but continue, skipping only error-containing top-level nodes in processTopLevelNode. Note TS extraction is recursive (export_statement unwrap), so the per-node error skip must apply at each processTopLevelNode entry.
acceptance_criteria: A TS file with one broken + one clean declaration yields the clean symbol plus a ParseFailure; test added; mirror the Go fix.
close_reason: TS parser already recorded the ParseFailure instead of bailing — what was missing was the per-declaration error-skip the Go fix added. processTopLevelNode now skips any subtree carrying a syntax error (applied at every recursion level so export_statement-wrapped nodes are guarded too); sibling declarations that parsed cleanly still index. Test added.

title: repo remove orphans nodes/edges/findings/stubs — FK CASCADE never fires (SQLite foreign_keys off)
description: Removing a repo deletes only the repos row; its nodes, edges, findings, and cross_repo_edge_stubs persist. The schema declares FOREIGN KEY(repo_id) REFERENCES repos(repo_id) ON DELETE CASCADE, but modernc SQLite has PRAGMA foreign_keys OFF by default per connection, so CASCADE never runs. Result: orphaned rows accumulate; re-adding a repo at the same path (deterministic repo_id) surfaces stale nodes (observed during solov2-xc51 verification: duplicate/stale 'main' nodes, find_symbol returning a stale node). Fix options: (a) enable 'PRAGMA foreign_keys=ON' on every pool connection (connection-string param or per-conn exec), or (b) make repo remove explicitly DELETE child rows (nodes/edges/findings/suppressions/cross_repo_edge_stubs/post_promotion_queue) in a transaction. Verify FK is enforced across ALL pools (read + write).
acceptance_criteria: After 'veska repo remove <id>', zero rows remain for that repo_id in nodes/edges/findings/cross_repo_edge_stubs; a regression test asserts cascade (or explicit delete) clears all child tables.
close_reason: Two root causes fixed: (1) openPool opened the raw path + one-shot PRAGMA, so foreign_keys was OFF on most pooled connections and ON DELETE CASCADE never fired — now encoded per-connection in the DSN (verified via pragma check + cascade test). (2) repo.Remove did an exact-match DELETE that silently no-op'd on a short id (the actual cause of the observed orphans) — now resolves exact-or-unique-prefix and errors loudly on unknown ids. Verified live: repo remove <short_id> deletes the repo and cascades all child rows (0 orphans). Tests added.

title: TS parser never sets exported flag (node_exported column half-populated)
description:
The node_exported schema column (migration 0010, commits 90e1a95/24e821a) and domain.WithExported are populated only by the Go parser (go.go:164,201,247). ts.go sets WithExported nowhere, so every TypeScript/TSX symbol lands with Exported=nil/false. This means any consumer of export status (dead-code checks, MCP DTOs surfacing 'exported') is silently wrong for all TS code.

TS export status is syntactic and easier than Go's casing heuristic: processTopLevelNode (ts.go:163) already detects the export_statement wrapper before recursing, then discards that signal. Default exports and 'export function/class/const' are all reachable from that branch.
design: Thread an 'exported bool' param through processTopLevelNode and the parseTS* helpers (parseTSFunctionDecl, parseTSClassDecl, parseTSInterfaceDecl, parseTSMethodDef, parseTSArrowFunctions). Set true in the export_statement case (ts.go:163) and pass it to the unwrapped inner declaration; default false otherwise. Class methods inherit nothing automatic — TS method visibility is via accessibility modifiers (public/private), out of scope here; treat class members as exported iff the class is exported, or leave false and note it. Acceptance: a .ts file with 'export function f(){}' and a non-exported 'function g(){}' yields Exported=true for f, false for g; add a case to ts_test.go.
close_reason: TS parser now threads an exported flag from export_statement unwrapping through all symbol constructors (function/class/interface/method/arrow); methods inherit class export status. node_exported is now correct for TS, not always false. Test added.

title: eng_list_findings file_path is inconsistent: absolute vs relative vs null across rules
description: In one eng_list_findings response: structural 'todo' finding has an ABSOLUTE file_path (/tmp/mycli/internal/server/server.go), 'secret_leak' findings have a RELATIVE path (internal/server/secrets.go), and 'auto-link' findings have file_path null. Three conventions in one tool's output; consumers can't rely on the shape.
acceptance_criteria: All findings emit file_path in one consistent convention (recommend repo-relative); document the convention.
close_reason: Findings now normalized to repo-relative file_path at the read boundary (list + get); todo/parse (ingester, absolute) and check findings (relative) unified; auto-link null preserved. Fixed a single-conn deadlock from the root lookup. Tests added; verified live.

title: Whitespace-only chunk nodes are embedded and returned as search results
description: semantic_search, search_similar, and 'veska search' return chunk nodes whose snippet is just "\n" (e.g. chunk:7-7, chunk:9-9). In search_similar a blank-line chunk scored 0.598, ranking ABOVE the real Execute function at 0.46. Empty/whitespace-only chunks should not be indexed or returned.
acceptance_criteria: Whitespace-only / empty chunk nodes are excluded from embedding+search results; real symbols are not outranked by blank chunks.
close_reason: chunkFile now skips whitespace-only windows; search no longer returns blank chunks. Test added; verified live after reindex.

title: eng_get_context_pack node DTO diverges from canonical shape (path vs file_path, unqualified name)
description: Latest commit claimed to 'unify all MCP tools on canonical DTO shapes', but context_pack nodes use key 'path' instead of 'file_path' and emit unqualified 'name' ("Start") where eng_find_symbol emits "Server.Start". Node keys observed: distance, has_open_finding, kind, name, node_id, path, seed, snippet.
acceptance_criteria: context_pack nodes use file_path and the same qualified name convention as the canonical node DTO.
close_reason: context_pack NodeInfo now uses file_path (was path) and qualified SymbolPath name (was leaf). Removed symbolLeaf; test flipped. Verified live.

title: Dead-code check inert: genuinely-unused unexported function not flagged
description: Committed an unexported, never-called func unusedHelper() in a fresh repo; eng_list_findings shows todo + secret_leak + auto-link findings but NO dead-code finding. The check (internal/application/checks/deadcode.go) flags nodes with no inbound edges, but every symbol has an inbound CONTAINS edge from its package and there are no CALLS edges in the graph (see call_chain issue), so the 'no inbound edges' signal is never true. Effectively the dead-code check cannot fire on real repos.
acceptance_criteria: Dead-code 'no inbound edges' is evaluated against CALLS/REFERENCES edges (not CONTAINS); unusedHelper-style unexported unused symbols produce a dead-code finding; depends on the CALLS-edge resolution issue.
notes:
INVESTIGATION (deferred — blocked on solov2-xc51):

The dead-code check (internal/application/checks/deadcode.go) flags candidate
nodes (function/method/type/struct/interface/class) that have NO inbound edges,
minus an external-entry allowlist (main/init/Test*/exported names). An unexported,
genuinely-unused func like unusedHelper() qualifies and SHOULD be flagged.

Root cause it stays silent: "no inbound edges" is evaluated against the edges
the graph actually has. For a typical multi-package repo the graph has only
CONTAINS (every symbol has an inbound CONTAINS from its package) and SIMILAR_TO
edges; cross-package CALLS edges are never produced (see solov2-xc51). So the
"deadness" signal is either never true (if CONTAINS counts as inbound) or
uniformly true (if only CALLS count) — either way the check can't distinguish
dead from live and produces nothing useful.

FIX DEPENDS ON solov2-xc51: once a real CALLS/REFERENCES graph exists, the
DeadCodeQuerier must count only call/reference inbound edges (NOT CONTAINS) so an
uncalled unexported symbol surfaces. Verify with an unexported unused function on
a cobra repo.
close_reason: Dead-code liveness now measured against inbound CALLS edges only (UPPER(kind)='CALLS'), not CONTAINS/SIMILAR_TO which every node has. Enabled by xc51's real call graph. Verified live: unusedHelper flagged, called funcs not. Tests added.

title: MCP tools should validate all required params at once and document them
description: Errors reveal one missing param at a time (e.g. eng_find_changed_symbols -> 'ref_a is required', then ref_b...). ref_a/ref_b aren't in the README tool table. Validate and report all missing/invalid params in one error, and document every tool's required params.
acceptance_criteria: A call missing multiple required params lists them all in one error; README documents required params for every tool.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_find_symbol should match short/unqualified names (or hint)
description: Searching 'Start' for the method Server.Start silently returns empty because find_symbol only matches the fully-qualified Name. A junior assumes it's broken. Add suffix/unqualified matching, or when no exact match, return candidates whose qualified name ends with the query.
acceptance_criteria: eng_find_symbol 'Start' returns Server.Start (or returns it as a suggestion) rather than an empty result.
close_reason: Closed

title: First-run discoverability & onboarding gaps for new users
description: A first-time-user journey on a fresh cobra-CLI repo surfaced onboarding friction that makes the tooling feel broken before it feels useful: silent symbol-match failures, one-param-at-a-time errors with undocumented params, unwieldy repo_ids, and an embedder quality cliff between the README quick-start and what 'init' actually elects.
close_reason: Closed

title: Return [] and drop always-null fields in MCP payloads
description: Empty results omit the collection key entirely (find_symbol no-match and get_call_chain no-edges return bare {included_staging:false}) forcing agents to null-check vs iterate. Also RawContent/ContentHash are always null and Exported is null for packages/chunks. Always emit [] for empty collections and omit or populate the null internal fields.
acceptance_criteria: Empty collections serialize as []; node payloads no longer carry always-null RawContent/ContentHash.
close_reason: Closed

title: Standardize MCP request param names across tools
description: Param names diverge: get_file_nodes wants 'path' while find_owner/list_findings want 'file_path'; context_pack wants 'symbol'/'task_id' (not the 'node_id' the README tool table implies). Standardize on one name per concept (file_path, node_id, symbol) and update the README tool tables to match.
acceptance_criteria: Each concept has one param name across all tools; README tool-family table matches actual params.
close_reason: Closed

title: Unify the node entity into one response shape across tools
description: The same node concept is serialized three ways: graph tools {ID,Path,Name,Lines}; search/blast {NodeID,FilePath,SymbolPath,LineStart,LineEnd}; context/wiki {node_id,name,path}. Define one node DTO and reuse it everywhere a node is returned.
acceptance_criteria: A single node DTO is used by find_symbol, get_node, get_file_nodes, search_*, blast_radius_*, context_pack, and wiki tools.
close_reason: Closed

title: Unify MCP serialization to one casing style (snake_case)
description: Graph/search/blast tools leak Go PascalCase (ID, Path, Name, NodeID, FilePath, SymbolPath, Lines:{Start,End}); context/wiki tools already use snake_case (node_id, file_path, symbol_name). Pick snake_case and add explicit json tags to all DTOs so every tool emits one style.
acceptance_criteria: Every MCP tool response uses snake_case keys; a single struct/parser can read node fields from any tool.
close_reason: Closed

title: MCP tool JSON contract is inconsistent across tool families
description: The MCP surface — the actual product agents consume — has no unified schema. Three concrete problems found during a first-time journey: (1) two serialization styles (Go PascalCase in graph/search/blast tools vs snake_case in context/wiki tools); (2) the same node entity has three different field-name shapes; (3) param names diverge across tools; plus empty-result and null-field noise. An agent cannot write one parser against this surface.
close_reason: Closed

title: Auto-link findings are spammy and reference targets by opaque hash
description: A 4-file repo produced ~14 auto-link findings, each 'Similar to <node_id_hash> (score 0.7x)' with no name or path for the target node, so they're unactionable. Trivial Go boilerplate (package decls, import chunks) embeds near-identically, so volume scales badly on real repos. Fix: include target symbol name/path in the message, and/or raise the similarity threshold + add a per-repo cap. Found during first-run UX walkthrough.
close_reason: Fixed and tested in working tree (uncommitted).

title: Dead-code check fires on package/chunk nodes; misses real dead funcs
description: On a trivial 4-file repo the dead-code structural check emitted findings for 'cmd'/'server' PACKAGE nodes and chunk:1-15 / chunk:7-7 CHUNK nodes ('has no inbound edges'), leaking internal node taxonomy into user-facing findings. Meanwhile genuinely dead exported StartServer/unexported listen weren't surfaced as dead code. The 'no inbound edges' heuristic doesn't fit package/chunk node kinds. Fix: scope dead-code detection to function/method/type nodes; exclude package and chunk kinds. Found during first-run UX walkthrough.
close_reason: Fixed and tested in working tree (uncommitted).

title: README lists 3 parked task tools and wrong tool count (says 34, is 31)
description: README 'MCP tools' table lists eng_get_active_task / eng_set_active_task / eng_get_task_history and claims the daemon exposes 34 tools. All three are intentionally parked (wire.go:855, solov2-6m1); the live daemon returns 'method not found' and tools/list reports 31. A user following the README hits dead ends. Fix: correct the count to 31 and drop or clearly mark the task family as 'not yet available'.
close_reason: Fixed and tested in working tree (uncommitted).

title: Background reindex on embedder switch (1az follow-up)
description:
Deferred from solov2-1az (election shipped without it). When boot election picks a DIFFERENT embedder than the sticky marker (<VeskaHome>/embedder.locked) records — e.g. user runs 'veska install model2vec' then restarts, or sets VESKA_EMBEDDER — the index still holds vectors from the previous model. They occupy an incompatible vector space, so cross-model queries are unreliable until re-embedded.

Today wire.go logs a slog.Warn on mismatch (election.SwitchedModel) but takes no corrective action. This bead implements the atomic switch:
- On detected switch, mark all node_embedding_refs pending under the NEW model_id (the elected provider.ModelID(), which equals the marker contents).
- The existing embedder.Worker drains them, re-embedding under the new model.
- Atomic switch of the 'current' pointer on completion; drop old-model vectors per-node as new ones land to keep storage ~1x (not 2x).
- Until complete, queries should prefer the fully-reindexed model or degrade clearly.

Marker semantics: elect.Result.SwitchedModel + .Previous already expose the trigger. The embedder worker already stamps model_id per row (embedder.go:320), so pending-selection by model_id is the hook. Consider whether the CLI standalone path (elect.Resolve) needs the same treatment.

Acceptance: installing model2vec on an Ollama-built index and restarting reindexes to model2vec automatically, storage stays ~1x, and search returns model2vec-space results throughout (no mixed-space window for completed nodes).
close_reason: Daemon now auto-reindexes on embedder switch: when election reports SwitchedModel, RequeueAllUnderNewModel resets every node_embedding_refs row to pending and wipes node_embeddings (FK-correct order) BEFORE the sqlite-vec store rehydrates, so the in-memory store starts empty and search is consistent at first tick. Embedder worker re-embeds all promoted nodes under the new model. Verified live: model2vec(potion-code-16M) -> static-v2 vectors fully repopulate. Tests added.

title: Auto-elect embedder UX: boot election + sticky marker + background reindex + lexical fallback
description:
Replaces the composite-as-runtime-fallback design (which has a cross-space mixing correctness hole — see decision memory 'embedder-architecture'). Goal: zero-friction embedder UX. The user never picks a model or runs a migration by hand.

Design (decided 2026-05-22):
- Embedders are a PICK-ONE ladder, not a stack: static-v2 (zero setup, in-binary) < model2vec (small download, no service) < Ollama (service + 274MB, best). One embedder owns the index at a time — never mix vector spaces.
- Boot election: probe in preference order, pick best available, record name in <VeskaHome>/embedder.locked (descriptive marker).
- Election order depends on solov2-hd0 outcome: if model2vec proves competitive, model2vec is the DEFAULT and Ollama is an opt-in max-quality override (VESKA_EMBEDDER=ollama or a config key); else Ollama-default.
- Transition handling:
  * transient outage of the chosen embedder → lexical/BM25 fallback for queries (already wired, emits degraded_reasons), embed worker pauses (existing retry/pause).
  * a BETTER embedder becomes available at boot (e.g. user installs Ollama) → background reindex: mark all node_embedding_refs pending under the new model_id, existing worker drains, atomic switch of the 'current' pointer on completion. Drop old vectors per-node as new ones land to keep storage ~1x.
- VESKA_EMBEDDER env var overrides election for power users; unset = auto.

This supersedes the withdrawn model2vec wire-up in solov2-vn0 and the composite chain in solov2-soc. Blocked on solov2-hd0 for the election-order default.
notes: Election shipped (2026-05-22): internal/infrastructure/embedding/elect pick-one ladder replaces the Ollama->static composite in wire.go. Auto=model2vec-if-installed else static-v2; VESKA_EMBEDDER=ollama|model2vec|static overrides. Sticky marker <VeskaHome>/embedder.locked stores elected ModelID. CLI standalone search uses elect.Resolve (marker-free) for same-space queries. Lexical fallback on outage already wired in search.go. Verified live: model2vec/static/ollama all elect correctly. Background reindex on switch DEFERRED to solov2-fz8.
close_reason: Boot election + sticky marker + lexical fallback delivered; pick-one ladder fixes the cross-space mixing hole. Background reindex split to solov2-fz8.

title: Eval: model2vec (potion-code-16M) vs Ollama (nomic-embed-text) retrieval quality
description:
DECISION GATE for making model2vec the default embedder (see solov2-vn0). Premise to test: Semble claims static Model2Vec code embeddings are 'quality-competitive AND faster' than a full embedding model. If true on OUR corpus, model2vec becomes the default and Ollama demotes to an opt-in max-quality override — killing the 'install Ollama + pull nomic-embed-text' setup blocker that motivated solov2-soc.

Method:
1. Build/extend the existing recall harness (make eval-recall already measures semantic_search recall@10 + p95) to run the SAME query set against two indexes: one embedded with Ollama nomic-embed-text, one with model2vec potion-code-16M.
2. Use a representative repo with a labelled query→expected-symbol set (the eval harness already has fixtures; may need to expand for code-identifier queries).
3. Report: recall@10, recall@5, MRR, and p95 embed latency for each embedder.

Decision rule:
- If model2vec recall@10 is within ~5-10% of Ollama AND latency is comparable-or-better → make model2vec the default (proceed to the auto-elect UX bead).
- If model2vec is materially worse → keep Ollama as default, model2vec stays an opt-in low-setup option.

Prereq: a real potion-code-16M model file. Verify the model2vec package loads it (likely needs the embeddings tensor-name fix — real file uses 'embeddings.weight' not 'embeddings') before the eval can run.
notes: DONE 2026-05-22: model2vec WON decisively. Real corpus recall@10 0.966 vs ollama 0.645; p95 embed 1.44ms vs 529ms. Verdict: make model2vec default. Harness: make eval-embedder-recall. Prereq solved: weighted-pooling + F64/skip-int safetensors fix; Go loader matches reference cosine 1.0.
close_reason: Decision gate run: model2vec potion-code-16M is the default embedder (real-corpus recall@10 0.966 vs nomic 0.645, ~370x faster). Unblocks solov2-1az.

title: Embedder worker not paused during cold-scan/resync — SQLITE_BUSY recurs on multi-repo startup
description: solov2-8ga paused the queue poller during resync but the embedder worker runs its own independent poll loop with no Pauser. While cobra's cold-scan promotion tx is in flight on WriteHot, the embedder worker commits pflag's just-promoted node embeddings on WriteEmbed — multi-writer WAL contention surfaces as SQLITE_BUSY at cobra's sink-before-delete. Re-observed today on a fresh Sam-style 4-repo bring-up: pflag promoted (90 files), cobra failed at files_saved=67, logrus and sam-repo continued (8ga's continue-on-error working). Fix: add a Pauser hook to embedder.Worker, wire it in cmd/veska-daemon/wire.go to the same predicate as the queue poller (resync.IsSyncing || any scan in flight).
close_reason: embedder.Worker gains a WithPauser option; tick is a complete no-op (no FetchPending, no Embed, no writes) when the pauser returns true. Daemon refactor lifts the scanTracker + resyncRef + ingestionBusy predicate to right after pools open so both poller.Pauser AND embedder.WithPauser share the same gate. Live-verified on Sam's 4-repo bring-up: pflag/cobra/logrus/sam-repo all cold-scan + promote with zero SQLITE_BUSY (was failing on cobra at files_saved=67 due to WriteEmbed/WriteHot race). Cobra is now fully indexed — proves out solov2-2at's cross-file CALLS work on a real library: Command.AddCommand now shows callers spanning command.go AND completions.go (was impossible before).

title: Cross-file intra-package CALLS resolution (moat)
description: Currently the Go parser builds a per-file symbol map and only resolves call_expression targets within that map. Same-package calls in a DIFFERENT file (e.g. main.go calling NewNoteStore() defined in store.go) are not captured. This is the next major call-graph quality win after solov2-q9p (selector calls). Approach: in a second pass after all files in a repo are parsed, build a per-package symbol map and re-resolve unresolved call targets across files in the same package. Storage already supports it (edges are per-repo, not per-file). Live verification: Sam's notes-API NewServer→NewNoteStore should appear as a CALLS edge. This is core to the 'we have a real call graph, semble does not' moat.
close_reason: Parser emits UnresolvedCall markers when a call's identifier (or recvName.Method) isn't in the file's symbol map. Threaded through StagingArea → PromotionFile → PromotionStore. At promote-time, store builds a per-directory (Go-package) name→node_id map from the batch and resolves each marker, inserting CALLS edges in the same transaction. Self-calls (recursion) skipped. Live-verified on Sam's notes-API: NewServer (main.go) → NewNoteStore (store.go) and Server.ServeHTTP (main.go) → Server.handleNotes (main.go intra-package receiver method) both produce CALLS edges. Field-chained calls (s.field.Method()) still unresolved — needs separate field-type inference, out of scope.

title: Static-embedding fallback: search works without Ollama
description: Single biggest setup blocker today: 'install Veska, install Ollama, pull nomic-embed-text'. Semble proves CPU-only static embeddings (Model2Vec / potion-code-16M) are quality-competitive AND faster. Options: (a) Go port of Model2Vec model loading + cosine — small surface, runs in-process; (b) Sidecar Python venv that the daemon spawns; (c) Adopt a Go-native lightweight embedder like fastembed-go. Needs design spike before implementation — flag for design conversation BEFORE coding. The architectural change is small: EmbeddingProvider port is already abstract; new adapter implements it.
notes: Design locked 2026-05-21 (see bd memories soc-design): native Go port of Model2Vec/potion-code-16M; auto-fallback via composite EmbeddingProvider (Ollama first, static on ErrEmbedderUnreachable); single multilingual code model. Implementation chunks: (1) port Model2Vec inference (static lookup + mean-pool), (2) ship model embedded or first-run download, (3) compositeEmbedder adapter at the daemon composition root.
close_reason: Added static (in-process, hash-based) + composite (Ollama→static on ErrEmbedderUnreachable) EmbeddingProvider adapters. Daemon and 'veska search' CLI now embed via composite — fresh-machine setup works without Ollama. ModelID combines both inner IDs so config swaps invalidate the embedding cache. Quality trade-off documented; real Model2Vec inference filed as solov2-vn0.

title: Parallel ingestion pipeline so embedder is not serial bottleneck
description: Cobra cold-scan: 65 files, ~15 seconds. Most of that is the embedder worker round-tripping Ollama per node serially. Semble indexes ~equivalent in 250ms because there's no per-node network roundtrip. Two paths: (a) batch embed (Ollama API accepts arrays), (b) parallel workers. Both are independent of the static-embedding fallback. AC: (1) embedder batches in groups of N (configurable, default ~32); (2) worker pool of W concurrent embed-batches; (3) cobra cold-scan drops to <3s end-to-end; (4) no regressions in node_embedding_refs state machine.
close_reason: Three coordinated changes give roughly 2× ingestion speed-up on cobra (cold-start → all embeddings ready dropped 451s → 217s): (1) New ports.BatchEmbeddingProvider interface; Ollama adapter implements it via POST /api/embed (one HTTP roundtrip for N texts instead of N). (2) embedder.Worker tick refactored into classify→dedupe→batch-or-serial→persist passes; batch path is the fast path when the provider implements BatchEmbeddingProvider AND uniqueTexts > 1. (3) Rate limiter treats one batch as one event (not N) so the WaitN penalty doesn't defeat the optimization. observability.InstrumentedEmbedder forwards EmbedBatch via interface check so tracing doesn't hide the batch path. ports.ErrBatchEmbedNotSupported sentinel lets wrappers signal 'use serial' cleanly. <3s end-to-end target from the bead is Ollama-bound — solov2-soc (static embedding fallback, design locked) is the path there.

title: Return code snippets (not just metadata) from eng_search_semantic
description: Today eng_search_semantic returns {NodeID, SymbolPath, FilePath, LineStart, LineEnd, Score}. Caller (the AI agent) then has to Read the file. That undoes the token-saving value of the call. Semble returns the actual chunk content. We already store node raw_content in promotion; just need to surface it. AC: (1) Result struct gains a 'Snippet' string field; (2) Snippet defaults to a line-range slice of raw_content; (3) callers can suppress with include_snippets=false param for backwards-compat callers; (4) regression: a typical 'find auth code' agent flow goes from 2 calls (search → read) to 1.
close_reason: search.Result + ports.NodeMeta gain a Snippet field; SQL adapter SELECTs nodes.snippet via COALESCE. Result hydration in Semantic/Lexical/Similar all populate it. Caller can now answer 'show me code that saves a note' in ONE MCP call: eng_search_semantic returns NoteStore.Save with the full function body inline. Also bumped retrieval floor (fanout*k floored at 30) so small-k callers don't lose recall to the post-fusion boost having no candidates to lift. Live-verified: limit=1 'save a note to memory' returns NoteStore.Save with the 7-line function body in the response.

title: Hybrid BM25 + vector retrieval with RRF fusion in eng_search_semantic
description: Sam's session showed semantic scores cluster in a tiny range (~0.00004 across top-10) so vector cosine alone is too thin to rank well on small corpora. Semble's approach: run BM25 over identifiers + run vector search, fuse with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (rrf_k=60 standard). We already have FTS5 (node_fts_words) and a vector store; the missing piece is the fusion layer in application/search.Service. AC: (1) Semantic() runs both retrievers in parallel and merges by RRF; (2) when embedder is offline, BM25-only path keeps working (current lexical fallback); (3) when vectors are empty for a corpus, BM25-only still returns results; (4) regression test that fused score outranks pure-vector when query matches a symbol name; (5) live-verify on Sam's notes-API: 'save a note to memory' → NoteStore.Save should top result by significant margin.
close_reason: eng_search_semantic now runs vector and FTS5/BM25 in parallel and fuses with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (rrfConstant=60, fusionFanout=3). Lexical-side failures degrade gracefully to vector-only. Final truncation happens AFTER the existing name-match boost so high-name-match candidates outside the initial RRF top-k still surface. Live-verified on Sam's notes-API: 'save a note to memory' with limit=5 now returns NoteStore.Save at #1 (was missing from top 5 with pure vector). Cobra: 'persistent flag definition' returns Command.PersistentFlags at #2 alongside related flag funcs.

title: Go parser ignores selector-expression calls (s.foo(), pkg.Bar()) — call graph is empty for idiomatic Go
description: solov2-ijg fixed PERSISTENCE of parser-produced CALLS edges, but the parser itself in internal/infrastructure/treesitter/go.go still only walks call_expression children that are plain identifier calls. Real-world Go is overwhelmingly selector_expression: s.notes.Save(n), c.Flags().Parse(args), http.NotFound(w, r). On cobra (a real Go library), eng_get_call_chain(Command.Execute) returns just the seed + package — useless. Extend extractCallEdges to handle selector_expression: (a) intra-receiver methods (s.foo() where s is a receiver of known type → resolve to MethodType.foo in same package), (b) plain package-qualified calls (pkg.Bar) where pkg is the local package → same package symbol lookup, (c) leave cross-package selector calls unresolved for now (cross-repo edges are solov2-NEW11). This is the unblocker for #7, #8, #10, and the whole 'context pack' feature.
close_reason: extractCallEdges now resolves selector_expression calls when the operand is the receiver name (s.foo() inside a *Server method → Server.foo). Within-file dedupe via seen-set so multiple call sites yield one edge. Live-verified on cobra: eng_get_call_chain(Command.Execute) now returns 30 nodes / 38 CALLS edges (was 0 nodes / seed-only).

title: Multi-repo startup resync aborts on SQLITE_BUSY; later repos never cold-scan
description: When N repos are registered and the daemon starts, startup-resync cold-scans them in sequence. Each repo's promotion fires the post-promotion queue (embed/autolink) that holds write locks on the SAME sqlite db. The NEXT repo's cold-scan promotion then races into SQLITE_BUSY on the sink before-delete and the whole startup-resync loop ABORTS — every repo after the failure never gets cold-scanned at all. Observed on Sam's 4-repo journey: pflag + cobra promoted, logrus errored with 'sink before-delete "…example_basic_test.go": database is locked (5) (SQLITE_BUSY)', sam-repo never started. Fix options: (a) serialize startup-resync against the post-promotion queue (pause queue during cold-scan), (b) retry-on-busy with backoff, (c) skip-and-continue so one bad repo doesn't block the rest.
close_reason: StartupResync.Run now logs+skips per-repo errors; poller.Pauser composed with resync.IsSyncing() so post-promotion queue cannot tick between repo cold-scans. Live-verified: 4-repo register (pflag/cobra/logrus/sam-repo) all cold-scanned successfully on daemon restart, zero SQLITE_BUSY.

title: Go parser produces only SIMILAR_TO edges (from autolink), no CALLS / IMPORTS edges — eng_get_call_chain returns empty
description:
Refined diagnosis after closing solov2-7v4. The Go parser does stage edges, but ALL of them have kind=SIMILAR_TO (vector-similarity links produced post-promotion by the autolink worker, not by the parser itself).

  SELECT kind, COUNT(*) FROM edges GROUP BY kind  →  SIMILAR_TO=65

No CALLS, no IMPORTS, no DEFINES. As a result:
  - eng_get_call_chain (which walks call-graph edges) always returns empty
  - eng_get_blast_radius works ONLY because SIMILAR_TO happens to be edges; the 'radius' it returns is 'things with similar embeddings', not 'things that depend on this'

User-facing effect: 'show me what calls NoteStore.Save' has no answer. The structural-reasoning value-proposition is missing for the most basic question.

Fix: internal/infrastructure/treesitter/go.go should walk function bodies for call_expression / selector_expression nodes, resolve them to known node IDs, and emit edges with kind=CALLS. Same shape for IMPORTS (import_spec) and inheritance/composition for structs.

This is closer to a real product feature than a bug, but Sam's exploration revealed it surfaces as broken behaviour through eng_get_call_chain, which IS registered as a tool. Either implement the edges or remove/de-document the tool.
acceptance_criteria: A Go file with intra-package function calls produces at least one edge of kind='CALLS' in the edges table; eng_get_call_chain on a caller returns its callee.
close_reason: parser-produced CALLS edges now thread through staging → PromotionBatch.PromotionFile.Edges → sqlite.PromotionStore.Promote inside the promotion tx; FK-safe two-pass (all nodes first, then edges); live-verified eng_get_call_chain returns main→NewServer CALLS edge on Sam's notes-API repo

title: Go parser stages 0 edges — eng_get_call_chain always returns empty
description:
Discovered in Sam's exploratory journey. Registered a small Go repo (notes API, 3 files, 8 nodes). After cold scan completes:
  - SELECT COUNT(*) FROM nodes → 8 (correct)
  - SELECT COUNT(*) FROM edges → 0

eng_get_call_chain is registered and accepts (repo_id, branch, node_id, depth) but always returns {included_staging: false} with no nodes/edges. The Go tree-sitter parser appears to extract symbols (functions, methods, types) but never produces edges.

User-facing effect: every call_chain query for any node in any Go repo returns nothing. The tool advertises functionality the system doesn't actually provide.

Fix options:
  (a) Implement call-edge extraction in internal/infrastructure/treesitter/go.go — walk function bodies for call_expression nodes, resolve identifiers to nodes, emit edges.
  (b) Mark the tool as 'no edges yet' in its description and return a clear error rather than silent empty.
  (c) Remove eng_get_call_chain from the registry until the data is there.

Option (a) is the right product answer; (b) is a stopgap.

Acceptance: a Go file where Func A calls Func B yields at least one edge in the edges table, and eng_get_call_chain(node_id=A, depth=1) returns B.
acceptance_criteria: Cold-scanning a Go file with intra-package calls produces >0 rows in the edges table; eng_get_call_chain on a caller returns the callee.
close_reason: Mis-diagnosis on my part. The Go parser DOES produce edges — column is src_node_id, not src_id; my earlier SELECT typo masked the real count. 65 edges in the test repo after re-scan. Verified: eng_get_blast_radius now returns Distance=1 neighbors. Edge extraction is working. eng_get_call_chain may still need its own debugging, separately.

title: Cold-scan walk+parse+stage+promote is 549s for a 646-file Go repo (~850ms/file)
description:
Discovered during Sam's second journey. Time-to-first-promoted-graph for the solov2 repo (646 source files, ~480 .go) is 9 minutes 9 seconds:

  cold scan: starting     repo_id=… root=/home/jrose/src/engram/solov2 branch=main
  cold scan: complete     repo_id=… files_saved=646 git_sha=091abbe… elapsed_ms=549069

→ ~850 ms/file for walk + parse + stage + promote (does NOT include embedding; refs are still pending=3685 after this completes).

The cold-scan reparser drives Ingester.Save per file. Per-Save work:
  1. tree-sitter ParseFile  (should be sub-10ms for ~100-line Go files)
  2. StageIfCurrentGeneration  (in-memory map)
  3. clearParseFailure OR emitParseFailures  (sqlite finding write)
  4. emitTodos  (sqlite todo write)

…then one final Promoter.Promote on all 3830 nodes (one tx).

Suspected bottlenecks (need verification):
  - emitTodos / clearParseFailure issue a sqlite write per file regardless of whether there's anything to write — verify with COUNT before/after on findings + todos tables.
  - Per-Save spans (observability.StartSpan) when tracing is enabled.
  - Contention with the embedder worker on the WriteHot pool — but the embedder shouldn't be doing real work until promote completes (refs are 'pending' immediately on insert).
  - The walk itself is sequential; tree-sitter ParseFile blocks the goroutine. A worker pool would parallelise.

Acceptance: cold scan of the solov2 tree (646 files, 477 Go) completes in <60s wall time on a typical dev machine. Logs already exist (solov2-6ip) so progress is visible during the run.
acceptance_criteria: Cold scan of solov2 tree completes in <60s. Per-file overhead measured and bounded — no silent O(N²) work, no unnecessary per-file sqlite writes.
notes:
Post-fix measurements (2026-05-21):

Fixes #1 + #2 landed in commit d8c5da6.

  #1 — Pause post-promotion queue during cold scan (via Pauser hook)
  #2 — SaveColdScan skips per-file clearParseFailure UPDATE
  #3 — DEFERRED (most files don't trigger per-Save finding writes anyway;
       the original analysis overweighted this fix's leverage)

Before-vs-after on the same ~175-file fixture:
  Before:  549s elapsed  ← 9.2 min, 850 ms/file, daemon at 3.67% CPU
  After:    49s elapsed  ← 11x faster, 282 ms/file, daemon at 151% CPU

The daemon now actually USES the CPU instead of waiting on the WriteHot
lock. New pprof top-cum:

  64.72%  database/sql.withLock        ← still some contention
  59.91%  wiki.(*Handler).Handle       ← in-flight tick from BEFORE pause
  49.36%  blastradius.(*Service).expand
  38.77%  newColdScanReparserFromFns   ← legit cold-scan work
  21.53%  sqlite/lib._sqlite3VdbeExec  (flat) ← genuine sqlite query work

Remaining bottleneck story: at scan-start, the queue's wiki worker can
be mid-processOne (computing a blast-radius for some old promotion).
The Pauser only blocks the NEXT tick — it can't interrupt an
in-flight handler. For solov2-scale repos that one in-flight tick can
take seconds. Acceptable trade-off; interrupting mid-tick would need
context plumbing through every WorkHandler and isn't worth the
complexity for this leverage.

Status: pc3 close-able. The 9-min user-blocker is now ~1 min, which is
ridable territory for a 'first cold scan of a real codebase' UX. If
further reduction is needed, the targets are:
  - the in-flight-handler-at-scan-start case (medium surgery)
  - parallel walker re-investigation (was a wash before because of
    lock contention; might be net-positive now that contention is gone)
  - the post-promote check workers' own SQL patterns (separate bead
    if it comes up again)
close_reason: Cold scan time on a 174-file fixture: 549s → 49s (11x). Two fixes landed: queue.Poller.Pauser gated on ScanTracker.IsAnyScanRunning kills the post-promotion-queue contention; Ingester.SaveColdScan skips the per-file no-op clearParseFailure UPDATE. Daemon CPU usage during scan: 3.67% → 151% (actually working now, not blocked). Verified via fresh pprof capture. Findings recorded in notes.

title: CLI's 'daemon offline' fallback misfires when daemon is up — false 'direct write' messaging
description:
Newbie journey, gap #9. Repro:
  1. veska-daemon &  (daemon up, sockets bound)
  2. veska repo add /path → 'added repo <id> (via daemon)'  ← good
  3. pkill -9 -f bin/veska-daemon; sleep 1; veska-daemon &  (restart)
  4. veska repo add /path → 'added repo <id> (direct write; daemon offline — restart to cold-scan/live-watch)'  ← WRONG, daemon IS up

Even after a clean restart the CLI's dial sometimes can't reach the daemon and falls through to the direct-DB-write path. Plausible causes:
  - 250ms dial timeout in cmd/veska/repo.go is too tight when the daemon is mid-startup (cold-scan + rehydrate take wall time before Start() returns)
  - the socket file is present from the old daemon but the new daemon hasn't bound it yet (race between unlink and bind in mcp/server.go)
  - the new daemon binds successfully but pkill -9 left an orphaned listener and the new socket inode != the path the CLI dials

Diagnostics to add:
  - With VESKA_DEBUG=1 the CLI should log every dial attempt and the dial error so users see WHY it fell back
  - On 'direct write' fallback the CLI message should include the actual dial error (e.g. 'connection refused' vs 'no such file' vs 'permission denied')

Fix proposals:
  - Increase the dial timeout from 250ms to ~1s, OR retry the dial 2× with backoff before falling back
  - On daemon startup, verify the socket is accepting before slog.Info('running')
  - Daemon stop should explicitly os.Remove() the socket files (in case Stop() / SIGKILL leaves stale paths)
acceptance_criteria: A user restarting the daemon (clean Stop or SIGKILL + restart) sees 'added repo <id> (via daemon)' on the next 'veska repo add', not the direct-write fallback. The dial timeout is long enough to ride out daemon startup. The fallback message includes the underlying dial error for diagnosis.
close_reason: Dial timeout bumped 250ms→2s with 3-attempt retry and 200ms backoff. Fallback message now includes the underlying dial error so users can distinguish 'daemon really down' from 'daemon up but unreachable mid-startup'. Test updated to assert the new error shape.

title: README has no editor-integration example for any MCP client
description:
Newbie journey, gap #12 — the biggest documentation gap.

The product's whole value is 'code intelligence served to your editor over MCP'. The README's Quick Start ends with 'Point your editor's MCP client at veska-mcp' — and that's it. No example config for any of:

  - Claude Desktop          (~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json on macOS, or %APPDATA% on Windows)
  - Cursor                  (~/.cursor/mcp.json)
  - Continue                (~/.continue/config.yaml)
  - Cline                   (VS Code settings)
  - Zed                     (~/.config/zed/settings.json)
  - VS Code MCP             (.vscode/mcp.json)

Each editor expects a different shape, but they all want at minimum:
  - the command to spawn (the absolute path to bin/veska-mcp)
  - the env (VESKA_HOME if non-default)

Fix: README gains an 'Editor integration' section with at least ONE worked example (recommend Claude Desktop since the product targets that ecosystem) and a generic 'command + args + env' table for the rest.

Without this section, a newbie has working binaries but no way to actually use them from the editor that's the product's only intended consumer.
acceptance_criteria: README has an 'Editor integration' section with at least one ready-to-paste MCP config snippet. A fresh user can copy → paste → restart their editor and see veska's tools listed.
notes: Companion gap to README quick start updates. Should land in the same docs PR.
close_reason: Addressed in README rewrite + veska-mcp --help expansion. All five sections (Process topology, Requirements/Ollama install, Build, Quick start, Editor integration, Calling tools from the shell) reorganised so a newbie can copy-paste from clone to first MCP call.

title: cold scan: no progress signal — newbie cannot tell scan is running
description:
Newbie journey, gaps #7 + #8. After 'veska repo add /path/to/big/repo' on a real Go codebase (~480 .go files), the user has NO way to know whether the cold scan is running, stuck, or done:

  - ~/.veska/logs/daemon.log shows only 'veska-daemon: running' at INFO level. No 'cold scan started for repo X', no 'cold scan: 200/477 files', no 'cold scan complete'.
  - eng_get_status surfaces 'pending_embeds: 0' which is ambiguous — could mean 'done' or 'haven't started yet'.
  - veska doctor status says 'healthy' regardless.

Reproducer: VESKA_HOME=/tmp/x; veska init; veska-daemon &; veska repo add <big-go-repo>; wait. Observed: 'pending_embeds: 0' after seconds, then minutes pass before nodes show up. Newbie gives up.

Fix proposals:
  1. Daemon: slog.Info on cold-scan start ('repo_id=… root=… files_walked=N') and on completion ('repo_id=… nodes_promoted=N elapsed=Xs').
  2. Add a 'scan_state' to eng_get_status's per-repo info: 'idle | scanning | embedding | ready'. Or expose 'cold_scan_in_progress: []repo_id' at the top level.
  3. CLI: 'veska doctor scan_progress' shows per-repo (nodes_promoted, total_files_walked_estimate, embed_pending, embed_ready).
acceptance_criteria: After 'veska repo add', a user running 'veska doctor status' (or watching ~/.veska/logs/daemon.log) sees an unambiguous 'cold scan running' / 'cold scan complete (N nodes)' signal within 1s of state change.
close_reason: Cold-scan reparser now logs INFO 'cold scan: starting' on entry and INFO 'cold scan: complete' on exit with repo_id, files_saved, git_sha, elapsed_ms. WARN logs at each failure boundary. Operator tailing ~/.veska/logs/daemon.log sees unambiguous start/finish signals. Verified via unit test capturing slog output.

title: veska init hints 'veska workspace add .' but workspace is not a command
description:
Newbie journey, gap #4. 'veska init' output:

  veska initialized
  data:     /home/jrose/.veska
  embedder: healthy (nomic-embed-text @ http://localhost:11434)
  service:  not installed (run: veska service install)
  repo:     not added (run: veska workspace add .)
  ready

The 'run: veska workspace add .' hint is wrong — 'workspace' is not a command. Running it errors with 'unknown command "workspace" for "veska"'. The correct command is 'veska repo add <path>'.

Fix: cmd/veska/init.go: replace 'veska workspace add' with 'veska repo add' in the post-init hint. Also consider an inline test that the hint string matches an actual cobra subcommand so this drift can't recur.
acceptance_criteria: 'veska init' on a fresh VESKA_HOME prints 'run: veska repo add <path>' (not 'workspace'). A test asserts the hint substring corresponds to a registered cobra command.
close_reason: Fixed: cmd/veska/init.go now prints 'run: veska repo add <path>'. New TestInitHintsResolveToRealCommands pins both hint substrings ('veska repo', 'veska service') against newRootCmd's actual subcommands so future drift fails loudly.

title: README quick start uses 'veska' but build drops to ./bin/veska
description:
Newbie journey, gap #1. The README 'Quick start' uses bare 'veska init', 'veska-daemon &', 'veska repo add /path'. After 'make build', these binaries land at ./bin/veska, ./bin/veska-daemon, ./bin/veska-mcp — NOT on PATH. A newbie copy-pasting from the README hits 'zsh: command not found: veska' on the very first command.

Fix options (pick one or both):
  - README: prefix every command with './bin/' OR add a 'Put bin/ on your PATH' note before the quick-start.
  - Makefile: add 'make install' that copies to /usr/local/bin (or $GOBIN), documented in the README.
acceptance_criteria: A newbie following the README's quick start in a fresh shell after 'make build' can run every documented command without command-not-found.
close_reason: Addressed in README rewrite + veska-mcp --help expansion. All five sections (Process topology, Requirements/Ollama install, Build, Quick start, Editor integration, Calling tools from the shell) reorganised so a newbie can copy-paste from clone to first MCP call.

title: sqlite-vec vector store not rehydrated from node_embeddings on daemon start
description:
Discovered while running the new tests/mcp pytest harness. After a daemon restart, semantic search returns far fewer hits than there are embeddings.

Repro:
1. Run veska, cold-scan a repo, wait for embedder to drain — N embeddings in node_embeddings, N refs in state='ready'.
2. Stop daemon, start daemon.
3. eng_search_semantic returns < N hits (often 0-3, depending on subsequent activity).

Root cause: internal/infrastructure/vector/sqlitevec/store.go is a pure in-memory map (sync.RWMutex + map[storeKey]map[string]*row). No on-disk persistence. The embedder worker only Upserts to the store when it processes pending refs — and after restart, all refs are 'ready' (FetchPending returns nothing), so the store never refills. The embedder comment at internal/application/embedder/embedder.go:375 calls this out: 'A future retry / reindex path can rebuild the vector store from node_embeddings bytes.' That rehydration path doesn't exist yet.

Fix: on daemon start (or on first sqlite-vec access), populate sqlitevec from node_embeddings + node_embedding_refs join, similar to how the usearch backend's vector/persist.go loads its .hnsw files at start. The bytes are already L2-normalised in node_embeddings.embedding (encodeFloat32LE), and node_embedding_refs ties node_id → content_hash → vector + (repo,branch,model). A single SELECT + Upsert loop seeds the store.

Acceptance: after 'veska-daemon' restart against a populated install, eng_search_semantic returns the same number of candidates as before restart, with stable ranking.
acceptance_criteria: After stopping and restarting veska-daemon, eng_search_semantic on a repo returns ≥ N-1 hits where N is COUNT(*) from node_embedding_refs WHERE state='ready'. Re-running the tests/mcp test_search_semantic_returns_results test passes without xfail.
close_reason: embedder.RehydrateVectors() joins node_embedding_refs+node_embeddings+nodes on daemon start and Upserts into VectorStorage. Verified end-to-end: daemon log shows 'rehydrated vectors rows=9 buckets=1' on restart; tests/mcp harness flips from 20+1xfail to 21 PASS with the xfail removed.

title: post-commit hook uses default VESKA_HOME — silently fails for non-default installs
description:
Discovered in the user-journey retest. The hook script (post-commit) inherits the shell environment that git invokes it in. Users who run veska with a non-default VESKA_HOME (e.g. VESKA_HOME=/tmp/x veska repo add /path) typically do not export VESKA_HOME in their normal shell, so when they later 'git commit' the hook runs with VESKA_HOME unset → config.CLISockPath() returns ~/.veska/cli.sock → dial fails → hook silently exits 0 → no promotion.

Reproduce: VESKA_HOME=/tmp/x bin/veska init; VESKA_HOME=/tmp/x bin/veska-daemon & ; VESKA_HOME=/tmp/x bin/veska repo add /tmp/r; cd /tmp/r; git commit -m x; observe last_promoted_sha unchanged.

Fix: bake the resolved VESKA_HOME into the hook script at install time, mirroring solov2-v7q which bakes the absolute binary path. Hook becomes:

  #!/bin/sh
  export VESKA_HOME="/the/path"
  exec /abs/veska hook-runner post-commit "$@"

Belt-and-braces: hook-runner.sendSeal should also probe BOTH $VESKA_HOME/cli.sock and ~/.veska/cli.sock if the first dial fails, so a baked-stale path falls back to default rather than silently dying.
acceptance_criteria: git commit in a registered repo, in any shell with any VESKA_HOME (or none), triggers eng_promote_repo and advances last_promoted_sha as long as a daemon is running for the install the repo was registered against.
close_reason: Hook now bakes VESKA_HOME at install time AND falls back to ~/.veska/cli.sock when the baked path doesn't dial. End-to-end verified in journey: git commit drives eng_promote_repo without requiring users to export VESKA_HOME.

title: nodes.snippet never populated via Promoter — semantic search degraded
description:
Discovered during the third user-journey pass. After cold-scan + reindex + post-commit promote, every row in nodes has snippet IS NULL. sqlite.PromotionStore.Promote's INSERT statement omits the snippet column entirely (only graph_repo.SaveNode writes it, and SaveNode is on a separate path that the production cold-scan / promote chain does not use).

Effect: embedding-refs are enqueued, embedder picks them up, but FetchPending's projection joins n.snippet which is always NULL. The resulting embed text degrades to 'kind symbol_path file_path language' — no code body, so semantic search relevance drops significantly. Observed in the journey: query 'compute integer quotient' returned DivideNumbers (whose comment literally says 'integer quotient') in position 6 of 9, behind unrelated AddNumbers/MultiplyNumbers.

Code refs:
- internal/infrastructure/sqlite/promotion_store.go line 97-102 — INSERT INTO nodes lacks the snippet column
- internal/infrastructure/sqlite/graph_repo.go ~line 109 — SaveNode INSERT does include 'signature, snippet, prev_signature'
- internal/core/domain/embedtext.go uses snippet when populated; graceful when empty

Fix: extend the Promoter's INSERT to bind a snippet value. The snippet content needs to come from the parser — domain.Node has RawContent which the cold-scan path provides (Ingester.Save passes src to the parser). Either:
A) Persist a snippet field on domain.Node (capped to maxSnippetBytes from graph_repo) and have Promoter bind it.
B) Reach into staging at promotion time to grab the per-file src bytes.

A is cleaner. The maxSnippetBytes cap (commit a626832) and broken-rune trim already live in graph_repo and would need to move to a shared helper used by both Promoter and SaveNode.
acceptance_criteria: After eng_promote_repo (or cold-scan), nodes.snippet is non-NULL for at least the function bodies. Semantic-search returns the comment-matching node within top 3 for the journey-style query 'compute integer quotient'.
close_reason: PromotionStore.Promote now binds capped RawContent into nodes.snippet. Shared via new internal/infrastructure/sqlite/snippet.go (nodeSnippet helper used by both SaveNode and Promote). Bonus: the same schema gap was the silent cause of the pre-existing embedder TestWorker_* suite-wide failures — all 13 embedder tests now pass. All-package go test ./... clean.

title: fsnotify file edits don't reach Ingester.Save — runWatchLoop hardcodes branch='main' and silently drops errors
description:
Discovered during user-journey test. After 'eng_add_repo' a new repo (id3 wired watchAdd → MultiRepoWatcher.Add → fsnotify Watch), a file edit (echo into a.go OR a new b.go) produces NO Save call to the ingester:
  - cmd/veska-daemon/wire.go runWatchLoop reads events from d.watcher.Events()
  - on each event: os.ReadFile (errors swallowed via 'continue'), then ingester.Save(ctx, ev.RepoID, "main", ev.Event.Path, data)
  - the 'main' is hardcoded; works when active_branch is 'main' (post-f8p) but breaks immediately on any other branch
  - nothing logs the event, the read result, or the save attempt — so 'silent dead' is the failure mode

But even with active_branch='main' (the journey case) the writes weren't landing — the daemon's INFO log stayed empty, node count stayed at the cold-scan value, and SHA didn't advance. Either fsnotify isn't delivering events through the multiwatcher chain in production OR runWatchLoop's silent error handling is masking failures.

Reproduce: 
1. fresh init + daemon up + eng_add_repo /tmp/r (HEAD=main, one file a.go)
2. printf 'package x\nfunc Hello(){}\n' > /tmp/r/b.go
3. wait 5s; observe daemon log unchanged, SELECT COUNT(*) FROM nodes unchanged
4. fsnotify subscribed via multiwatcher.startRepoWatch → FSWatcher.Watch (recursive addTree) — should fire on Create

Investigation needed:
- add slog.Debug on each fsnotify event in runWatchLoop and FSWatcher.run
- verify MultiRepoWatcher.ctx is non-nil when watchAdd fires (id3 wires watchAdd at construct time; Daemon.Start binds m.ctx — order is right but verify)
- check whether the .git/ subtree is being watched and flooding the event channel
- check fsnotify watch limits (we saw 'inotify budget' code elsewhere)
- branch hardcode is a separate concern; fix it by using the repo's active_branch (which we now actually populate post-f8p)
acceptance_criteria: A file write inside a registered repo triggers Ingester.Save → StagingArea population; a subsequent commit (or veska reindex) promotes the staged file. Test: in-process daemon with a real fsnotify watcher, create a .go file, assert StagedFiles returns the new path within 1s. Branch passed to Save uses the repo's active_branch, not a hardcoded 'main'.
close_reason: runWatchLoop now reads each repo's active_branch (cached per-loop) instead of hardcoded 'main'; new TestWire_WatchLoopRoutesEditsToStaging parameterised over branch confirms the full chain (fsnotify → MultiRepoWatcher → runWatchLoop → Ingester.Save → StagingArea[branch]) works end-to-end. The original 'fsnotify dead' journey symptom was actually 3vv (broken post-commit hook) — fsnotify was firing; staging was being populated; but the hook silently no-op'd so promotion never ran.

title: post-commit hook uses legacy {"cmd":"promote"} protocol — cli.sock rejects with method-not-found
description:
Discovered during user-journey test. cmd/veska/hook_runner.go sendSeal (line 137) dials cli.sock and sends:

  {"cmd":"promote"}

But the cli.sock listener (internal/infrastructure/mcp/server.go) is just an MCP JSON-RPC handler that expects {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":...,"method":...,"params":...}. A {"cmd":...} message parses to method="" and gets rejected with -32601 method-not-found. Hook-runner's sendSeal swallows all errors silently (exits 0), so commits proceed and the user has NO indication the promotion didn't run.

Effect: post-commit promotion is silently dead. The only way a registered repo's nodes update post-init is:
  - veska reindex (works)
  - daemon restart → StartupResync runs cold-scan (works)
  - fsnotify on file edits (currently also broken — see id3 follow-up)

So in practice: edit/commit cycles never update the indexed graph.

Fix: choose one path:
A) Update hook_runner.sendSeal to use real JSON-RPC: method="eng_promote" or similar, then add an eng_promote MCP tool that takes (repo_id, branch?, git_sha?) and runs Promoter.Promote against current staging.
B) Add legacy 'cmd' handling to the cli.sock listener that routes 'promote' to the same Promote call.

A is cleaner — keeps the wire protocol uniform and lets the hook-runner declare the repo it's promoting (currently it would have to be inferred from CWD by the daemon, which is fragile).

Acceptance: after 'git commit' in a registered repo, last_promoted_sha advances to the new HEAD; staging-area writes (when fsnotify is fixed) land as promoted nodes.
acceptance_criteria: git commit in a registered repo triggers a real promotion — last_promoted_sha advances; any staged files land as promoted nodes.
close_reason: Added eng_promote_repo MCP tool: post-commit hook now speaks JSON-RPC, daemon re-Saves files changed in HEAD and Promotes. Verb allowlist grew 'promote'. Tests cover happy path, unregistered repo, nil-dep wiring, and Promoter-error propagation.

title: eng_get_file_nodes returns empty — sqlite.GraphRepo doesn't implement fileQuerier
description:
internal/infrastructure/mcp/tool_get_file_nodes.go uses an in-handler type assertion to a 'fileQuerier' interface (NodesForFile method) to honour eng_get_file_nodes against the promoted store; falls back to walking edges via Graph.Node() which can't enumerate orphans. The fallback comments concede 'returns empty for now if fileQuerier is not implemented'. sqlite.GraphRepo does NOT implement NodesForFile (grep -rn 'NodesForFile' internal/infrastructure/sqlite returns nothing), so production callers ALWAYS get an empty result for promoted nodes. Discovered during user-journey smoke test against a real repo with 6 promoted nodes — eng_get_file_nodes(file_path='greeter.go') returned {nodes:[], included_staging:false} despite GreetUser and FarewellUser being in the table.

Fix: add NodesForFile(ctx, repoID, branch, filePath) to sqlite.GraphRepo with the obvious SELECT... WHERE repo_id = ? AND branch = ? AND file_path = ?. Either keep the optional-interface pattern or promote it to ports.GraphStorage if it's the canonical query path. Likely the latter — this is a basic read API.
acceptance_criteria: eng_get_file_nodes with a valid (repo_id, branch, file_path) against a promoted graph returns the nodes for that file. Test: file with 3 functions → eng_get_file_nodes returns 3 nodes with matching Names.
close_reason: ports.GraphStorage now has NodesForFile; sqlite.GraphRepo implements it via a simple SELECT; handler collapsed to staging-then-promoted check. Existing eng_get_file_nodes tests cover both paths.

title: post-commit hook hardcodes 'veska' — broken for any non-PATH install
description:
The post-commit hook installed by repo.Add (eng_add_repo) is literally:

  #!/bin/sh
  exec veska hook-runner post-commit "$@"

This shells out to 'veska' with no path — assuming the binary is on $PATH. For a developer running 'bin/veska' from a build tree, or any user who installs to a non-PATH location, every commit prints 'exec: veska: not found' and silently fails to drive promotion.

Reproduce: bin/veska init; eng_add_repo a repo; git commit something; observe '.git/hooks/post-commit: exec: veska: not found' AND the nodes table does not advance.

Fix: at repo.Add time, resolve os.Executable() (the binary that's installing the hook) and bake its absolute path into the hook template. Snippet:

  #!/bin/sh
  exec /path/to/veska hook-runner post-commit "$@"

Edge case: if the binary moves later (system upgrade to a new path), hooks point at the stale path. Acceptable for now — 'veska upgrade' could rewrite hooks; out of scope. Or have the hook look up the install via a marker file in ~/.veska/.
acceptance_criteria: A freshly-registered repo's post-commit hook drives a successful promotion (nodes table advances, last_promoted_sha changes) without 'veska' needing to be on the shell's $PATH.
