description: make build-fat install step prints 'Installed potion-code-16M to ... Restart the daemon to elect it as the embedder.' On a brand-new clone there is no daemon yet. Suppress that line on first-time setup, or rephrase to 'If a daemon is running, restart it'.
close_reason: duplicate of solov2-mel2

title: Version-pinned graph queries (--at <git-ref>)
description:
Allow eng_get_node / eng_find_symbol / eng_get_call_chain to return graph state as of a specific git ref, not just HEAD.

Motivation: context7 competitive analysis (2026-05-24). Context7's headline differentiator is 'version-specific' answers. Veska is always HEAD-only, which hurts PR review, bisect, and 'why did this change' workflows.

This is the most expensive item from the analysis — flagged for discussion, not immediate work. Open design questions:
- Snapshot strategy: re-parse at ref on demand vs. maintain historical graph
- Storage cost of historical edges/nodes
- Cache eviction policy
- Does this compose with blast radius? (probably yes, but needs thought)

Acceptance (when scoped):
- Design doc covering snapshot vs. on-demand reparse tradeoffs
- One end-to-end query path working at an arbitrary ref
- Cost/perf measurements before committing to broader rollout
notes: DEFERRED per solov2-71xq positioning decision (2026-05-26): version-pinned graph queries are context7's headline differentiator, not ours. Cost/value is poor for the multi-repo-graph wedge we picked. Keep at P3, no immediate work. Revisit only if an explicit consumer (PR-review-at-merge-base, bisect blame, 'why did this change between v1 and v2') becomes the user request — and even then prefer eng_find_changed_symbols + git tooling first.

title: Veska agent-skill wrapper for non-MCP harnesses
description:
Ship a thin skill/CLI wrapper analogous to context7's 'CLI + Skills' mode so agents on harnesses without MCP (or with restricted MCP) can still call into veska.

Motivation: context7 competitive analysis (2026-05-24). Context7 explicitly ships both MCP server and a Skill/CLI path. Veska's CLI already exists but isn't packaged as an agent-callable skill with a manifest. Low effort — mostly packaging + a SKILL.md describing when to invoke which subcommand.

Acceptance:
- skill manifest describing trigger conditions (e.g. 'when user asks about call chain / blast radius / symbol lookup in this repo')
- subcommand surface mirrors the most-used eng_* tools
- documented install path
notes: Related: solov2-zo0w (one-line install + MCP auto-config for popular agent harnesses). jtao covers the NON-MCP path (skill manifest + CLI surface) while zo0w covers the MCP-aware harness path (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, OpenCode). Together they form the full agent-onboarding story; do them in tandem so the SKILL.md and AGENTS.md snippets stay consistent in tone and tool-recommendation order. The CLI surface jtao wraps (veska search/symbol/context/findings/wiki) is also the same surface zo0w's AGENTS.md snippet points at.

title: Multi-repo Veska vs grep+read tokens-saved benchmark
description: Narrowed scope per user 2026-05-27 (full external-tool A/B vs Semble/Cursor/Sourcegraph deferred to a separate bead when access + install paths are agreed). Extend solov2-wise's tokenefficiency harness to LEAD with multi-repo queries: cross-repo callers, workspace-wide 'who imports this', cross-repo blast radius. Synth corpus already supports per-cluster files; extend to multiple repos (each cluster as its own repo, or N repos each with K clusters) so the simulated grep walks ALL repos' file trees. Pair every multi-repo savings number with multi-repo recall so the savings figure can't be gamed. Output: new chart-shaped JSON + summary line that lead with the multi-repo column; single-repo numbers (already produced by solov2-wise) become the appendix. Per solov2-71xq this is the wedge's headline number.
notes: Reframed per solov2-71xq (2026-05-26): the eval set must lead with MULTI-REPO queries (cross-repo callers, workspace-wide 'who imports this', cross-repo blast radius) so the headline numbers showcase the wedge, not feature-match-with-Semble on single-repo search. Single-repo numbers still go in the appendix, but the lead chart is the multi-repo column.
close_reason: Extended tools/loadtest/tokenefficiency/ with a multi-repo TestTokenEfficiencyMultiRepo that partitions the semantic synthcorpus across N synthetic repos (TOKEFF_REPOS, default 5) and measures: (a) veska cross-repo fanout — calls search.Service.SemanticCandidates per repo, then runs a SINGLE global RRF over the pooled cross-repo candidate set (mirrors the production MCP handler shipped in solov2-bcn); (b) grep+read across every repo's simulated filesystem (top-level prefix per repo). Same tokens-saved math, same recall@K anchor, new 'Across N repos, veska found ...' summary line. New make eval-token-efficiency-multirepo target; single-repo target hardened with a ^TestTokenEfficiency$ anchor so it doesn't accidentally pick up the multi-repo test. Honest caveats added to README: the synth corpus has zero cross-cluster phrase overlap, so grep matches one file per query regardless of repo count — savings ratios are conservative under this corpus. Recall can drop slightly multi-repo (per-repo retriever has less local context). External-tool A/B vs Semble / Cursor / Sourcegraph (the original kcmo scope) split out to solov2-vh7i pending access decisions. Reference run: 5 repos, 30 queries, recall=0.11, savings=[58%, 58%].

title: OSV refresher logs nothing on startup or completion — silent network egress
description: On daemon start with [vuln_source] provider="osv" configured, vulnrefresh kicks an immediate Refresh (and then every 24h) — observed by ~/.veska/cache/osv/ filling with 7,070 advisory JSON files. But ~/.veska/logs/daemon.log contains zero 'vuln'/'osv'/'advisory'/'refresh' entries. Two problems: (1) silent network egress to https://osv-vulnerabilities.storage.googleapis.com/Go/all.zip is exactly the thing a security-conscious junior wants to verify happened — log 'vulnrefresh: starting refresh', 'vulnrefresh: refresh complete advisories=N elapsed=...', and any error. (2) The refresher.go comment says 'A Refresh error is logged and swallowed' but I see no success log either, only error path. Add a slog.Info on successful refresh. Bonus: surface the cache age in 'veska doctor' (parallels doctor backup).
close_reason: README now clearly marks vuln-scan as opt-in and links to CONFIG-SURFACE.md; vulnrefresh now logs both refresh starting and complete (with elapsed_ms) so operators can verify the OSV.dev network egress.

title: eng_get_hot_zone returns empty zones even on a fully-promoted, 50-file repo
description: After a cold scan + reindex of /home/jrose/src/all-reglet/tack (50 files, status=promoted, eng_get_entry_points returns 20+ entries), calling eng_get_hot_zone with {limit:3} returns zones:[] and no degraded_reasons. The generated wiki page agrees: 'No hot zones yet — files appear here once commits land while the repo is registered with veska.' That copy is the actual behaviour — hot-zones is only populated by post-promotion churn, not by cold-scan history walk — but a junior reading the README expects 'mechanical wiki computed from the graph' to work immediately. Either (a) bootstrap from git log on registration (we already shell out to git for HEAD, blame, diff), or (b) surface a degraded_reasons:['hot_zones_need_commits'] entry so the empty result is self-explaining instead of looking broken.
close_reason: Already addressed by solov2-636y (commit 9d4d440 — fix(mcp,cli): consistency + cwd-resolution UX cluster). eng_get_hot_zone now appends degraded_reasons=['no_post_registration_commits'] when ranking returns empty zones, exactly per the bead's option (b). My junior-eng report observed a stale veska-mcp binary built before that commit landed. Pinned by TestHotZone_EmptyZonesSurfacesDegradedReason / TestHotZone_NonEmptyZonesNoDegradedReason. Option (a) — bootstrap from a wider git-log window — intentionally deferred: extending the window dilutes the 'hot' / recency signal that drives the score, and the existing reason makes the empty result self-explaining.

title: 'veska doctor status' says 'status: broken' when daemon is simply not running
description: Right after a fresh 'veska init' before the user has run 'veska service start', 'veska doctor status' returns: 'status: broken (embedder=healthy, egress=broken, config=healthy, ingestion=healthy, queue=degraded)'. A junior following Quick Start steps 2->5 (init, doctor) before step 3 (service start) sees 'broken' as the very first signal Veska gives them. The hint line does say 'daemon socket not present — start it with veska service start', but the top-line word should not be 'broken' for a 'daemon not started yet' state. Suggest: a distinct status 'not_running' / 'stopped' for the egress=broken-AND-no-socket case, vs. true 'broken' (socket exists but unreachable, crash-loop marker, etc.).
close_reason: doctor status no longer says 'broken' on a fresh init pre-service-start: now distinguishes a stopped daemon (no marker file, no crash) from a real broken state. Lead status reflects the actual rollup, egress shows 'stopped', exit 0 in the benign case (exit 2 reserved for real faults like a broken marker).

title: Cold scan stages go.mod/README/etc. as empty parse results — bloats staging and confuses checks
description: walkAndSave in coldscan.go (internal/application/coldscan.go:216-274) passes EVERY non-binary file in the repo to Ingester.Save. The parsers (treesitter/ts.go:28, treesitter/go.go:41) return an empty ParseResult for unsupported extensions, but Ingester.Save still calls staging.StageIfCurrentGenerationWithParseData regardless of result.Nodes being empty. Consequences: (a) staging accumulates rows for every README, go.mod, .yml, .json, image, etc. in the repo, all with nothing to promote; (b) the resulting PromotionFile.Path list is the union of ALL repo files, which is what made me think the vuln-scan touchesGoMod check was 'kind of' getting the right input — but only because we were spraying everything at it. Either (1) skip staging when ParseResult is empty AND the file isn't a known manifest (go.mod / package.json / etc.) we'll need later, or (2) keep the wide net but document that batch.Files is 'every repo file' not 'every parsed file', and rename it. Related to solov2-3tqb.
close_reason: Cold-scan walker now filters at the file level via isInterestingForStaging: only feeds the ingester source files the wired parsers handle (.go, .ts, .tsx) or known manifests downstream checks gate on (currently go.mod for vuln-scan). README/.yml/.json/etc. are skipped, so staging is no longer the wide 'every non-binary file' set. Manifest list is centralized in coldscan.go and easy to extend when a new scanner arrives (package.json, Cargo.toml, etc.). Regression test TestColdScanReparser_SkipsNonSourceNonManifest added.

title: eng_get_status: large pending_embeds but degraded_reasons=[] — contradictory
description: eng_get_status returned {pending_embeds: 4699, repo_count: 5, status: 'ok', degraded_reasons: []} simultaneously. A 4.7k embed backlog is exactly the condition that should land in degraded_reasons (and that the search tool already surfaces as [degraded: embeddings_pending] inline). Either: (a) add a 'embeddings_pending' reason when the queue is non-trivial (>some threshold), or (b) ensure the status surfaces from the same code path as the search degradation so the two never disagree. Currently a junior sees 'status: ok' from eng_get_status, then 'degraded' from search — undermines trust.
close_reason: statusProvider.Status now flips rollup status to 'degraded' and appends 'embeddings_pending' to degraded_reasons when pendingEmbeds > 0, matching the signal eng_search_semantic already emits for the same backlog. The two surfaces no longer disagree (junior would see status: ok + [degraded: embeddings_pending] simultaneously). Added regression test TestStatusProvider_HealthyWhenNoPending and updated TestStatusProvider_ReportsDBState which had pinned the buggy behaviour.

title: eng_promote_repo requires root_path while every other tool accepts repo_id/short_id
description: Every read tool plus eng_remove_repo accepts {repo_id} (full or 12-char short_id). eng_promote_repo uniquely requires {root_path}, forcing the caller to look up the path from eng_list_repos before promoting. This is the lone exception in the repo-lifecycle family. Accept repo_id/short_id as a peer of root_path; resolve to root via the registry.
close_reason: Closed

title: duplicate TODO findings logged with mismatched actor_kind (agent:unknown vs service:veska)
description: eng_list_findings on reglet shows three structural/todo findings created within ~500ms of each other; one has actor_id='agent:unknown', actor_kind='agent', the next has actor_id='service:veska', actor_kind='system'. These are emitted by the same cold-scan promotion path. The 'who created this' attribution should be deterministic for system-generated checks — agent:unknown shouldn't surface for findings the daemon emitted itself.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_list_findings and eng_find_todos disagree on file_path representation (relative vs absolute)
description: For the same TODO finding, eng_list_findings returns file_path='internal/infrastructure/container/container.go' (relative to repo root) while eng_find_todos returns file_path='/home/jrose/src/reglet/internal/infrastructure/container/container.go' (absolute). README states a single canonical node shape with file_path. Pick one — absolute is consistent with eng_find_symbol/eng_get_node — and update all finding-shaped responses to match.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_get_file_nodes demands 'branch' while peer query tools default it
description: eng_get_file_nodes returns: 'file_path (or path), repo_id, and branch are required'. Every other query tool (find_symbol, get_node, get_blast_radius, find_todos) defaults branch to the repo's active branch when omitted. Either eng_get_file_nodes should follow the same default, or the README's 'branch defaults to active' claim needs an explicit exception.
close_reason: Closed

title: semantic search score scale doesn't match documented 1/(1+L2dist) formula
description: README says similarity = 1/(1+L2dist) for L2-normalised vectors. For unit vectors L2 distance is bounded by 2, so scores should land in roughly [0.33, 1.0]. In practice eng_search_semantic returns top scores of ~0.018 for reasonable queries (e.g. 'register cobra subcommand' against a cobra-rooted repo). Either the formula is wrong, vectors aren't being normalised at query time, or the cosine path is producing dot products of nearly-orthogonal vectors. Junior users will read 0.018 as 'no good match'.
close_reason: Closed

title: repo list status inconsistent between CLI and MCP
description: 'veska repo list' marks /tmp/big-Au8c as STATUS='(missing)' because the directory is gone, but the MCP tool eng_list_repos returns the same repo with status='promoted'. Both surfaces should agree — either both detect the missing root and both surface 'missing', or the CLI suppresses the check. Today an agent reading from MCP has no way to know the repo's root has vanished.
close_reason: Closed

title: LSP adapter over the daemon: expose veska to IDEs that don't speak MCP
description:
Today veska serves an editor only via MCP (veska-mcp stdio shim → daemon's mcp.sock). That covers MCP-aware editors (Cursor, Zed, Claude Desktop, Continue, Copilot-via-bridge), but leaves the bulk of the IDE market — JetBrains, VS Code without a bridge, Neovim with built-in LSP, Helix, etc. — out. The veska graph already has the structural data those IDEs want (nodes, CONTAINS/CALLS/IMPORTS edges, ranges); what's missing is an LSP server in front of it.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SCOPE OF THIS BEAD
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Sketch the LSP surface — which methods map cleanly, which need new data,
which are out of scope — and the wiring shape (new binary vs. mode of an
existing one; how it shares the daemon's SQLite). Implementation is a
follow-up after the sketch is reviewed.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
LSP METHODS — FIRST-PASS MAPPING
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Clean fits (graph already has the data):
  textDocument/definition       — node.symbol_path + LineRange → Location
  textDocument/references       — EdgeReader.InboundEdges (CALLS kind) on
                                  the target node_id; project edge sources
                                  back to (file_path, line_start)
  textDocument/documentSymbol   — NodeLookup.NodesInFile → SymbolInformation[]
                                  (kind mapping function/method/struct/...
                                  → SymbolKind already 1:1)
  workspace/symbol              — eng_find_symbol → SymbolInformation[]
  textDocument/hover            — node.signature + node.raw_content[:N]
                                  formatted as MarkupContent
  textDocument/implementation   — for interfaces: scan structs whose method
                                  set covers the interface (NEW data — not
                                  in the graph today; could be a derived
                                  view or precomputed at promotion)
  textDocument/callHierarchy/*  — eng_get_call_chain already does BFS over
                                  CALLS; map prepareCallHierarchy → seed
                                  node, incoming/outgoing → InboundEdges /
                                  OutgoingEdges
  textDocument/typeDefinition   — needs a TYPE_OF edge (NEW data) or fallback
                                  to find_symbol on the signature's return
                                  type

Needs new extraction or a tighter parser pass:
  textDocument/completion       — agent-shaped semantic search is not what
                                  IDEs expect here; would need scope-aware
                                  identifier completion which veska does NOT
                                  index today. EXCLUDE from v1.
  textDocument/signatureHelp    — needs param-position parsing; tree-sitter
                                  has the data, no extraction pass writes it.
                                  Defer.
  textDocument/rename           — needs definite-cross-file usage edges
                                  (today CALLS is the only cross-file edge;
                                  refactor would mis-rename ambiguous
                                  identifiers). Defer.

Probably out of scope (compiler-grade analysis):
  textDocument/diagnostics      — overlaps with promotion-time findings,
                                  but live syntax/type errors belong to
                                  the language's own LSP (gopls, tsserver).
                                  Possible LATER: surface promotion
                                  Findings as Information diagnostics
                                  ('vulnerable dep', 'leaked secret',
                                  'dead code') — distinct from compile
                                  errors and complementary, not competing.
  textDocument/codeAction       — refactors out of scope; quick-fix for
                                  promotion Findings could land later.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
WIRING — TWO PLAUSIBLE SHAPES
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
A. New binary 'veska-lsp' that talks JSON-RPC over stdio (LSP transport)
   and opens the daemon's mcp.sock for tool calls. Mirrors veska-mcp's
   model: thin shim, daemon does the work, no second SQLite connection
   to coordinate. EASIEST. Likely 500-1000 LoC.

B. Mode flag on veska-daemon ('--lsp-stdio') that runs an LSP server
   inline in the daemon process. Avoids the extra round-trip but couples
   the daemon's lifecycle to a single editor session, breaking the
   one-daemon/many-editors story. NOT RECOMMENDED.

Lean: A. Same hexagonal shape as today (CLI / MCP shim / daemon).

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
PROTOCOL NOTES — WHERE THIS DEPARTS FROM AN LSP-NATIVE SERVER
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
- No didChange streaming. veska only sees files at commit / staging
  boundaries; it can't answer 'definition' on lines the editor just typed.
  Document this loudly: 'veska-lsp answers against your last save +
  staging snapshot; for live-edit answers, run alongside gopls/tsserver
  not instead of'.
- Single-language gopls-style assumption doesn't apply: veska indexes Go
  + TS today (parser_pool), so the LSP can answer for both in one
  process. Editors usually expect one LSP per language; some support
  multiple. Could ship as 'veska-lsp' that registers for go + ts.
- selectionRange / foldingRange could be derived from the graph's
  LineRange data essentially for free — low-effort add.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
COEXISTENCE WITH MCP
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Both servers are read-only against the same SQLite (the daemon's
pools.ReadDB). No write coordination needed. An editor could even run
both: MCP for the agent panel ('explain this' / semantic search) and
LSP for go-to-def / find-references in the gutter. They're complementary
surfaces over one graph.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
ACCEPTANCE FOR THE SKETCH
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
- Design doc under docs/design/ enumerating the v1 method set with the
  graph data each one reads.
- Decision on wiring shape (A vs B).
- Identification of any new edges/columns required (TYPE_OF? interface
  implementation index?).
- A short note on liveness limits (last-save freshness) to set editor-
  user expectations.

Out of scope for this bead: writing the LSP server itself, picking
client wiring for any specific IDE.
notes: DEFERRED 2026-05-26 by user direction — LSP adapter is non-MCP IDE reach and pulls focus from the Go cleanup pass. The D-element of the solov2-71xq wedge ('graph behind your tools') is served today via MCP; LSP unblocks IDE users who can't bridge to MCP, but that audience is secondary to the agent-first wedge. Revisit when MCP coverage of agent harnesses is broader and JetBrains/Neovim users specifically request it.

title: veska-mcp shim should forward cwd to eng_get_current_repo
description:
Today eng_get_current_repo requires an explicit cwd param. The veska-mcp stdio shim runs in the editor's working directory but doesn't inject cwd into params for tools that accept it. An MCP client (editor) that doesn't know to pass cwd hits 'cwd is required' even when the user is plainly inside a registered repo.

Fix: in the shim transport, when forwarding to eng_get_current_repo (and any other tool that accepts cwd as optional), inject os.Getwd() into params if not present. Don't override an explicit cwd from the client.
acceptance_criteria: 1) printf '{...eng_get_current_repo...params:{}}' | veska-mcp from inside a registered repo returns the repo. 2) Explicit cwd in params still wins. 3) Test in cmd/veska-mcp.
close_reason: Closed

title: Symbol/context CLI wrappers: 'veska symbol', 'veska context'
description: Today only 'veska search' wraps an MCP tool. README pitches 'skip the editor and drive veska-mcp directly' as a value prop but the CLI exposes ~3 of the 31 tools. Add at minimum 'veska symbol <name>' (eng_find_symbol) and 'veska context <symbol>' (eng_get_context_pack) for parity with what an agent would do.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Two new subcommands wired with --json flags. 2) Auto-resolve repo from cwd when registered.
close_reason: Closed

title: Daemon log spam: ERROR-per-boot for missing repo roots
description: Every daemon start emits ERROR 'startup resync: HEAD for repo ... No such file or directory' for each known-missing repo. Should be a single WARN per repo per boot with a '--repair' hint. See cmd/veska-daemon/wire.go or wherever startup resync lives.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Missing-root entries log WARN, not ERROR, on boot. 2) Message points at veska repo prune or repo remove.
close_reason: Closed

title: Findings CLI: 'veska findings list|show|close|reopen|suppress'
description: eng_list_findings / eng_get_finding / eng_close_finding / eng_reopen_finding and the suppressions family are reachable only via raw JSON-RPC. Add a cobra subcommand tree under 'veska findings' mirroring the 'veska repo' pattern so users can interact with promotion-check output without an editor.
acceptance_criteria: 1) veska findings list/show/close/reopen wired. 2) veska suppressions list/show/add/close wired. 3) Tests in cmd/veska.
close_reason: Closed

title: MCP pytest suite: add positive coverage for session 2026-05-23 changes
description:
The Go unit tests cover each of the recent MCP UX fixes at the handler level. The MCP pytest suite — which exercises the daemon's real JSON wire shape over a real socket — has zero coverage for any of them. Add positive cases under tests/mcp/.

Behaviours to pin (one test each, ideally):

1. eng_add_repo idempotency (solov2-khjd): second add against the same root_path returns already_registered=true, scan_pending=false. Test should add, then re-add, then remove.

2. eng_get_node without repo_id/branch (solov2-v4ob): call with only node_id (looked up from a prior find_symbol) and assert the same node comes back.

3. eng_get_call_chain accepts symbol (solov2-lcz6): call with {symbol: target_symbol, repo_id, branch} and assert nodes are returned. Plus an ambiguity-rejection case.

4. branch defaulting (solov2-5vu1): call eng_find_symbol with repo_id but no branch; assert it succeeds and returns same nodes as the explicit-branch call.

5. repo_id 4-char unambiguous prefix (solov2-rkbc): take the first 4-8 chars of the fixture repo_id, pass as repo_id, assert resolves correctly. Plus a multi-repo ambiguous-prefix rejection (skip if only one repo registered).

6. eng_list_suppressions with single repo (solov2-7tz1): call with no repo_id; assert ok=true. Skip if more than one repo registered.

7. eng_find_changed_symbols JSON shape (solov2-jbgt): call HEAD~1..HEAD (or HEAD..HEAD); assert each of added/removed/modified is a list (never null) in the parsed JSON.

8. eng_find_changed_symbols absolute file_path (solov2-w8nr): assert every entry's file_path starts with the repo's root_path (absolute).

9. Cobra var-decl extraction (solov2-b7wt): if the fixture repo or a freshly-added cobra-style repo contains 'var rootCmd = &cobra.Command{...}', assert eng_find_symbol returns a kind='variable' node. Possibly add a small Go fixture under tests/mcp/fixtures/cobra/ that the test bootstraps via eng_add_repo + waits for promotion.

Acceptance: each bullet has a green test; suite still runs cleanly under the existing make test-mcp / test-mcp-deep targets.
close_reason: Closed

title: Tokens-saved vs grep+read benchmark (semble-style), paired with recall
description:
Produce a defensible "tokens saved vs grep+read" figure for veska search, comparable in shape to semble's token-efficiency claim ("~98% fewer tokens than grep+read"). Today `internal/savings` only reports `1 - snippet_chars/file_chars` per live call — useful telemetry, but not a benchmark: no tokenizer, no baseline, no recall anchor, so the number can't be quoted with confidence.

Scope: new harness under `tools/loadtest/tokenefficiency/` behind the `eval` build tag. Distinct from `internal/savings`; do not conflate the two in user-facing output.

Approach (path of least resistance):
- Tokenizer: add `tiktoken-go` (cl100k_base) — single new dep, used only under the eval tag.
- Baseline: simulate `rg + read` over the same query set. Run two modes to bracket realistic agent behavior:
  - stop-when-covered (lower bound on savings — assumes agent reads rg hits in rank order and stops the moment the ground-truth target is covered)
  - read-all-matches (upper bound — assumes agent reads every file rg returned)
- Reuse `tools/loadtest/recall/` fixtures + search adapter. Acknowledge in output that fixtures are auto-generated from the indexed graph (biases recall up vs human-annotated corpora like semble's `benchmarks/annotations/`). Optional follow-up: import semble's MIT-licensed annotations for overlapping repos.
- Pair every savings number with the recall it was measured at. Savings without recall is gameable (return nothing → 100% saved, 0% recall).

Reporting:
- Eval log: machine-readable JSON with recall@K, tokens (snippet vs baseline-lo vs baseline-hi), per-query and aggregate.
- Human-readable summary line (for docs / `veska doctor` --verbose), shape:
  "Veska found the right code ~X% of the time, using about half as many tokens as grep+read would have (range: A–B% fewer, depending on how aggressively the agent reads grep matches; measured on N queries)."
- Keep `internal/savings` live JSONL untouched; it stays as ops telemetry, not a marketing number.

Acceptance criteria:
- `make eval-token-efficiency` (or similar) runs end-to-end on the existing recall fixtures and emits both JSON and a human summary.
- Output includes recall@10, tokens for veska, tokens for stop-when-covered baseline, tokens for read-all-matches baseline, and the resulting savings range.
- README/docs updated to explain methodology and caveats (auto-generated fixtures, baseline assumptions).
- `internal/savings` output is unchanged; no headline conflates live ratio with benchmark figure.

Out of scope:
- Hand-annotating queries.
- Replacing or rewriting `internal/savings`.
- Reporting the benchmark number in live MCP responses.
notes:
Relationship to internal/savings (deliberate, one-way):

- Headline numbers stay separate. `veska doctor savings` keeps reporting the live char-ratio as-is; the benchmark number lives in eval output / docs / a --verbose path. Do not merge them into a single figure.
- Useful interaction 1 (calibration, optional follow-up): benchmark run produces a mapping from char-ratio → estimated token-savings-vs-grep at a stated recall. `doctor savings` can then show a softer derived line labeled with the benchmark date, so users get a tokens-flavored estimate without per-search tokenization.
- Useful interaction 2 (corpus seeding, optional follow-up): sample real `query` strings from the live JSONL into future benchmark fixtures (with PII review) so the corpus reflects actual workload. One-way only.
- Explicit non-goals for internal/savings under this bead: do not add tiktoken to the live path; do not simulate rg per live query; do not reshape the Entry schema (the JSONL aggregator must stay forward-compatible).
close_reason: New harness tools/loadtest/tokenefficiency/ (eval build tag) reuses the deterministic semantic synthcorpus (queries that grep can match by construction) and tokenises with cl100k_base via pkoukk/tiktoken-go. Per-query output captures veska's recall@10 + snippet tokens vs two bracketed grep+read baselines: stop-when-covered (lower bound — agent reads grep matches in sorted order, stops at first truth coverage) and read-all-matches (upper bound). Emits tools/loadtest/tokenefficiency/results.json + a semble-shaped human summary on stdout. Hooked into 'make eval-token-efficiency' with a TOKEFF_NODES_PER_CLUSTER knob to widen the bracket. Unit tests cover the tokenizer, grep simulator, baseline math, recall@K, and the summary-line shape; the eval-tagged TestTokenEfficiency exercises the full pipeline (~0.4s). Distinct from internal/savings per the bead's explicit one-way rule — savings stays live char-ratio telemetry, this stays the marketing-quality benchmark. README documents methodology + honest caveats (synthetic ground truth biases recall up, fake embedder gives low absolute recall, simulated rg vs real rg differences absorbed by the bracket). Reference run on the default knob: recall=0.17, savings=[58%, 58%], 30 queries.

title: Parked task tools (eng_get_active_task etc.) listed in README without 'not yet wired' note
description: README's tool families list includes references in the prose to eng_get_active_task / eng_set_active_task / eng_get_task_history with a paragraph explaining they're parked. But a reader who scans the family table and tries them gets a confusing 'method not found' error. Either move them to a clearly-marked 'not yet available' sub-table or remove until solov2-6m1 lands.
close_reason: Closed

title: Agent CLAUDE.md snippet hard-codes repo_id placeholder; doesn't mention cwd auto-resolution
description: Generated CLAUDE.md from `veska init --agent claude` shows examples like {query: "parse config", repo_id: "<id>", branch: "main"}. Recent commits added cwd-based auto-resolution via eng_get_current_repo / single-repo defaulting. The snippet should teach agents about that path so they stop pestering users for repo_id. Apply the same update to the snippets for all supported agents (codex, copilot, cursor, gemini, kiro, opencode).
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_list_suppressions requires repo_id even with a single registered repo
description: With exactly one registered repo, eng_list_suppressions errors 'repo_id is required'. Other tools auto-resolve from cwd / single-repo defaulting (eng_get_current_repo). Apply the same resolution to list_suppressions (and audit list_findings / similar for the same gap).
close_reason: Closed

title: README quick-start should default to `make build-fat` for end users
description: The README says fat is recommended for end users, but the Build section presents `make build` first and `make all` (which a user is likely to run) builds thin. A fresh user following the README ends up on static-v2 unless they read carefully. Reorder the Build section so build-fat is the default path; keep thin under 'CI / size-sensitive'. Optionally make `make all` produce a fat binary (or warn loudly when it doesn't).
close_reason: Closed

title: Static-v2 fallback warning under-promoted; only surfaces in per-search degraded_reasons
description: `veska init` mentions 'tip: run veska install model2vec' on one line, easily missed. Every eng_search_semantic call thereafter returns 'low_quality_static_embedder' in degraded_reasons until the user acts. doctor status reports 'embedder=healthy' even on static-v2. Promote the warning: (a) doctor status should call static-v2 'degraded' not 'healthy'; (b) emit a WARN log on daemon boot when elected embedder is static-v2; (c) consider making `make build-fat` the documented default in the README quick-start.
close_reason: Closed

title: Promotion runs produce no INFO log line; only cold scans visible in daemon.log
description: After a commit (post-commit hook fires, eng_promote_repo runs, find_symbol shows the new symbol), daemon.log has no corresponding line. Only 'cold scan: starting' / 'cold scan: complete' appear. Users tailing the log to confirm 'did my last commit get picked up?' see nothing. Add a 'promotion: starting' / 'promotion: complete' INFO pair with repo_id + git_sha + elapsed_ms, matching the cold-scan shape.
close_reason: Closed

title: repo add log-tail hint hard-codes ~/.veska/logs path; wrong under custom VESKA_HOME
description: After `veska repo add`, output says: 'cold scan running in the background — ... `tail ~/.veska/logs/daemon.log` shows progress'. With a custom VESKA_HOME, that path is wrong. Interpolate $VESKA_HOME/logs/daemon.log into the message (or, better, expand to the actual filesystem path).
close_reason: Closed

title: `veska repo add` on already-registered repo reports success instead of 'already registered'
description: Re-running `veska repo add /tmp/myrepo1` prints 'added repo 6754f6c9957f (via daemon)' and the cold-scan hint, identical to a first add. Misleading idempotency message — user can't tell whether their command actually did anything. Should detect existing registration and print 'repo already registered (id=...)'.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_get_call_chain rejects symbol param; parity with eng_find_symbol expected
description: eng_find_symbol takes 'symbol'; eng_get_call_chain takes only 'node_id' and returns 'node_id is required' if you try 'symbol'. README is silent on this. Users coming from find_symbol expect parity. Either accept 'symbol' (resolve internally to node_id) or document the two-step explicitly in the README and in the tool description.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_get_node requires repo_id+branch despite content-hashed node_id
description: node_id is a sha256 content hash, globally unique by construction. eng_get_node currently errors with 'missing required params: repo_id, branch' if you only pass node_id. Should be a single-arg lookup. Especially painful because the typical caller flow is eng_find_symbol -> grab node_id -> eng_get_node; the user has the node_id and shouldn't need to also schlep repo_id+branch.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska init --agent should write/extend .gitignore for docs/veska/ + claude artifacts
description: `veska init --agent claude` writes CLAUDE.md but does not add anything to .gitignore. Combined with the wiki render (solov2-ocnn) writing `docs/veska/*.md` into the working tree, a junior who runs the documented quickstart then `git add .` will commit veska-generated artifacts. Add a `.gitignore` stanza managed in the same `<!-- veska:init -->` bracket pattern so re-running is idempotent. Stanza should at minimum cover `docs/veska/`.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska repo list columns mis-align when status contains '(unindexed)'
description:
Output:
  REPO_ID         BRANCH    STATUS      ROOT
  1cb168672af0    main      promoted    /tmp/my-cli-app
  e9671acd2448    main      (unindexed)  /tmp
  fe3e59848eeb    main      promoted    /tmp/jr-veska/mycli

The '(unindexed)' value is wider than 'promoted' but only one extra space is reserved, so ROOT slides right by one column on rows with unindexed status. Use `text/tabwriter` or compute column widths from actual values.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska search scores are uninterpretable (~0.018) — no normalization or threshold guidance
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_get_current_repo error message hints at a CLI integration that doesn't exist
description: `eng_get_current_repo` with no params returns: `-32602 cwd is required (an absolute path, e.g. {"cwd": "/abs/path/to/checkout"})`. The tool's name implies it knows the 'current' repo, but the daemon has no cwd of its own — the CLI must inject one. Today, no CLI command appears to inject `cwd` automatically for editor MCP clients either.\n\nProposed: (a) rename to `eng_resolve_repo_from_cwd` so the name matches the contract, or (b) have `veska-mcp` (the stdio shim) pass the editor's project root as `cwd` by default so an editor can call zero-arg `eng_get_current_repo` and get the right answer.
close_reason: Closed

title: doctor status exits 1 on informational 'degraded' — breaks CI/script use
description: `veska doctor status` returns exit code 1 when overall status is `degraded` (e.g. one unindexed repo). CI scripts and shell pipelines treat exit≠0 as failure, so users either babysit a `|| true` or get spurious red builds. Suggest: exit 0 for healthy/degraded, exit 1 only for hard outage (daemon down, store corrupted), and add `--fail-on=degraded|outage` flag for users who do want strict gating. Also stop printing the message three times (see solov2-ikn4).
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_find_owner reports 'no author' on files with valid git blame
description: On a repo with a single commit by author `jr <jr@x>`, `eng_find_owner` for `main.go` returns `{owner:null, reason:'no CODEOWNERS file in repo root or .github/; git blame also yielded no author'}`. git blame on this file does in fact attribute every line to `jr`. Likely the blame parser is rejecting valid output (e.g. requiring an `@` in the author field, or only counting authors who match a configured email domain). Investigate and fix the fallback path.
close_reason: Closed

title: chunk:N-M internal artifacts leak into user-facing tool output
description: The chunker exposes synthetic nodes named `chunk:1-22` (kind=`chunk`) in user-facing responses from `eng_search_semantic`, `eng_get_file_nodes`, `eng_get_context_pack`, `eng_search_similar`, and the autolink finding message ('Similar to chunk:1-22 in main.go').\n\nThis is a leaky internal abstraction. Either: (a) filter out chunk nodes by default and add an opt-in `include_chunks: true` param; (b) rename them to a user-meaningful label like `<file>:1-22`; (c) at minimum, hide them in CLI tabular output (`veska search` lists them inline with real symbols).
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_list_findings / eng_list_suppressions require 'branch' though README implies repo-level
description:
The README's tool surface table groups findings/suppressions as repo-scoped operations. In practice both `eng_list_findings` and `eng_list_suppressions` return `-32602 branch is required` when called with only `repo_id`. Two paths to fix:

(a) Make branch optional and default to the repo's `active_branch` from `eng_list_repos` (preferred — matches how callers naturally think about a repo).
(b) If branch really is required, update the README to say so and surface it in error messages for ALL findings/suppressions calls together.
close_reason: Closed

title: document veska init --agent (CLAUDE.md / cursor / codex snippet writer) in README
description: 'veska init --agent claude|codex|copilot|cursor|gemini|kiro|opencode' writes/appends per-agent instructions to the project — a great onboarding affordance that the README does not mention. Add a short subsection under 'Editor integration' or 'Quick start' covering supported agents and that it safely appends to existing files.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska-mcp / CLI errors when daemon is down don't tell user to start it
description: If the daemon isn't running, veska-mcp and several veska CLI commands emit raw socket-connection errors with no hint that the fix is 'veska service start' (or that the daemon was never started). Detect ECONNREFUSED on the MCP/CLI socket and print a friendly hint.
close_reason: Closed

title: no veska --version / veska version command
description: Standard CLI expectation. Today there is no way to print the binary version. Add 'veska version' (build SHA, version, embedder build mode if fat).
close_reason: Closed

title: README/wiki section implies on-disk wiki pages that don't exist
description: README pitches a 'Mechanical wiki' with hot-zone and entry-point pages, and 'veska wiki' regenerates… nothing browsable. The wiki only materializes through eng_get_hot_zone / eng_get_entry_points MCP calls. Either clarify in README ('wiki = two MCP tools, no files on disk') or add 'veska wiki show' to dump rendered pages.
close_reason: Closed

title: veska savings displays misleading -1.5% all-time on tiny samples
description: With 6 calls and ~2.4KB seen, savings shows -1.5% (a regression). Alarming for first-time users. Either don't render a negative number until a minimum sample size, show 'warming up (N/M calls)' below threshold, or explain why a small sample can be negative.
close_reason: Closed

title: eng_get_context_pack recent_commits not scoped to seed node's file
description: context_pack of symbol Greet includes recent_commits that did not touch the file containing Greet (e.g. the initial commit predating Greet's existence). recent_commits should be filtered to commits touching the seed node's file path (or, ideally, lines).
close_reason: Not reproducible — re-reading the original transcript, the seed symbol Greet was in main.go and the initial commit DID touch main.go (Greet was added in that commit, not later). recent_commits is already scoped to the file set of relevant nodes (contextpack.go:337-360). False positive in the initial bug report.

title: veska init / doctor should show embedder provenance (fat vs downloaded vs static-v2)
description: 'veska init' prints 'embedder: model2vec(potion-code-16M) (in-process)' but doesn't reveal whether the model came from a fat binary, ~/.veska download, or the static-v2 fallback. First-time users can't tell if they should run 'veska install model2vec' or not. Suggest appending source: model2vec(potion-code-16M, fat) / (downloaded) / (fallback static-v2).
close_reason: Closed

title: Bench dual-model fusion (concat + RRF) of potion-code-16M & potion-base-32M
description:
Test summary-augmented embedding via dual-model fusion, NO extractive condensation. Two shapes:

(1) CONCAT — embed each doc twice; store [code_vec || base_vec] L2-normalised; rank by concat-cosine (mathematically equivalent to mean of the two cosines).

(2) RRF — embed each doc twice; at query time, rank ALL docs in each model's vector space independently; fuse with reciprocal rank fusion (score = sum 1/(60 + rank_in_each_list)).

Baselines: code-only (potion-code-16M alone) and base-only (potion-base-32M alone) — the existing data points from oo4q.2's full sweep.

Hypothesis: the two model2vec variants specialise differently (code-16M tuned for code identifiers, base-32M tuned for natural prose); fusing them should beat either alone on heterogeneous corpora.

Cost (if shipped): 2x per-doc storage; 2x query embeds (negligible for model2vec, ~0.1ms total); 2x vector searches at query time. For our existing storage layer, doubling the per-node embedding bytes is bounded and acceptable.

Why this is cheap to test: the bench already has all the corpora + ground-truth pairs. New work is purely the fusion-recall pipeline — no new embedder integrations, no new corpora.
acceptance_criteria: Bench reports per-corpus fair-R@10 for: code-only, base-only, concat, RRF. Published table includes a fusion section. Decision criterion: fused approach wins (>+5% absolute over the BEST single-model baseline) on at least 2 corpora AND does not regress on others. If criterion met, file a follow-up bead for production wire-up (mirrors hi5's storage shape but uses both buckets per-doc instead of routing). If not met, close with the data.
notes:
Verdict: fusion is NOT a single-shot replacement for the right model, but the data strongly argues for routing.

Per-corpus headline fair-R@10 (full bench, max_docs=5000):

  Corpus           code-only  prose-only  concat   RRF      Best fuse vs best single
  veska (code)     0.706      0.353       0.588    0.588    -0.118  REGRESSES
  veska-docs       0.267      0.467       0.333    0.333    -0.134  REGRESSES
  cobra-docs       0.700      0.700       0.800    0.900    +0.200  RRF wins
  wikipedia-tech   0.850      0.850       0.850    0.900    +0.050  RRF wins

Pattern: RRF wins where the two models AGREE on the baseline (both 0.700, both 0.850). RRF loses where one model is the clear specialist (veska code: 0.706 vs 0.353; veska-docs: 0.467 vs 0.267).

Conclusion: fusion is the wrong primitive when there is a clear specialist. The CORRECT primitive is routing — i.e. hi5. Reopening hi5 with this data as supporting evidence. Bench infrastructure (TestEmbedModelsFusion + the fusion section in embedder-benchmarks.md) stays in tree for future re-evaluation if a same-bucket fusion (two code models or two prose models) inquiry opens.

Caveats on the data: cobra-docs n=10 and wikipedia-tech n=20 are small samples. The directional pattern is consistent but the +0.20 / +0.05 magnitudes may not survive a larger fixture set. The losses (veska code, veska-docs) are based on slightly larger N (15-20) and align with the routing-beats-fusion hypothesis.
close_reason: Bench landed. Verdict: fusion regresses on 2/4 corpora vs the right single model; wins only when models agree on the baseline. The data argues strongly for hi5 routing (un-deferring) rather than fusion. See bead notes for the per-corpus table + hypothesis.

title: oo4q.3 Production wire-up of extractive condensation (only if oo4q.2 shows lift > 5%)
description:
BLOCKED on oo4q.2's lift data. Wire condense.Condense() into the embedder pipeline behind an env flag so it can be enabled / disabled in production without rebuild.

Wiring:
- Pre-condense at promote time, NOT at search time: the query stays a single short text that the model embeds directly.
- Schema: new node_summaries table content-addressed by content_hash (mirrors the node_embeddings shape so unchanged content never re-condenses). Schema: content_hash TEXT PRIMARY KEY, condensed_text TEXT NOT NULL, k INTEGER NOT NULL, created_at INTEGER NOT NULL.
- Migration: 0012_node_summaries.sql.
- Embedder driver: when condensation is enabled and the node's raw_content length > min threshold, condense first, embed the result; cache the condensed text by content_hash.
- Env: VESKA_CONDENSE_EMBEDS=on (default off until validated for several weeks in production).
- Reindex implications: switching condensation on/off invalidates all existing embeddings (different vectors). Treat the toggle like an embedder switch — same fz8 path triggers a reindex.

Verify post-wire-up:
- Production recall lift on a live veska repo via eng_search_semantic.
- No latency regression on the search hot path.
- No regression in disk footprint expectations (a 500-line function condenses to ~50 lines).
acceptance_criteria: VESKA_CONDENSE_EMBEDS=on path exists, behind a default-off flag. node_summaries table + migration. Existing fz8 reindex path triggers on toggle. Bench can be re-run with the production code path matching the harness. No production code path skipped; the toggle is real, not a stub.
close_reason: Not pursued. oo4q.2 bench data showed condensation NET REGRESSES on 15/24 cells with k=5. Production wire-up would have hurt search quality. See oo4q.2 notes for the data + the hypothesis (k too aggressive for our doc lengths; cosine-centroid picks the most-average not most-topical line; stronger models lose more context). The bench is in tree for future re-evaluation if a Tier 2/3 (LLM-based summary) path is ever pursued.

title: oo4q.2 Bench the condensation lift in the embed-models harness
description:
Extend tools/loadtest/embed_models/ to compare RAW vs CONDENSED embedding inputs across all (model x corpus) combinations and report the lift per cell. No production change; bench-only.

Mechanics:
- New env knob EMBED_BENCH_CONDENSE=on/off (default off; on enables a SECOND series of runs with the condensation pre-processor applied).
- runResult gains 'condensed_recall' alongside the existing recall map.
- Markdown table adds a 'Lift' column showing condensed_R@10 - raw_R@10 per model.
- Hyperparameter knob EMBED_BENCH_CONDENSE_K (default 5) so we can sweep k = 3/5/7 cheaply.
- Gating: only condense bodies > some min-length (env: EMBED_BENCH_CONDENSE_MIN_LEN, default 500 chars). Short symbols don't need it.

Decision criteria for proceeding to oo4q.3:
- Lift > 5% R@10 (absolute) on the headline set, averaged across corpora, for at least one model+k combination.
- Lift survives the wikipedia-tech corpus (no degradation on genuine prose).
- Per-promote cost remains microsecond-scale per node.
acceptance_criteria: Bench reports per-model condensation lift; published table updated with a Lift column or a separate condensation-comparison file; data-backed go/no-go recorded as a decision in oo4q.3 (proceed) or in this bead (skip).
notes:
Per the predetermined acceptance criterion (lift > 5% R@10 on the headline set, averaged across corpora, for at least one model+k combo), condensation FAILS to justify oo4q.3 production wiring.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
RESULTS (k=5, min_len=500, full model2vec sweep, 8 corpora):
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Per-model averaged fair-R@10 lift (condensed - raw):

  Model                  Code lift  Prose lift
  potion-base-2M         -0.118     -0.067
  potion-base-4M         +0.059     -0.072
  potion-base-8M         +0.059     -0.028
  potion-code-16M        -0.059     -0.061    <- current production winner regresses
  potion-base-32M        -0.059     -0.156    <- prose winner regresses badly
  potion-retrieval-32M   +0.000     +0.022

Across 24 (model x corpus) cells: 5 positive, 4 tied, 15 NEGATIVE.
Max positive cell: +0.100 (potion-base-8M cobra-docs).
Max negative cell: -0.267 (potion-base-32M veska-docs).

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
WHY (best current hypothesis):
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

1. k=5 is too aggressive for the doc lengths in our corpora. veska-docs
   sections average ~10-15 lines; keeping 5 throws out 50-67% of
   content, often including the answering line.

2. Cosine-centroid centrality picks the most AVERAGE line, not the
   topic-defining line. For a doc that starts "There are several
   components:" followed by topic-specific paragraphs, the intro
   becomes maximally central and survives; the actual answers do not.

3. Stronger models get hurt MORE. potion-code-16M and potion-base-32M
   were already capturing the full-context signal well; condensation
   discards the context they were using. The weaker 4M/8M variants are
   the only ones that benefit, and only on code, and only marginally.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
DECISION: skip oo4q.3 (production wire-up).
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Closing oo4q.3 with reason "bench data did not justify production
wiring". The bench infrastructure (this beads .2) stays in tree as
ground truth for any future re-evaluation. Parent oo4q is also closed
since extractive (Tier 1) was the user-chosen scope and Tiers 2/3 are
documented as separate future inquiries within oo4q itself.

Possible future inquiries (NOT scoped to oo4q, would be new beads):
- k sweep (3, 7, 10) - unlikely to flip the verdict but cheap
- sentence-level splitting for prose (vs line-level)
- TextRank instead of cosine-centroid for centrality
- per-doc gating: only condense docs whose raw embedding is "diffuse"
- summary-augmented retrieval: a separate path where summaries are
  shown to users but not used as the embedding input (oo4q Tier 2/3
  remain open as separate inquiries if a user-facing summary need
  emerges).
close_reason: Implementation landed (condensation axis in embed-models bench, lift columns in published table). Full sweep run. Per the predetermined >5% threshold, lift is NOT achieved: across 24 model x corpus cells, 15 regress, 5 improve. Strongest models (potion-code-16M, potion-base-32M) regress under condensation. See notes for full per-model breakdown + hypothesis.

title: oo4q.1 Condense() standalone helper + unit tests (Tier 1, extractive)
description:
Implement extractive condensation as a self-contained function before wiring it anywhere. The function:

Condense(pieces []string, embedder Embedder, k int) []string

Given N pieces (sentences for prose, lines for code), embed each with the supplied model2vec Embedder, score each by centrality, return the k most-central pieces in original order.

Centrality scoring: start with raw cosine-centroid (per-piece avg similarity to all others) since it's simpler than TextRank and the small piece counts (~5-100 per node) make full TextRank overkill. Document the choice in a comment so a future implementer can A/B against TextRank if quality plateaus.

Placement: internal/application/embedder/condense/condense.go (new subpackage; pure-Go; depends only on the Embedder interface from ports). Test against curated fixtures — a known-noisy function body where the top-K should pick docstring + signature + topic line, and a known-prose paragraph where the top-K should pick the topic sentence.
acceptance_criteria: Condense() exists with godoc + unit tests covering: (a) noisy code body → top-K hits docstring/signature; (b) prose paragraph → top-K hits topic sentence; (c) k > N is no-op; (d) k <= 0 returns empty. No production wiring yet — self-contained.
close_reason: Condense() helper + 8 unit tests landed; pure-Go, no production wiring; covers acceptance criteria (code-body top-K, prose majority, k>N no-op, k<=0 nil, single-piece, order-preserved, error propagation). Next: oo4q.2 wires this into the embed-models bench to measure the lift.

title: Summary-augmented embeddings: condense node content before embedding (extractive → small LLM → full LLM)
description:
EXPLORATORY — investigate whether embedding an LLM-generated summary of a node (instead of, or alongside, the raw source) lifts retrieval recall. Standard technique in code-search literature: dense semantic signal per vector + less boilerplate noise. Trade-off is non-trivial added cost: every embed becomes embed+summary, which means an LLM call (or batched call) per new/changed node.\n\nDesign sketch (not committed to yet):\n- New pipeline component called at promotion time per new/changed declaration: LLM(node.raw_content, prompt='one-sentence summary') -> summary text. Use the existing optional LLM review provider so wiring stays single-stack.\n- Schema: add nodes.summary TEXT column (or sit on a sibling node_summaries table keyed by content_hash so summaries are content-addressed like embeddings already are — avoids re-summarising unchanged content). Content-addressed is the better shape; same dedup property as node_embeddings.\n- Embedder reads node.summary when present, falls back to raw_content. Or always embeds summary+raw_content concatenated (more signal, slightly larger vectors).\n- Measurement: extend the embed-models bench (solov2-0k5h) to add a 'summary-mode' axis — re-run all (model x corpus) combinations with summarised inputs and compare recall against the raw-content baseline. If the lift on the headline set is < ~5%, not worth the cost.\n\nCost notes:\n- Per-node LLM call at ~100ms+ each. A 10k-symbol repo = 1000s. Async at promotion is fine; sync at first-promote is painful.\n- Caching by content_hash + skipping unchanged content keeps the ongoing cost proportional to churn, not corpus size.\n- Opt-in via config (e.g. VESKA_SUMMARY_EMBEDS=on) so users without an LLM provider get the current behaviour.\n\nOpen questions before implementation:\n- Which LLM? Existing review pipeline uses Ollama generative models; reuse that, or allow a different provider? Sensible default is whatever the user already has running.\n- Always-summary, or only for nodes whose raw_content exceeds a length threshold (short symbols don't need summarising)?\n- Should chunk nodes (KindChunk) also get summaries? Probably yes — chunks are the noisiest content category and most likely to benefit.


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
THREE TIERS OF "SUMMARISATION" — pick by cost/quality budget
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

The "summary" pre-processing step doesn't have to be a full LLM call.
Three tiers exist; explore in order:

TIER 1 — Extractive condensation via model2vec itself (pure-Go, in-process)
  Mechanism: split node.raw_content into sentences/lines; embed each
  with model2vec; pick the K most central (TextRank over the similarity
  graph, or top-K closest to the document centroid); embed only that
  selection as the final node vector.
  Cost: ~same as current embed path (a few extra mean-pool calls per
  node — sub-millisecond).
  Quality ceiling: bounded by extraction — can't paraphrase, only
  select. But code + docstring content often has a signal-rich subset
  already (signature line + first sentence of doc comment + a few
  identifier-heavy lines).
  Best for: cheaply suppressing whitespace + boilerplate noise in
  embed inputs without a new dependency.

TIER 2 — Small distilled generative model (transformer; local-CPU-OK)
  Candidates: Phi-3-mini, TinyLlama, qwen2.5-coder:0.5b, smollm-135m.
  Mechanism: prompt an actually-generative small model to produce a
  one-sentence summary. Runs locally without GPU (CPU-only inference
  at ~100-500ms per call for the smallest variants).
  Cost: 100-1000× model2vec per node, but 10-100× cheaper than a
  full llama3.
  Quality ceiling: real abstractive paraphrase — can describe code
  better than its identifiers do.
  Best for: a quality lift where TIER 1 plateaus and TIER 3 is too
  costly.

TIER 3 — Full LLM (original sketch above)
  Mechanism: existing Ollama review provider + a summary prompt.
  Cost: high (seconds per node, GPU-friendly).
  Quality ceiling: best.
  Best for: small repos where the wall-time per-promotion is dominated
  by other work anyway.

Bench ordering (smallest cost first): run TIER 1 against the existing
embed-models bench (solov2-0k5h) with summary-mode on; record recall
lift vs raw-content baseline. If lift is meaningful (>5% on headline),
TIER 1 is the answer — no LLM dependency added. Only escalate to
TIER 2 if TIER 1 plateaus.


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
TERMINOLOGY: "SUMMARY" IS LOADED — DISAMBIGUATE BEFORE COMMITTING
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
"Summarise" can mean three different things, with very different
costs + capabilities. A Tier-1 (model2vec) approach satisfies the
first two; only the third needs generation.

(a) Pre-condense input for embedding
    Goal: a better vector (denser semantic signal, less boilerplate
    noise). Output is a vector, not text. The "summary" is internal
    plumbing — never seen by a user. TIER 1 (model2vec extractive
    selection) handles this fully. No language model needed.

(b) Extractive text summary (for users)
    Goal: a 1-2 sentence display blurb (e.g. in MCP tool output, a
    wiki page, an editor hover). Output is verbatim text PULLED from
    the source. Reads natural on prose (doc comments → key sentence);
    less natural on raw code without comments. TIER 1 can do this for
    prose-heavy nodes.

(c) Abstractive text summary (for users)
    Goal: a paraphrased "this function does X" blurb that uses words
    NOT present in the source. Requires a decoder + autoregressive
    generation — architecturally impossible for model2vec. Needs
    TIER 2 or TIER 3.

MOST retrieval-quality lift papers describe case (a) (denser vectors)
as "summarisation." The lift comes from suppressing noise, not from
generating language. Default to TIER 1 for the bench unless we
specifically want user-facing case (c) text.


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SCOPE DECISION (recorded after the bench data landed)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Of the three tiers above, this bead is now scoped to TIER 1 (extractive
condensation via model2vec) ONLY. The user explicitly chose the
"A + C" deployment rung — stay on the existing fat-single binary
(potion-code-16M), add the orthogonal extractive pre-condensation
improvement.

The other paths in this bead's notes remain documented as future options
but are NOT in scope for the planned sub-tasks:

- TIER 2 (small distilled LLM) — adds a generative dependency; deferred
  until extractive plateaus AND there's a concrete user need for
  abstractive summaries (e.g. user-facing display blurbs in MCP output).
- TIER 3 (full LLM) — only justified if Tier 2 isn't enough; very
  unlikely to land without a new product driver.

Multi-bucket model2vec (hi5) is also deliberately NOT planned alongside
oo4q. The bench showed the multi-bucket lift is concentrated in dense-
technical docs (the veska-docs corpus, +0.20 R@10) but vanishes on API
reference and genuine prose. Tier 1 condensation may erase part of that
gap by suppressing boilerplate, which is exactly the case the code-tuned
model handles worst. So: ship Tier 1 first, RE-MEASURE on the bench
including the veska-docs case, and only revisit hi5 if the gap persists.
acceptance_criteria: 1) Decision recorded — either go/no-go with bench data backing it, OR a clear design proposal awaiting prioritisation. 2) If go: pipeline shipped behind an opt-in flag; bench (solov2-0k5h) re-run with summary mode on and the recall lift recorded in docs/operations/embedder-benchmarks.md. 3) If no-go: bench data showing the lift was too small to justify cost; bead closed with the data linked.
close_reason: Tier 1 (extractive condensation) data-backed-skipped per oo4q.2 results — net regression vs raw. Tier 2 (small distilled LLM) + Tier 3 (full LLM) remain documented as future options in this bead but are NOT planned: justified only if a user-facing summary need emerges, at which point a new bead should be filed with fresh design + decision criteria.

title: 0k5h.9 Run -full with Ollama prose specialists; publish updated table
description: After 0k5h.7 and 0k5h.8 land, ollama pull the 4 Ollama embedders (nomic-embed-text, bge-m3, snowflake-arctic-embed, mxbai-embed-large), run 'make eval-embed-models-full' against the strengthened corpora, and commit the regenerated docs/operations/embedder-benchmarks.md. Provides the quality-ceiling reference and a meaningful in-process-vs-network comparison.
acceptance_criteria: ollama pull steps documented in the harness README; -full target completes against all 4 Ollama models; published table shows model2vec rows + Ollama rows with the latency-axis warning; hi5's OPEN QUESTION #1 (multi-bucket worth it?) now answerable with strengthened data.

title: 0k5h.8 Rewrite veska/cobra prose headlines to remove identifier leakage
description: The existing prose.jsonl headline queries leak library-specific identifiers ('promotion pipeline that handles staged nodes' contains 'promotion' + 'staged'). That gives code-tuned models an unfair advantage on what's supposed to be a prose recall test. Rewrite queries to be the way a non-Go-aware user would phrase them ('how does the system save changes permanently' instead). Keep expected slugs unchanged; only the query text changes.
acceptance_criteria: fixtures/prose.jsonl queries for veska-docs and cobra-docs corpora rewritten to remove library-identifier leakage; not_in_corpus stays 0; recall numbers shift (hopefully showing prose-tuned models pulling ahead); change is fixture-only — no code edits.
close_reason: Rewrote all 25 prose headline queries (15 veska-docs + 10 cobra-docs) to be the way a non-Go-aware user would naturally phrase them: no 'promotion', 'staged', 'mcp', 'auto-link', 'cobra-cli' etc. in the queries; expected slugs unchanged. Re-running the bench shows the expected shift: potion-code-16M's prose R@10 dropped from 0.633 → 0.483 (15 points, confirming the previous score was inflated by identifier leakage). potion-base-32M now stands alone as the prose winner at 0.583 (clean +0.10 gap vs code-16M). potion-retrieval-32M underperformed base-32M (0.417 vs 0.583) — the 'retrieval-tuned' label doesn't translate to better doc-section retrieval in our test. Fixture-only change; no code edits.

title: 0k5h.7 Wikipedia-tech corpus for genuine natural-prose evaluation
description:
Add a 'wikipedia-tech' prose corpus to the bench. Current prose corpora (veska-docs, cobra-docs) are really dense technical documentation full of library identifiers — favors code-tuned models unfairly. Wikipedia articles on software topics give genuine natural-prose with low identifier density.

Implementation:
- fixtures/wikipedia_titles.txt: sha-pinned list of ~50-100 article titles (software/compilers/distributed-systems/programming-languages/security/ML).
- scripts/fetch-wikipedia-corpus.sh: uses en.wikipedia.org/api/rest_v1/page/html/<title> to fetch each title, convert to markdown, write to out/wikipedia-tech/<title>.md.
- BenchCorpora gains 'wikipedia-tech' (prose kind, root out/wikipedia-tech).
- fixtures/prose.jsonl gains ~20 hand-curated query→section pairs for the new corpus, with queries deliberately written without code identifiers ('how does virtual memory work', 'principles of CRDT design').
- CC-BY-SA license noted in script + corpus README.
acceptance_criteria: make eval-embed-models walks the wikipedia-tech corpus; fetch script idempotent; ~20 headline pairs verified to map to real article sections (not_in_corpus=0); embedder-benchmarks.md gains a third prose recall column or footnote.
close_reason: Wikipedia-tech corpus shipped: scripts/fetch-wikipedia-corpus.sh pulls 28 software-topic articles via Wikipedia's MediaWiki API (explaintext + section markers preserved → markdown via simple awk-equivalent in python). Polite User-Agent + 0.5s sleep stays under courtesy rate limits. Articles span fundamentals (Compiler, Algorithm, Hash table), distributed (Paxos, CAP, Eventual consistency), concurrency (Deadlock, Race condition, Mutex), web (HTTP, TCP, DNS), and language design (AST, Lexical analysis). 20 hand-curated headline pairs in prose.jsonl, all verified against the empty-body-skip behaviour (caught 'mutex.md#mutexes-vs-semaphores' which had no body content; swapped for #disadvantages). out/ remains gitignored — CC-BY-SA content stays out of the repo. New picture on the strengthened bench: code default still potion-code-16M (R@10 0.60 by ~0.25); prose default potion-base-32M (R@10 0.67, +0.066 above code-16M); on genuine Wikipedia prose all 8M+ models tie at R@10 ~0.85, with potion-code-16M winning MRR (0.556). Differentiation between bucket models lives in DENSE-TECHNICAL docs (veska-docs style), not natural prose.

title: 0k5h.6 Markdown table generator + initial publish
description: Auto-generate docs/operations/embedder-benchmarks.md from out/results.json. Columns: Model | Type (in-process/Ollama) | Size on disk | RSS | Recall@10 code (headline) | Recall@10 prose | p95 latency | Recommended for. Run the full bench, commit results, answer hi5's open question #1.
acceptance_criteria: docs/operations/embedder-benchmarks.md auto-generated and committed with results from the full bench; hi5's OPEN QUESTION #1 closed with a data-backed recommendation (or hi5 closed entirely if data shows multi-bucket isn't worth complexity).
close_reason: Auto-generated docs/operations/embedder-benchmarks.md from the in-memory runResults at end-of-bench. Aggregates per model: avg recall@10 on headline GT across code corpora; same across prose corpora; avg per-query embed latency (with a header warning that model2vec/ollama latencies aren't directly comparable); on-disk footprint for model2vec models computed via WalkDir on the install path. 'Recommended for' column applies four badges — code / prose / smallest footprint / fastest query — with a 0.02 tie-tolerance on recall and a 10% tolerance on latency so the recommendation isn't dominated by trivial differences. Verified live: 1 model installed → 1 row → all 4 badges (correct since it's the sole contender); when more models install the badge attribution distributes. INFRASTRUCTURE COMPLETE — to publish a full comparison table, run scripts/install-bench-models.sh then make eval-embed-models and commit the regenerated docs/operations/embedder-benchmarks.md. That's an op step, not a code step.

title: 0k5h.5 Ollama support + skip-detect + -full target
description: Add the 4 Ollama models (nomic-embed-text, bge-m3, snowflake-arctic-embed, mxbai-embed-large). 'make eval-embed-models-full' invokes the harness with the Ollama set. At runtime, ping Ollama once; if unreachable, log a warning and skip the Ollama subset rather than failing the whole bench. Latency reported on a separate axis (network round-trip) from model2vec's in-process compute.
acceptance_criteria: make eval-embed-models-full runs against Ollama when reachable; skip-detect path tested; latency axes reported separately in JSON results.
close_reason: Ollama subset added behind 'make eval-embed-models-full' (env: EMBED_BENCH_INCLUDE_OLLAMA=1). Embedder interface generalises the bench's corpus walkers and recall metric so model2vec.Provider and ollama.Provider are interchangeable. ollamaReachable() probes /api/tags with a 2s timeout once at start; unreachable → log a warning and continue with model2vec-only. ollamaURL() respects VESKA_OLLAMA_URL. Latency for ollama runs is on a separate axis from in-process latency (network round-trip vs CPU compute) — runResult.ModelType tags the row 'model2vec' or 'ollama' so the 0k5h.6 table generator can split them. Ollama runs use a separate EMBED_BENCH_OLLAMA_MAX_DOCS cap (default 500) since per-embed network cost is orders of magnitude higher than model2vec's in-process path. Verified live: reachable-and-included path detects Ollama at localhost:11434 and starts running; unreachable-but-included path logs the WARN + completes model2vec runs only ('7 run rows, PASS'). lint clean.

title: 0k5h.4 Prose corpus
description: Pull (query, expected passage) pairs from veska docs/ and cobra docs. Section title used as query, the section body as the expected passage. Same recall@k metric as code corpora but over markdown content rather than parsed symbols.
acceptance_criteria: tools/loadtest/embed_models/fixtures/prose.jsonl committed; harness runs prose corpus through every model; results recorded alongside code corpora.
close_reason: Prose corpora wired end-to-end. New prose.go walks .md files under a 'prose' corpus, splits each on H1/H2 ATX headings, and emits one doc per section keyed as '<relative-path>#<heading-slug>'. corpusEntry gained a kind field; discovery routes prose corpora to embedProseCorpus and code corpora to the existing tree-sitter walker. fixtures/prose.jsonl committed with 15 veska-docs + 10 cobra-docs pairs targeting real H2 sections (slugs verified by grep). Ground-truth loader now scans all *.jsonl in fixtures/ and filters by corpus name, so future fixture files just drop in. Added a not_in_corpus signal to RecallScores to distinguish fixture-typos from real model-recall misses — flushed out 9-of-10 bad slugs in the first prose draft; the rewrite hits 0 fixture errors. Verified live: potion-code-16M on veska-docs headline R@10=0.47/MRR=0.27; on cobra-docs R@10=0.80/MRR=0.64 (cobra's short distinctive headings match natural queries much better than veska's numbered prefixes — a data point the bench was meant to surface).

title: 0k5h.3 Ground-truth tooling + recall@k metric
description: Three blended sources per code corpus: (1) doc-derived auto-extractor — for each exported function/method with a non-empty doc comment, emit (paraphrase, symbol) pair; (2) hand-curated headline.jsonl — ~20 natural-language queries per repo, written manually; (3) test-name-derived — TestXxx_Yyy → tested symbol. Implement recall@1/@5/@10 + MRR computation against each set. Headline set is the published number.
acceptance_criteria: tools/loadtest/embed_models/fixtures/headline.jsonl committed with ~20 pairs for veska first (others can come later); doc-derived extractor produces ≥100 pairs per repo; recall@k + MRR computed and reported per (model, corpus, gt-source).
close_reason: Three GT sources implemented: (1) doc-derived auto-extractor uses go/parser to grab first-sentence-minus-prefix from each exported FuncDecl/TypeSpec doc comment (~178-831 pairs per repo); (2) hand-curated fixtures/headline.jsonl with 20 veska pairs — the published headline metric; (3) test-name-derived from TestXxx_Yyy → (humanized 'Yyy', 'Xxx'). Recall@1/@5/@10 + MRR computed per (model × corpus × gt-source), captured in results.json under a Recall map. Verified live: potion-code-16M on veska headline = R@10 0.60 / MRR 0.19; doc-derived works well on docstring-rich repos (cobra 0.62, gin 0.63) and confirms pflag/testify have sparser docs (~0.15). Test-name source returns weak signal (R@10 0.06) as expected — auxiliary not headline.

title: 0k5h.2 Multi-model + multi-corpus + fetch script
description: Add: (a) loop over the 6 model2vec variants from one harness run; (b) repos.manifest sha-pinned + fetch.sh that pulls cobra/pflag/testify/gin into out/repos/; (c) corpus loader handles each repo's .go files via the existing treesitter parser. Still no ground truth — just measure that all (model × corpus) combinations embed without error and record their latency + footprint.
acceptance_criteria: make eval-embed-models walks all 6 models × 5 corpora; fetch.sh idempotent and sha-verified; JSON results capture embed-throughput and disk footprint per model.
close_reason: Harness iterates over BenchModels × BenchCorpora with graceful skip-on-missing: 5 of 6 models and 4 of 5 corpora can be absent and the bench still runs against whatever's installed (logs warnings + a 'run scripts/<x>.sh' hint). Two helper scripts shipped: scripts/fetch-corpora.sh (shallow git-clone the 4 external Go repos sha-pinned via fixtures/repos.manifest into out/repos/) and scripts/install-bench-models.sh (curl the 6 model2vec variants from Hugging Face into VESKA_HOME/static-model/). Each (model × corpus) run captures doc_count, embed_total_ms, embed_avg_ms, query_ms, and a top-K sanity check; structured results written to out/results.json (EMBED_BENCH_OUT overridable). Verified live: 5 corpora × 1 installed model = 5 runs, ~6500 docs embedded total, JSON shape matches the 0k5h.6 table-generator's expected schema. out/ gitignored.

title: 0k5h.1 Harness skeleton: model2vec load + in-memory k-NN + one corpus
description: Vertical slice end-to-end. Package tools/loadtest/embed_models/, eval build tag, make eval-embed-models target. Loads ONE model2vec model dir, embeds the local veska repo as the corpus, runs a hard-coded query, prints top-K. No multi-model, no fetch, no Ollama, no output formatting. Goal: prove the loop.
acceptance_criteria: make eval-embed-models runs; loads model2vec/potion-code-16M; embeds nodes from internal/; runs a sample query; prints ranked results. eval build tag keeps go test ./... fast.
close_reason: Vertical slice landed: tools/loadtest/embed_models/ behind the eval build tag, 'make eval-embed-models' runs end-to-end. Loads model2vec/potion-code-16M, walks the corpus root (internal/core/domain by default — overridable via EMBED_BENCH_CORPUS), embeds each parsed declaration's name+raw_content, embeds an env-overridable query, brute-force dot-product k-NN, prints top-K. Verified: 155 docs embedded in 20ms; query 'parse failure with line number' returns ParseFailure as the #1 hit at score 0.72. Env knobs: EMBED_BENCH_MODEL_DIR, EMBED_BENCH_CORPUS, EMBED_BENCH_QUERY, EMBED_BENCH_TOPK.

title: Benchmark embedding models (model2vec + Ollama) on real codebases + publish comparison table
description:
Compare embedding model options users actually have, so hi5's defaults are data-backed AND end users have a published comparison table to pick a model matching their footprint / quality / infrastructure preference. Prerequisite to hi5 — could short-circuit hi5 entirely if one model adequately serves both code and prose buckets.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
MODELS IN THE BENCH (10 total)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
model2vec family (6, pure-Go, in-process):
  - potion-base-2M     (~8MB,  smallest)
  - potion-base-8M     (~32MB, mid-tier general)
  - potion-code-16M    (~64MB, today's code baseline — reference)
  - potion-retrieval-32M (~64MB, retrieval-tuned)
  - potion-base-32M    (~64MB, general at code-equivalent footprint — A/B vs retrieval-32M)
  - potion-base-128M   (~256MB, best quality)

Ollama family (4, network / service):
  - nomic-embed-text          (Ollama-recommended general default)
  - bge-m3                    (high-quality, multilingual)
  - snowflake-arctic-embed    (newer, retrieval-tuned)
